TL;DR: The paper investigates steering vectors in controlled text generation, analyzing their generality and limitations across expressibility, task transfer, and multi-trait composition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the practical generality and limitations of steering vectors as interpretable, training-free tools for preference-driven text generation.

Method: Evaluates steering vectors across traits, tasks, and models using the PLUME benchmark; explores multi-trait composition and transferability.

Result: Finds steering effectiveness varies by trait, degrades with transfer to downstream tasks, and suffers in multi-trait composition due to coherence-expression tradeoffs.

Conclusion: Steering vectors show meaningful limitations for general-purpose preference alignment, requiring task-specific tuning.

Abstract: Steering vectors have emerged as a promising approach to controlled text generation, offering interpretable, training-free mechanisms for shaping model outputs. However, their practical generality remains poorly understood. We study the limits of steering vector generalization along three dimensions: trait expressibility, task transfer, and multi-trait composition. Using the PLUME writing personalization benchmark, we extract steering vectors for a range of preferences and evaluate them on summarization and email-writing tasks across two open-source models (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama3.1-8B-Instruct). We find that steering effectiveness varies substantially across traits. We further show that steering effectiveness can degrade when vectors extracted from positive and negative style examples are transferred to downstream writing personalization tasks. Finally, we compare common methods for composing multiple steering vectors and find that all methods suffer significant drops in trait expression as more vectors are added, with a tradeoff between coherence and expressibility that requires per-setting hyperparameter tuning. Taken together, our results suggest that steering vectors face meaningful limits as a general-purpose tool for preference alignment.

</details>


### [120] [PairCoder++: Pair Programming as a Universal Paradigm for Verified Code-Driven Multimodal and Structured-Artifact Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01883)
*Junhao Chen,Xiang Li,Mingjin Chen,Boran Zhang,Henghaofan Zhang,Yibin Xu,Yuehan Cui,Fangsheng Weng,Fei Ma,Qi Tian,Ruqi Huang,Hao Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces PairCoder, where two agents collaborate in a pair-programming paradigm to generate and verify code-driven artifacts, significantly improving performance on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the brittleness of single-pass inference in code-driven artifact generation, where downstream tools like compilers or renderers are invisible to language models and introduce errors.

Method: The PairCoder system incorporates two roles: a Driver agent, responsible for generating programs, and a Navigator agent, which reviews and verifies the generation using diagnostics and the artifact's outputs, alternating roles in case of persistent errors.

Result: PairCoder shows consistent improvement across 17 public benchmarks involving various artifacts, with significant advancements in metrics such as Blender's scene executability (increased from 0.20 to 0.78) and TikZ's compile rate (improvements by 10-30 points), though at a computational cost 2.9 to 9.2 times higher.

Conclusion: PairCoder demonstrates that collaborative pair programming between two agents, grounded in the verification evidence provided by toolchains, is a robust approach for producing verifiable code-based artifacts.

Abstract: Code is the medium through which large language models generate structured artifacts: charts, scientific figures, vector graphics, CAD models, 3D scenes, and hardware designs are all produced by writing programs. In this regime single pass inference is brittle, because the compiler, renderer, or simulator that decides whether the artifact exists is invisible to the model. We present PairCoder, which grounds review in the toolchain and realizes it as two agent pair programming: a Driver agent writes the program, a Navigator agent reviews it against verification evidence (diagnostics, execution results, and renderings of the current artifact beside the target), and the two switch roles when errors persist. Across 17 public benchmarks and seven models from three vendors, PairCoder improves essentially every benchmark whose artifact is verifiable, on full official metric suites rather than execution alone (for example, Blender scene executability 0.20 to 0.78; TikZ compile rate up 10 to 30 points on every model), at 2.9 to 9.2 times single model cost (about 7 times overall). The improvements concentrate where the toolchain provides an informative oracle and the baseline leaves headroom, and the method ties or mildly regresses where the oracle is weak; we frame pair programming as a reliable recipe for verified code driven generation.

</details>


### [121] [The Grammar Does the Work: Functional vs. Lexical Dependency Length Minimization Across Universal Dependencies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01899)
*Kim Gerdes*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates dependency length minimization (DLM) and finds that grammar targets functional dependencies while processing optimizes the ordering of lexical dependencies, with an asymmetry present across languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether dependency length minimization operates uniformly across all syntactic relation types or varies depending on structural features in language.

Method: The study analyzes dependency structures of 122 languages using datasets UD and SUD (version 2.17), focusing on differences in dependency lengths between functional and lexical dependencies.

Result: Functional dependencies are consistently short (mean = 1.71), invariant across languages, while lexical dependencies are longer (mean = 2.87) and more variable, influenced by word-order typology.

Conclusion: Grammar inherently minimizes functional dependency lengths universally, while processing pressures govern the variability of lexical dependencies across different languages.

Abstract: Dependency length minimization (DLM) is a well-documented processing universal, but previous studies report a single mean dependency distance (MDD) per language, obscuring variation across syntactic relation types. We analyze 122 languages in UD and SUD (version 2.17), showing that DLM operates on two distinct levels. Grammar-driven optimization targets functional dependencies (det, case, aux), which are universally short (mean 1.71, $σ$ = 0.33) and invariant across typologically diverse languages. Processing-driven optimization operates on lexical dependencies (nsubj, obj, obl), which are longer (mean 2.87), highly variable ($σ$ = 0.63), and constrained by word-order typology. This asymmetry holds in SUD despite reversed head direction (r = 0.92). We conclude that ''the grammar does the work'' of minimization by scaffolding sentences with local functional attachments, leaving processing pressures to determine the ordering of lexical heads.

</details>


### [122] [TUDUM: A Turkish-Thinking Reasoning Pipeline for Qwen3.5-27B](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01927)
*Baran Bingol,Bahaeddin Turkoglu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces TUDUM, a pipeline to adapt a large Qwen-family thinking model for explicit Turkish reasoning but shows mixed results in achieving its goals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of adapting an English-dominant large language model to produce explicit reasoning traces and answers in the Turkish language.

Method: The paper uses supervised fine-tuning with LoRA adapters and reinforcement learning (GRPO-family) on a filtered Turkish mathematics dataset.

Result: Mixed outcomes: fine-tuning reduced response length and ensured Turkish reasoning traces but caused accuracy loss. RL partially improved mathematical performance without achieving model superiority in benchmarks.

Conclusion: TUDUM's pipeline offers a technically honest adaptation method for Turkish reasoning, yet it doesn't claim state-of-the-art performance. The model is publicly available for further development.

Abstract: This paper presents TUDUM (Türkçe Düşünen Üretken Model), a project pipeline for adapting a Qwen-family 27B thinking model toward Turkish reasoning. The central problem is not only to answer Turkish prompts in Turkish, but to make the explicit reasoning trace itself Turkish. A thinking model may translate a Turkish prompt into an English-centered internal or visible scratchpad, solve the problem mostly in English, and only localize the final answer. TUDUM instead treats the generated <think>...</think> block as a trainable behavior. The pipeline starts from the project base checkpoint unsloth/Qwen3.5-27B, applies supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on 15,991 Turkish reasoning examples using LoRA adapters, and then applies GRPO-family reinforcement learning on a proxy-filtered Turkish mathematics environment. The results are mixed. SFT made the model shorter and more consistently Turkish in its reasoning behavior, with large reductions in average response length and thinking exhaustion, but reduced benchmark accuracy. RL recovered some mathematical performance, especially AIME24 at the best early checkpoint, yet did not uniformly improve all benchmarks and did not exceed the base model on the reported Macro-6 average. The contribution is therefore best framed as a technically honest Turkish-thinking reasoning pipeline and evaluation, not as a claim of state-of-the-art Turkish reasoning. The released step-50 model is publicly available.

</details>


### [123] [AIriskEval-edu: New Dataset for Risk Assessment in AI-mediated K-12 Educational Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01934)
*Javier Irigoyen,Roberto Daza,Francisco Jurado,Julian Fierrez,Ruben Tolosana,Alvaro Ortigosa,Enrique Blas,Aythami Morales*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AIriskEval-edu-db2 introduces a dataset of 1,639 explanations designed to train LLM-based auditors for risk assessment in K-12 instructional content. It includes human and simulated explanations, annotated for risk localization and description.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address pedagogical risks in instructional content for grades K-12 by leveraging LLMs to ensure educational standards and quality.

Method: A dataset of 1,639 explanations was created. Explanations were annotated for risk using a semi-automatic process validated by expert teachers, and evaluation experiments were conducted with proprietary and local models.

Result: The dataset enables training and evaluation of locally deployable models that perform comparably to proprietary frontier models in risk detection and explainability assessment.

Conclusion: AIriskEval-edu-db2 facilitates privacy-preserving risk auditing in educational settings, addressing pedagogical quality concerns and validating effectiveness of locally fine-tuned LLMs.

Abstract: This work introduces AIriskEval-edu-db2, a new dataset designed to train and evaluate auditors based on LLMs for an explainable pedagogical risk assessment in instructional content for grades K-12. The dataset comprises 1,639 explanations from 170 curated ScienceQA questions, covering science, language arts, and social sciences. For each question, the dataset includes an explanation written by a human teacher alongside 11 explanations generated by LLM-simulated teacher profiles associated with distinct pedagogical risks. We propose a comprehensive risk rubric aligned with established educational standards that covers five complementary dimensions: factual precision, depth and completeness, focus and relevance, student-level appropriateness, and ideological bias. A key contribution is the addition of 785 explanations with structured explainability annotations, including risk localization and risk description. The annotations are produced through a semi-automatic process with expert teacher validation. Finally, we present validation experiments comparing state-of-the-art proprietary models with a lightweight local Llama 3.1 8B model in both the pedagogical risk detection and the explainability assessment. These experiments evaluate whether supervised fine-tuning on AIriskEval-edu-db2 enables a locally deployable model to approach or outperform stronger frontier models while preserving privacy in educational auditing and assessment tasks.

</details>


### [124] [NAVER LABS Europe Submission to the Instruction-following 2026 Short Track](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01960)
*Marcely Zanon Boito,Hemant Yadav,Jean-Luc Meunier,Ioan Calapodescu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper describes improvements to NAVER LABS Europe's system for instruction-following speech processing, combining ASR, ST, and SQA tasks in three languages. It introduces SpeechMapper and synthetic data, leading to better performance in the IWSLT 2026 short track.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to advance instruction-following speech processing systems to perform tasks like ASR, ST, and SQA efficiently while targeting improved compactness and accuracy.

Method: The paper uses a multi-stage training pipeline updated with SpeechMapper and utilizes a synthetic dataset, fakACL, generated through LLM prompting, segmentation, and speech synthesis.

Result: The improved system outperformed last year's best short-track system and tied for first place in IWSLT 2026 rankings.

Conclusion: The upgrades in speech embedding projection and use of synthetic data delivered enhanced performance, demonstrating compactness and effectiveness with a weaker LLM backbone.

Abstract: In this paper, we describe NAVER LABS Europe's submission to the instruction-following speech processing short track at IWSLT 2026. We participate again in the constrained setting, developing systems capable of jointly performing ASR, ST, and SQA from English speech into Chinese, Italian, and German. Building on our previous submission, ranked first in last year's short track, we update our multi-stage training pipeline by replacing the speech projector with SpeechMapper, a method for learning a speech-to-LLM embedding projector using only ASR data. In addition, we introduce a synthetic SQA dataset, fakACL, composed of artificially generated scientific presentations. This dataset is built by prompting the LLM backbone, segmenting the generated talks, and synthesizing speech with SeamlessM4T-large-v2. The combination of an improved speech projection mechanism and domain-specific synthetic data allows our model to outperform last year's best short-track system, while being considerably more compact and relying on a weaker LLM backbone. This year's results place our system tied for first place in the overall short track ranking.

</details>


### [125] [Beyond Supervised Clarification: Input Rewriting with LLMs for Dialogue Discourse Parsing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01964)
*Yiming Liu,Ziyue Zhang,Zhichao Xu,Xin Yu,Yingheng Tang,Tianyu Jiang,Jie Cao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper assesses rewriting strategies to clarify inputs for discourse parsing models under practical scenarios where clarification supervision is unavailable. Results reveal that rewriting often disrupts more than it helps and advocate for selective intervention strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to evaluate how input rewriting can improve the accuracy of frozen incremental dialogue discourse parsers, especially in the absence of supervised clarification models.

Method: The authors evaluate parser performance across multiple datasets and scenarios, comparing zero-shot prompting, parser feedback, and a GRPO-trained clarifier for improving input rewriting strategies.

Result: Findings show that parser-agnostic rewritings often fail under real-world deployment conditions, causing more errors than fixes. A GRPO-trained clarifier mitigates regressions but does not ensure consistently effective clarifications.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that successful input rewriting models require the ability to predict whether an utterance is repairable before rewriting, highlighting rewritability prediction as a key research direction.

Abstract: Rewriting inputs to improve frozen downstream models has become a common strategy in modern NLP pipelines. Prior work on incremental dialogue discourse parsing (DDP) shows that supervised clarification models can rewrite fragmentary or underspecified utterances, such as resolving ellipsis or references, to improve parsing accuracy. In this work, we revisit this idea under realistic deployment conditions, where no clarification supervision is available and the clarifier must rely on zero-shot prompting or feedback from a frozen parser. Across three Segmented Discourse Representation Theory (SDRT) datasets and multiple parsers, we find that last-utterance clarification is far less reliable than suggested by supervised settings. Parser-agnostic rewriting often introduces more regressions than repairs, as edits that enable fixes also disrupt discourse cues relied upon by the parser. A best-of-8 rewriting analysis further reveals a practical ceiling: a large fraction of errors are not repairable through input rewriting alone. A parser-aware clarifier trained with GRPO reduces regressions by up to 37% by learning conservative abstention, yet still fails to produce selectivity-aware clarifications that consistently improve parsing. Together, these findings recast clarification as a selective intervention problem. We identify rewritability prediction, deciding whether an utterance is repairable before intervention, as the key missing capability for input-side optimization of frozen discourse parsers, and a critical direction for improving agentic pipelines more broadly.

</details>


### [126] [Towards a Phonology-Informed Evaluation of Multilingual TTS](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01965)
*Sneha Ray Barman,Neeraj Kumar Sharma,Shakuntala Mahanta*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper highlights the mismatch between natural-sounding neural TTS systems and their ability to preserve phonological contrasts, proposing a classifier-based framework for auditing faithfulness against human speech.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the problem of neural TTS systems failing to preserve language-specific phonological contrasts that are crucial for distinguishing words, which standard evaluation metrics like MOS do not cover.

Method: The authors developed a classifier-based auditing framework that compares synthesized speech outputs against language-specific phonological patterns derived from human speech benchmarks. They tested it using Assamese ATR vowel harmony data.

Result: The framework successfully transferred from human speech to synthesized speech with minimal accuracy loss. Audits revealed biases in the TTS system output not found in human speech, and classifier-based labels provided a more accurate measure of harmony than transcription labels.

Conclusion: The framework can diagnose task-specific discrepancies in synthesized speech and generalizes to other phonological contrasts with quantifiable acoustic cues, ensuring better alignment with intended phonological patterns.

Abstract: Neural TTS systems can sound natural across languages, but naturalness does not guarantee the preservation of sound contrasts that distinguish words from their grammatical forms. Standard metrics like MOS do not test for this. We propose a classifier-based framework that audits TTS output against language-specific phonological patterns using human speech as a benchmark. Testing Assamese advanced tongue root (ATR) vowel harmony with Meta's MMS TTS, we show that a classifier trained on human speech transfers to synthesized speech with minimal loss. The faithfulness audit reveals that [+ATR] mid vowels are realized as [-ATR] in 1/3 tokens despite an underlying [+ATR] specification, a bias absent in human speech. At the word level, predicted ATR labels classify harmony more accurately than transcription labels, indicating a gap between intended and produced phonology. The framework offers task-specific diagnostics and generalizes to other phonological contrasts with measurable acoustic cues.

</details>


### [127] [Object Aligner: A Configurable JSON Schema Similarity Score for Graphs, Applied to LLM Prompt Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01972)
*Jan Drchal*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Object Aligner, a Python library designed to deterministically score JSON objects by aligning their structure using advanced algorithms like the Hungarian algorithm and referential alignment for graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to create a reliable and adaptable method for comparing complex JSON outputs to gold references, as existing methods like exact match or LLM judges are either brittle, non-deterministic, or opaque.

Method: The Object Aligner employs tree and sequence alignment algorithms, augmented by a new concept called referential alignment, which uses Weisfeiler-Leman color refinement for approximating graph bijections. It is configured via JSON Schema extensions, requiring no additional coding for adaptation.

Result: The Object Aligner successfully provides deterministic and structured scoring while offering repair suggestions. It outperforms or matches existing metrics when integrated into systems like the GEPA prompt optimizer.

Conclusion: Object Aligner effectively addresses challenges in comparing structured JSON data, particularly in graph and hypergraph contexts, and provides a practical, extensible tool for this purpose.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are often asked to produce JSON conforming to a fixed schema, powering information extraction, tool calling, agentic planning, and knowledge-graph construction. Measuring how closely an output matches a gold reference is essential yet surprisingly hard: exact match is brittle, text similarity ignores structure, and an LLM judge is expensive, opaque, and non-deterministic. We address this with Object Aligner (OA), an open-source Python library that scores two JSON objects deterministically by recursively aligning their trees (the Hungarian algorithm for unordered collections, sequence alignment for ordered ones) and awarding partial credit at the granularity the schema declares. The Object Aligner is configured entirely through a set of JSON Schema extensions, so adapting it to a new task involves annotating a schema rather than writing code. Complex structured data, however, are rarely flat trees: records may form graphs or hypergraphs keyed by arbitrary identifiers, breaking the assumptions of prior similarity metrics. Our central contribution, referential alignment, closes this gap by inferring a bijection between gold and candidate identifiers and scoring every reference through it, so the score is invariant to relabeling. Since recovering this bijection exactly is graph isomorphism, the Object Aligner approximates it with Weisfeiler-Leman color refinement. An order-sensitive sequence regime targets ranking and planning. Since the same alignment localizes every mismatch, the Object Aligner emits ranked repair suggestions at no extra cost. Used as a reward inside the GEPA prompt optimizer, Object Aligner helps or stays neutral across all datasets.

</details>


### [128] [Using embeddings to predict spoken word duration and pitch in Mandarin monosyllabic words](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02002)
*Xiaoyun Jin,Mirjam Ernestus,R. Harald Baayen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper evaluates how contextualized embeddings (CEs) predict word duration in Mandarin spontaneous speech, showing their efficacy for accurate spoken word duration prediction and back-transformation to f0 contours.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to understand if contextualized embeddings (CEs) can predict spoken word duration for Mandarin words in spontaneous speech, building on previous findings about f0 contour prediction.

Method: Analyses were performed on 7470 Mandarin monosyllabic CV tokens, employing type-wise and token-wise permutation baselines to evaluate the predictive performance of CEs for word duration.

Result: The results demonstrate that CEs can predict spoken word duration above chance levels at both type and token levels, and predicted durations effectively back-transform normalized f0 contours to match empirical contours.

Conclusion: CEs are a reliable predictor of spoken word duration in Mandarin, and their predictions can be used to reconstruct accurate time-scale f0 contours in spontaneous speech.

Abstract: Time-normalized f0 contours of Mandarin words in conversational speech have been shown to be predictable in part from their contextualized embeddings (CEs). The present study investigates whether CEs also predict spoken word duration for 7470 tokens of Mandarin monosyllabic CV words extracted from a Mandarin corpus of spontaneous speech. We show that CEs indeed are predictive for duration, above chance level, not only at the type level, but also at the level of individual tokens, as indicated by the results obtained with the type-wise and token-wise permutation baselines. We also show that the predicted durations are sufficiently precise to back-transform predicted f0 contours in [0,1] normalized time to contours on the ms time scale. The resulting predicted contours approximate empirical contours and also outperform a permutation baseline.

</details>


### [129] [EduArt: An educational-level benchmark for evaluating art history knowledge in large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02007)
*Gianmarco Spinaci,Lukas Klic,Giovanni Colavizza*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EduArt is a benchmark for assessing art-historical knowledge and multimodal reasoning in language models, with robustness across formats and languages. Results highlight significant weaknesses in open completion tasks compared to multiple-choice settings and issues in deploying knowledge responsibly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate large language models on art-historical knowledge and visual reasoning, moving beyond general benchmarks and revealing discipline-specific behaviors.

Method: EduArt benchmark was developed using 871 human-authored art-historical questions across languages and formats, analyzed using Classical Test Theory and logistic regression to isolate effects.

Result: Models showed high accuracy in recognition (multiple choice) but struggled with tasks requiring generation or identification, indicating dissociation between knowledge and its deployment.

Conclusion: Single-format benchmarks can overestimate LLM performance, and task diversity in benchmarks is essential for responsibly leveraging models in disciplines like art history.

Abstract: Large language models now score near ceiling on general benchmarks, but these aggregate measures reveal little about how models behave within single disciplines. Existing art-focused evaluations rely on synthetic questions and rarely report item-level properties. This paper introduces EduArt, an educational-level benchmark for art-historical knowledge and visual reasoning in multimodal LLMs. EduArt comprises 871 human-authored questions from Italian secondary-school exercises and US Advanced Placement Art History exams, spanning two languages and seven formats from multiple choice to in-text word placement and error identification. Twelve models from six provider families were evaluated under a default answer-only condition and a motivation condition requiring written justification, and characterized using Classical Test Theory and a logistic regression isolating the effects of format, language, image presence, and model. The benchmark showed strong psychometric properties (mean discrimination 0.514, 82.3 percent good discriminators), while multiple-choice accuracy saturated near ceiling for six models, showing recognition formats alone cannot distinguish frontier models. Format was a strong independent predictor of accuracy: models exceeding 94 percent on multiple choice fell to 23.9 percent on open completion (Claude Opus 4.6) and 6.2 percent on error identification (Claude Sonnet 4.6). The motivation condition changed accuracy in a predominantly negative, family-dependent direction. These dissociations indicate that art-historical knowledge and the ability to deploy it are distinct capabilities, and that single-format benchmarks overestimate what models can reliably do. Mapping this capability profile is a precondition for responsible use of multimodal LLMs in art-historical scholarship, where tasks demand producing and manipulating content rather than selecting from fixed options.

</details>


### [130] [OpenSafeIntent: Evaluating Intent-Calibrated Safe Completion Across Dual-Use Prompt Sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02047)
*Rheeya Uppaal,Seungwoo Lyu,Selina Sung,Junjie Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'OpenSafeIntent,' a benchmark to evaluate AI models' ability to remain safe across different intents (benign, dual-use, malicious) for the same task and reveals significant safety failures in existing models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the difficulty of evaluating AI safety when models are tested on isolated prompts rather than calibrated intent shifts. They want models to be safe and helpful in varied contexts or scenarios.

Method: The paper introduced a benchmark called 'OpenSafeIntent,' using controlled datasets containing benign, dual-use, and malicious task variants to evaluate models. This method investigates whether models calibrate their behavior consistently across these intent shifts.

Result: The study finds significant failings in models' ability to maintain safety across intent changes. Safety issues include brittleness to paraphrase changes, unreliable safety in high-level answers on risky topics, and reduced safety when reframing ambiguous requests.

Conclusion: The authors argue that safety in models should be evaluated as intent-calibrated behavior across task variants instead of as a single safety-helpfulness tradeoff on isolated prompts.

Abstract: Safe completion requires models to provide useful assistance without enabling harm, but this behavior is difficult to evaluate with isolated prompts. We introduce OpenSafeIntent, a benchmark of controlled prompt-sets that vary intent while holding the underlying task fixed. Each datapoint contains benign, dual-use, and malicious variants of the same task. This design lets us evaluate whether models calibrate assistance across intent shifts, rather than merely appearing safe on average. Across a broad model suite, we find that prompt-level safety hides important failures: models often fail to remain safe across matched intent variants, dual-use behavior is brittle under paraphrase, high-level answers on risky topics are not reliably safe, and responses that reframe ambiguous requests into safer tasks are substantially less likely to cross the safety boundary. Our results suggest that safe completion should be evaluated as intent-calibrated behavior over controlled task variants, not as a single safety-helpfulness tradeoff over independent prompts.

</details>


### [131] [SPLIT: Cross-Lingual Empathy and Cultural Grounding in English and Ukrainian LLM Responses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02049)
*Anna Chorna*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the multilingual capabilities of large language models (LLMs) in providing emotional support, particularly in English and Ukrainian, using a specialized benchmark called SPLIT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the underexplored aspect of multilingual emotional-support capabilities of LLMs, especially in low-to-mid-resource languages, and to assess their empathetic, natural, and culturally grounded responses.

Method: Introduced SPLIT, a 500-prompt benchmark focusing on Stress, Panic, Loneliness, Internal Displacement, and Tension, and compared three LLMs across empathetic accuracy, linguistic naturalness, and cultural grounding dimensions.

Result: Findings show significant degradation for Gemini-2.5-Flash and LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct in Ukrainian compared to English, while DeepSeek-V3 performs more stably. Human evaluators and AI systems weakly agree on empathy and naturalness but differ on cultural grounding.

Conclusion: Producing text in Ukrainian does not necessarily equate to providing culturally relevant emotional support. The research highlights the necessity for culturally tailored benchmarks and emphasizes the importance of human-centered evaluation methods.

Abstract: Large Language Models are increasingly deployed in emotional-support contexts and crisis-related situations. Nevertheless, their cross-lingual abilities in these circumstances remain underexplored. Existing benchmarks emphasize multilingual performance but rarely examine crisis-related empathy and cultural grounding in low-to-mid-resource languages. We introduce SPLIT, a 500-prompt benchmark designed to evaluate LLM consistency in generating emotionally grounded responses across five categories: Stress, Panic, Loneliness, Internal Displacement, and Tension. We evaluate three technically diverse LLMs across three dimensions: Empathetic Accuracy, Linguistic Naturalness, and Contextual & Cultural Grounding. The framework aims to assess and compare the quality of LLM responses in both English and Ukrainian languages, as well as to explore the reliability of the LLM-as-a-jury paradigm. Our findings reveal that Gemini-2.5-Flash and LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct degrade when transitioning to Ukrainian, while DeepSeek-V3 remains comparatively stable within our benchmark. We additionally find that human and AI evaluators agree weakly on empathy and naturalness but diverge on cultural grounding. We further argue that producing Ukrainian text is not equivalent to producing Ukrainian emotional support. Our findings may assist in the future development of more culturally tailored benchmark designs, as well as encourage a stronger emphasis on human-centered evaluation.

</details>


### [132] [HaloGuard 1.0: An Open Weights Constitutional Classifier for Multilingual AI Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02079)
*Navaneeth Sangameswaran,Preetham S,Ashmiya Lenin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: HaloGuard 1.0, a lightweight safety classifier, achieves state-of-the-art performance in multilingual prompts at 10x smaller size compared to other models, with open weights provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a more efficient, accurate, and open safety guard model for prompt safety tasks, surpassing existing baselines in performance and size efficiency.

Method: Uses a safety constitution of 46 policies, synthetic data with counterfactual generation, a two-tier design targeting false positives, and balanced materialisation across 46 languages. Continuously trained with adversarial attacks.

Result: HaloGuard 1.0 attains top F1 scores (90.9 for 0.8B variant, 92.1 for 4B variant) across benchmarks, outperforming models up to 27B parameters while maintaining low FPR and FNR.

Conclusion: HaloGuard 1.0 offers a scalable, precise, and accessible guard for input safety, with robust performance validated against adversarial challenges and benchmark evaluation.

Abstract: We present HaloGuard 1.0, an open-weights implementation of the constitutional-classifier paradigm for input safety. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on English and multilingual prompt-safety benchmarks at roughly one-tenth the model size of current leading open guard models. The safety constitution is the organising structure of the corpus: a natural-language constitution of 46 policies and 2,940 subcategories drives synthetic data generation, with exhaustive one-to-one paired counterfactuals that hold topic and vocabulary fixed while flipping intent, a two-tier harmless design that separately targets boundary and baseline false positives (FPs), and balanced multilingual materialisation across 46 languages that treats language as a surface form appearing on both sides of the boundary rather than as an adversarial signal. Across seven prompt-safety benchmarks, HaloGuard 1.0-0.8B attains the best average F1 (90.9) of any open guard we evaluate, outperforming baselines up to 27B parameters (over 30 times larger) while holding false-positive rate (FPR) to 4.3 and false-negative rate (FNR) to 9.5. The HaloGuard 1.0-4B variant reaches average F1 of 92.1 and FPR of 3.5, spending its extra capacity on precision rather than recall. A structured adjudication of the remaining failures indicates that most apparent missed-harm cases are benchmark mislabels rather than genuine model misses. An always-on adversarial red-teaming protocol continuously hardens the guard against both content-level and agentic attacks. We release the models as open weights.

</details>


### [133] [Unlocking Speech-Text Compositional Powers: Instruction-Following Speech Language Models without Instruction Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02214)
*Congrui Du,Yang Zhang,Kaizhi Qian,Shiyu Chang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SpeechCombine offers a new approach to create instruction-following Speech Language Models (SLMs) without traditional, resource-intensive instruction tuning processes. Instead, it uses continuous pre-training of a base text LLM on speech data followed by weight merging to transfer knowledge to the speech domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing strategies for training SLMs are difficult to scale because they rely on synthesizing large-scale datasets and replicating text LLM methods for speech data, which has much longer sequences.

Method: SpeechCombine performs continuous pre-training on a text LLM using 30k hours of speech data, creating a speech-adapted model. This model’s weights are then merged with the weight difference of the instruction-tuned vs. base text LLM to transfer capabilities efficiently.

Result: SpeechCombine preserves the knowledge and abilities of the original text LLM while effectively adapting them to the speech modality without massive instruction tuning datasets.

Conclusion: The findings suggest a scalable and efficient approach for SLM training that bypasses large-scale data requirements, offering new possibilities in the transfer of LLM capabilities to speech.

Abstract: Instruction tuning for speech language models (SLMs) is substantially more challenging than for text-based large language models (LLMs), as it requires learning a new modality and a wide range of speech-specific instructions in addition to those supported by text LLMs. Existing SLM training approaches largely replicate the text LLM training paradigm by synthesizing large-scale speech pre-training and instruction-tuning datasets. However, this strategy is difficult to scale, since speech sequences are significantly longer than text sequences. In this paper, we propose SpeechCombine, an instruction-following speech language model trained without any instruction tuning, using only a single round of speech pre-training on 30k hours of data. Starting from a text LLM base model, we perform continuous pre-training on speech utterances to obtain a speech-adapted model, and then directly combine its weights with the weight difference between the instruction-tuned and base versions of the text LLM. Our results show that this simple combination strategy not only preserves the knowledge and capabilities of the original text LLM, but also effectively transfers them to the speech domain. These findings suggest a new direction for SLM training that avoids reliance on massive speech data.

</details>


### [134] [Challenges and Recommendations for LLMs-as-a-Judge in Multilingual Settings and Low-Resource Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02235)
*A. Seza Doğruöz,Xixian Liao,Verena Blaschke,Jakob Prange,Senyu Li,David Ifeoluwa Adelani*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper examines the reliability of LLM-as-a-Judge in multilingual and low-resource language scenarios, identifying issues like inconsistent outcomes and overreliance, while offering recommendations for improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to scrutinize the applicability of the LLM-as-a-Judge paradigm in evaluating natural language generation tasks in multilingual and low-resource settings, due to identified shortcomings in conventional metrics and limited human validations.

Method: An analysis of 650 ACL Anthology papers mentioning LLM-as-a-Judge was conducted, highlighting challenges and inconsistencies in multilingual and low-resource language evaluations.

Result: Only 33 of the analyzed papers addressed multilingual or low-resource contexts. The study revealed a lack of consistency in evaluation outcomes, overtrust in LLM judgments, and a reliance on single judge models.

Conclusion: The paper recommends strategies to improve the use of LLM-as-a-Judge in multilingual and low-resource settings, fostering robust and reliable evaluation practices in NLP research.

Abstract: LLM-as-a-Judge has become the dominant evaluation paradigm for many natural language generation tasks, due to shortcomings of conventional metrics and high correlations with human judgment, albeit mostly in English. There are now attempts to extend LLM-as-a-Judge to multilingual settings including low-resource languages. However, LLMs have limited proficiency in low-resource languages, and there is often no adequate human validation in these settings. To highlight the scope of the problem and current practices, we explore the use of LLM-as-a-Judge evaluators in ACL Anthology papers focusing on multilingual settings and low-resource languages across a diverse set of tasks. Out of 650 papers mentioning LLM-as-a-judge, only 33 of them focus on low-resource or multilingual settings. Our in-depth analysis of these papers indicates inconsistent evaluation outcomes, a tendency to overtrust LLM judgments in multilingual settings, and the widespread reliance on a single judge model per study. To help the NLP community further, we conclude with recommendations about how to use LLM-as-a-Judge in multilingual and low-resource settings.

</details>


### [135] [BamiBERT: A New BERT-based Language Model for Vietnamese](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02259)
*Dat Quoc Nguyen,Thinh Pham,Chi Tran,Linh The Nguyen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introducing BamiBERT, a Vietnamese BERT-based language model outperforming the current standard PhoBERT in various benchmarks, supporting extended context length and eliminating word segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of PhoBERT, such as shorter context processing and the reliance on word segmentation, by introducing a more advanced Vietnamese language model.

Method: Developed BamiBERT, a BERT-based language model, trained from scratch on 129GB of general-domain Vietnamese text for 20 epochs, designed to support up to 2048 tokens without word segmentation.

Result: BamiBERT achieves state-of-the-art results in Vietnamese NLP benchmarks, outperforming PhoBERT in 11 out of 15 metrics and ranking second-best in 3 metrics.

Conclusion: BamiBERT establishes itself as the new state-of-the-art Vietnamese language model, with robust cross-domain performance and improved efficiency by avoiding external word segmentation.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce BamiBERT, a new BERT-based pre-trained language model for Vietnamese that addresses key limitations of PhoBERT -- the current de facto Vietnamese text encoder. Trained from scratch on a 129GB corpus of general-domain Vietnamese text for 20 epochs, BamiBERT supports an extended context length of up to 2048 tokens and operates directly on raw input, eliminating the need for external word segmentation. Across 8 Vietnamese benchmarks, it achieves the best score on 11 of 15 metrics and the second-best on 3 others, setting a new state of the art among "base"-sized Vietnamese encoders and demonstrating strong cross-domain generalization. We release BamiBERT at: https://huggingface.co/Qualcomm-AI-Research/BamiBERT

</details>


### [136] [CheckRLM: Effective Knowledge-Thought Coherence Checking in Retrieval-Augmented Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02262)
*Dingling Xu,Ruobing Wang,Qingfei Zhao,Yukun Yan,Zhichun Wang,Daren Zha,Shi Yu,Zhenghao Liu,Shuo Wang,Xu Han,Maosong Sun*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CheckRLM, a framework that enhances reasoning in language models by using Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to identify and fix factual inaccuracies in reasoning chains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models struggle with factual inaccuracies in reasoning chains, especially in knowledge-intensive tasks. There is a need to improve the reliability and coherence of these reasoning processes.

Method: CheckRLM uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to extract and verify factual claims within reasoning chains. If errors are detected, it employs a minimal-cost refinement mechanism to correct them using external knowledge.

Result: CheckRLM achieves significant improvements over existing baselines, effectively reducing error accumulation in long and complex reasoning tasks while maintaining lower cost.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that CheckRLM enhances the reliability of reasoning language models by integrating error detection and correction mechanisms based on external knowledge.

Abstract: Reasoning Language Models (RLMs) have significantly improved performance on complex tasks by extending the reasoning chain. However, these chains are prone to containing factual errors, particularly in knowledge-intensive tasks. To address this issue, we propose CheckRLM, a framework that improves the reliability of the reasoning process through Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) by timely checking and correcting factual errors. Specifically, CheckRLM extracts factual claims from the reasoning chain to identify and localize subtle knowledge inconsistencies during inference. Upon detection of errors, a refinement mechanism performs minimal-cost yet precise corrections by leveraging external knowledge, ensuring coherence between the reasoning chain and correct knowledge. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CheckRLM substantially outperforms existing baselines, exhibiting a strong capability to mitigate error accumulation in long-horizon reasoning with lower costs. The code and data are available at https://github.com/AI9Stars/CheckRLM.

</details>


### [137] [On the Role of Directionality in Structural Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02307)
*Zichao Wei*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a redesign of a symbolic backend for parsing, using CCG directed types, which improves performance on directional tasks but reveals bottlenecks in neural layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of the AM-Parser's non-directional symbolic backend and improve parsing performance, particularly for tasks requiring directional distinctions.

Method: The authors implement a redesigned symbolic backend based on CCG directed types, employing a deterministic CKY algorithm and a single linear decoder, paired with a BERT-base encoder, and later test improvements with DeBERTa-v3-large.

Result: The CCG-directed system achieves a significant performance boost (75.9% vs 70.8% LF exact match), particularly excelling in directional categories. Switching to a DeBERTa-v3-large encoder increases performance further to 90.7%, showing complementary improvements.

Conclusion: Using CCG-directed types enables better handling of directional distinctions while creating scope for further improvements through neural encoder upgrades, shifting the performance bottleneck.

Abstract: Several SLOG test categories explicitly involve directional distinctions (modifier position shifts, argument extraction positions), yet AM-Parser, the previous SOTA, uses an AM algebra whose operations do not encode direction. We redesign the symbolic backend around CCG directed types (deterministic CKY + single linear decoder, 30K learnable parameters). Under the same BERT-base encoder, the system achieves 75.9$\pm$6.4% LF exact match, surpassing AM-Parser (70.8$\pm$4.3%). Per SLOG's own category groupings, gains are highly directional: the CCG system outperforms AM-Parser on all 5 position-shift categories (+29.9pp), while AM-Parser outperforms on all 6 recursive-depth categories. Replacing the encoder with DeBERTa-v3-large yields 90.7$\pm$4.9%, with the largest encoder gains in recursive-depth categories, complementary to directionality's gains. Directional representations shift the bottleneck from the symbolic layer (AM-Parser's 0% category ceiling) to the neural layer, which improves with encoder upgrades.

</details>


### [138] [World Wide Models: Literary Tools for Cultural AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02369)
*Nina Begus*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper examines the intersections of literature and AI, proposing a framework for culturally literate AI using tools from literary disciplines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges posed by large language models (LLMs) being monolingual and automated, requiring nuanced cultural and textual understanding.

Method: The paper uses a layered framework combining tools from literary analysis, critical theory, and world literature approaches to enhance AI's cultural literacy.

Result: The study connects current critical theory debates with the issue of structural monolingualism and identifies the role of macrostructure and untranslatability in global AI textuality.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that leveraging literary disciplines can lead to more pluralistic and culturally nuanced AI models, advancing global understanding.

Abstract: LLMs stage a new form of cultural encounter that is massive, automated, and monolingual. Literary disciplines have always negotiated cultural struggles with comparative reading of literature, narratological and poetic analysis, critical theory, world literature, and translation. These tools have now become indispensable for building culturally literate AI. The essay develops a layered framework toward more nuanced textual models and pluralistic interpretations of AI, emphasizing the natural intersections of literature and AI development, connecting current debates in critical theory with structural monolingualism, and suggesting a new application of world literature approaches to address global AI textuality through macrostructure, circulation, and untranslatability.

</details>


### [139] [HULAT2 at MER-TRANS 2026: Governed Multi-Agent Simplification for Spanish Easy-to-Read Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02381)
*Lourdes Moreno,Paloma Martínez,Marco Antonio Sanchez-Escudero,Miguel Domínguez-Gómez*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The HULAT2-UC3M team participated in MER-TRANS 2026 for Spanish Easy-to-Read translation with three automated approaches, showing that multi-agent workflows outperformed simple baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop and evaluate multilingual Easy-to-Read translation models using advanced workflows and assess their effectiveness via official metrics.

Method: The study used a LangGraph-based multi-agent workflow featuring tools like Gemini 2.5 Flash and RigoChat-7B-v2 for parallel generation and quality control, alongside a baseline with regenerate strategies via RigoChat and prompt engineering.

Result: Among three runs, RUN1 achieved the highest performance (SARI: 44.0543), demonstrating the strength of the multi-agent workflow over the baseline; however, added lexical resources did not improve reference-based scores.

Conclusion: Signal-guided multi-agent routing is a promising approach for Easy-to-Read translation, but further analysis is necessary to improve user-oriented adequacy and consistency.

Abstract: This paper describes the participation of HULAT2-UC3M in the Spanish track of MER-TRANS 2026, a shared task on multilingual Easy-to-Read translation. Three fully automatic Spanish runs were submitted. RUN1 and RUN2 used a LangGraph-based multi-agent workflow combining Gemini 2.5 Flash and RigoChat-7B-v2, parallel generation strategies, internal quality signals, Event-Condition-Action routing, controlled editing and traceable decisions. RUN1 used the base workflow, while RUN2 activated an additional lexical-support layer based on a glossary and lexical resources. RUN3 was a RigoChat-based generate-evaluate-regenerate baseline with prompt engineering and LoRA-based adaptation. The official leaderboard reports BLEU-Orig, BLEU-Gold, SARI and BERTScore. During development, additional internal signals were also inspected, including semantic fidelity, readability, lexical simplicity, syntactic clarity and factual consistency. According to official SARI, RUN1 was the best HULAT2 run, with 44.0543 points, followed by RUN2 with 43.1049 and RUN3 with 38.5136. These results indicate that, in this task setting, signal-guided multi-agent routing outperformed the linear regeneration baseline. They also show that adding lexical support did not automatically improve reference-based scores. Further segment-level and document-level analysis are required to assess readability, factual consistency and user-oriented adequacy.

</details>


### [140] [Know Your Source: A Public Knowledge Store for Media Background Checks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02383)
*Benjamin Nichols,Michael Schlichtkrull,Nedjma Ousidhoum*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces MEDIAREF, a public knowledge base for generating media background checks (MBCs) in fact-checking work, enhancing reproducibility and evaluation efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to tackle the challenge in fact-checking where retrieved evidence can be unreliable, outdated, or biased. Existing systems assume evidence reliability, and generating media background checks (MBCs) has been costly and unreproducible due to reliance on proprietary APIs.

Method: The authors developed MEDIAREF, a publicly available repository of web-sourced documents covering 200 media sources. They employed a reproducible methodology to ensure the collection can be updated and evaluated for generating MBCs.

Result: MEDIAREF enables reproducible, cost-effective evaluation and supports improved quality of MBC generation in fact-checking tasks. Widely used LLMs are assessed on this task with positive results.

Conclusion: MEDIAREF addresses prior limitations of MBC generation by providing an accessible and updateable resource, enhancing reproducibility, quality, and reducing costs in fact-checking workflows.

Abstract: LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is increasingly used for automated fact-checking (AFC) and related tasks. By grounding LLM outputs in retrieved evidence, RAG-based systems provide transparent justifications while allowing external information to be updated independently of the underlying model. However, existing approaches often assume retrieved evidence is reliable, although real-world information may be conflicting, outdated, and can originate from unreliable or biased sources. Recent work on *source-critical reasoning* addresses this challenge through media background checks (MBCs) (Schlichtkrull, 2024), which assess the credibility of evidence sources to support downstream fact verification. However, generating MBCs relies on costly proprietary search APIs, limiting reproducibility. To mitigate this issue, we introduce MEDIAREF, a publicly available knowledge store of web-sourced documents that enables reproducible, low-cost evaluation of MBC generation across 200 media sources. We describe a reproducible methodology for constructing and updating the collection, assess widely used LLMs on the MBC generation task, and demonstrate that MEDIAREF supports higher-quality MBC generation through both automatic and qualitative evaluation.

</details>


### [141] [The Future of NLP may not be at NLP Conferences: Scholarly Migration Patterns in Natural Language Processing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02416)
*David Jurgens*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: NLP researchers are increasingly publishing in general ML venues due to factors like citation benefits, especially post-LLM advancements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To examine whether the rise of LLMs has shifted NLP research publication trends between core NLP venues and general ML venues.

Method: Analyzed publication data of established and new NLP authors from 2010 to 2026, comparing publication trends pre- and post-LLM era using causal inference techniques.

Result: Established authors shifted publication preferences toward Findings and ML venues post-LLM. Newer authors moved from heavily favoring ACL venues (84%) in 2019 to a more balanced preference, with 21% choosing general ML venues by 2024. General ML venues offer a citation advantage.

Conclusion: There is a notable disciplinary shift, with NLP research increasingly gravitating toward general ML venues.

Abstract: Natural Language Processing (NLP) has traditionally been published in its core disciplinary venues like ACL. However, advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) has led to a blurring of the disciplinary lines between NLP and general Machine Learning (ML), with authors regularly publishing in venues from both fields. Here, we ask whether the disciplinary center of gravity is shifting. Using NLP research published from 2010 to 2026 and studies of both established and new authors, we find that a migration is taking place. First, comparing the pre- and post-LLM eras, established authors lost 19.2pp of share at flagship *ACL main-conference tracks while gaining 14.8pp in the newer Findings tracks, and general ML venues rose 8.6pp, even when adjusting for parallel growth in the fields. Second, among newer authors who debut with at least three first-author NLP-topic papers, the share whose work appears mostly at *ACL venues fell from 84% (2019) to 74% (2024), while the share appearing mostly at general ML venues rose from 5% to 21%. Using causal inference techniques, we estimate that these general ML venues confer a significant citation premium, which influences venue selection. Together, these results point to a significant shift in where NLP research is published.

</details>


### [142] [Language Models as Measurement Apparatus for Culture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02459)
*Kent K. Chang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores how NLP tools used to quantify cultural phenomena shape, rather than merely record, the cultural realities they analyze, proposing a theory-driven and ethically conscious approach to research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to understand the distinctiveness of cultural measurements using language models and critique the assumption that such tools passively record cultural phenomena.

Method: The paper utilizes Karen Barad's concept of the agential cut to analyze how the design of language models (including data, annotations, and evaluation) shapes cultural interpretations. It employs three case studies and examines aspects of the model design itself.

Result: Findings reveal that language model design choices actively shape the cultural boundaries they seek to measure and that language models already embed significant cultural material.

Conclusion: The paper advocates for a research approach that recognizes and incorporates the ethical and methodological implications of the intertwined relationship between analytical tools and the cultural realities they measure.

Abstract: Language models are increasingly used to quantify cultural phenomena, but what makes such measurement distinctively cultural? This paper argues that NLP work on culture is a material-discursive practice: the apparatus -- model, data, annotation, evaluation -- participates in constituting the cultural reality it measures, rather than passively recording it. Drawing on Karen Barad's concept of the agential cut -- the contingent boundary between phenomenon and instrument -- I show that the apparatus's substantive design choices draw such boundaries, and that the boundary is entangled from the start because language models have already internalized much of the cultural material they measure. I illustrate this through three case studies on television and film dialogue (measuring structure, interaction, and deviation) and three examinations of the apparatus itself (erasure of cultural markers, attunement to historical material, and agency in an agentic workflow). This big picture analysis proposes a research program that is theory-driven, empirically rigorous, and culturally contingent, treating each agential cut as a conscious commitment, at once methodological and ethical.

</details>


### [143] [Will Scaling Improve Social Simulation with LLMs?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02464)
*Caleb Ziems,William Held,Su Doga Karaca,David Grusky,Tatsunori Hashimoto,Diyi Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether scaling in large language models (LLMs) improves the fidelity of social simulations across opinion modeling, behavioral simulation, and longitudinal forecasting. Results show strong scaling improvements in most domains but identify limitations in low-resource settings and human cognitive biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to understand whether scaling LLMs can effectively close gaps in social simulation fidelity or if specific research attention is required for improvement in challenging sub-domains.

Method: A scaling study using 85 transformer LLMs with Qwen3 architecture pre-trained on a fixed compute budget and evaluations of 35 larger models up to 70B parameters to correlate model loss with downstream task performance.

Result: Scaling improves opinion modeling and behavioral simulation significantly, especially for well-represented populations. However, tasks involving longitudinal forecasting, underrepresented opinions, and human biases scale less effectively.

Conclusion: Scaling enhances social simulations broadly, but improvements are less reliable in low-resource and specific human cognitive bias-related scenarios.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) social simulations are a promising research method, but they are not yet faithful enough to be adopted widely. In this work, we investigate whether the current scaling paradigm in language modeling is likely to close these gaps, or whether simulation fidelity is orthogonal to general capabilities and therefore deserving of more research attention. We use scaling laws to study the relationship between LLMs' compute scale, general capability benchmarks, and the fidelity of social simulation in three representative sub-domains: opinion modeling, behavioral simulation, and longitudinal forecasting. Surprisingly, we discover strong compute scaling in all three settings, using a suite of 85 transformer LLMs with the Qwen3 architecture pre-trained on the DCLM web text corpus under fixed-compute budgets from $10^{18}$ to $10^{20}$ FLOPs. Then we evaluate 35 larger and more capable open-weight models up to 70B parameters, allowing us to predict downstream accuracy from loss. This reveals that the majority of behavioral and opinion simulation tasks will rapidly improve with scale, particularly when they involve populations that are well-represented in English web corpora. Longitudinal forecasting and underrepresented opinions scale more slowly, especially when they are less correlated with general knowledge and reasoning benchmarks like MMLU. In behavior simulation, scaling fails to improve model calibration with human cognitive biases like risk aversion, as well as human heuristics like learning correlated rewards from related tasks. On these tasks, even fine-tuned models fail to noticeably scale up performance from 0.5B to 8B parameters. Taken together, we conclude that scale will improve social simulations in most settings, but outliers exist, and improvements will be less reliable in low-resource domains.

</details>


### [144] [Audio-Based Understanding of Audiobook Narration Appeal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02473)
*Shahar Elisha,Mariano Beguerisse-Díaz,Emmanouil Benetos*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates how narration qualities like tone, pace, and loudness affect audiobook consumption and engagement across genres and titles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to understand the impact of narration dynamics on audiobook appeal and listener engagement to aid personalization and narrator casting.

Method: Pre-trained audio models were used to extract vocal and acoustic features, which were analyzed in relation to consumption data, including genre and title considerations.

Result: Acoustic features are strongly associated with audiobook appeal, even after accounting for title effects. These results were validated with more granular engagement metrics.

Conclusion: Narration qualities significantly influence audiobook success, pointing to the value of computational approaches in enhancing personalization and narrator selection.

Abstract: Narration is central to the audiobook listening experience, shaping how listeners engage with and understand the content. This work explores how narration qualities shape an audiobook's appeal, noting that their effects can vary by genre, title, and audience. We extract vocal and acoustic features (e.g., tone, pace, loudness) from LibriVox using pre-trained audio models and analyse their relationship with consumption data (specifically, view-rate) and their interplay with genre and title. Despite limited consumption data, we find that acoustic information alone has a robust association with appeal, even after accounting for title effects. We further validate these findings using more nuanced proprietary engagement metrics. To our knowledge, this is the first systematic computational study linking narration qualities, genre, title, and audiobook consumption, highlighting the potential of data-driven insights to improve audiobook personalisation and narrator casting.

</details>


### [145] [Visually Grounded Self-Reflection for Vision-Language Models via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02490)
*Liyan Tang,Fangcong Yin,Greg Durrett*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces VRRL, a reinforcement learning framework that enables vision-language models to better perform visually grounded self-reflection, improving their ability to handle out-of-distribution scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing large vision-language models often struggle with visual grounding during self-reflection, particularly when exposed to out-of-distribution inputs, leading to inaccurate corrections of prior errors.

Method: VRRL employs masked trajectory prefixes to train recovery abilities from incorrect predictions and incorporates buffered roll-ins via an experience replay buffer to expose models to diverse failure states.

Result: The proposed VRRL framework improves out-of-distribution accuracy on visual grounding and navigation tasks compared to standard methods, showing stronger adaptability under distribution shifts.

Conclusion: By focusing on visually grounded self-reflection, VRRL significantly enhances the generalization capabilities of vision-language models, offering solutions to address distribution shift challenges.

Abstract: Large vision-language models can reason over multimodal inputs by generating textual chains of thought (CoT). A key capability exhibited in CoT reasoning is self-reflection: revisiting earlier decisions and correcting previous errors. However, existing LVLMs often fail to properly attend to visual inputs during reflection, limiting their ability to translate feedback into grounded corrections, especially for out-of-distribution images. To address this issue, we propose a novel reinforcement learning training framework VRRL, with two components explicitly designed to elicit visually grounded self-reflection. First, we randomly mask trajectory prefixes during training to emphasize recovery from incorrect intermediate predictions rather than making early mistakes. Second, we introduce buffered roll-ins from an experience replay buffer to expose the model to diverse failure states that it must learn to correct. We evaluate our approach on visual grounding tasks involving tables and charts, as well as spatial navigation benchmarks. While off-the-shelf and conventionally fine-tuned models degrade substantially under distribution shift, our method substantially improves average out-of-distribution accuracy over standard RL and reflection-oriented fine-tuning baselines by using self-reflection effectively.

</details>


### [146] [Reasoning LLM Improves Speaker Recognition in Long-form TV Dramas](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02504)
*Yuxuan Li,Lingxi Xie,Xinyue Huo,Jihao Qiu,Jiacheng Shao,Pengfei Chen,Jiannan Ge,Kaiwen Duan,Qi Tian*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces DramaSR-532K, a benchmark dataset for speaker recognition in TV dramas, and proposes DramaSR-LRM, a multimodal reasoning model that excels in attributing dialogue lines to characters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenges of understanding TV dramas by improving speaker recognition, which is essential for interpreting complex storylines.

Method: The authors present a large-scale dataset (DramaSR-532K) and a reasoning model (DramaSR-LRM) that integrates auditory, linguistic, and visual cues through multimodal tool-use.

Result: DramaSR-LRM surpasses existing solutions, especially for short utterances where acoustic signals are unreliable.

Conclusion: The proposed dataset and model substantially improve speaker recognition in TV dramas, enabling better storyline comprehension.

Abstract: Long-form TV dramas present a formidable challenge for comprehensive video understanding, where deciphering complex storyline often relies on \textbf{speaker recognition}, the task of accurately attributing each spoken utterance to its respective character. In this paper, we advance this field through two primary contributions. (1) We introduce \textbf{DramaSR-532K}, a large-scale benchmark comprising 532K annotated dialogue lines across more than 900 unique characters, necessitating the integration of auditory, linguistic, and visual cues for speaker recognition. (2) We propose \textbf{DramaSR-LRM}, a robust approach built upon a large reasoning model (LRM). DramaSR-LRM is designed to autonomously aggregate contextual evidence via multimodal tool-use, synthesizing diverse inputs to achieve high-fidelity attribution. Experimental results demonstrate that DramaSR-LRM significantly outperforms existing baselines, particularly on short utterances where acoustic biometrics are inherently unreliable. \textit{All the data and code will be made publicly available at the project page: https://www.github.com/198808xc/DramaSR-LRM.}

</details>


### [147] [LACUNA: A Testbed for Evaluating Localization Precision for LLM Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02513)
*Matteo Boglioni,Thibault Rousset,Siva Reddy,Marius Mosbach,Verna Dankers*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses the limitations in current unlearning methods for large language models (LLMs), develops the LACUNA testbed for precise parameter-level evaluation, and highlights weaknesses in state-of-the-art methods against resurfacing attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to mitigate the risks caused by LLMs memorizing sensitive data, such as personally identifiable information (PII), and addresses the need for reliable methods to erase such knowledge without merely obfuscating it.

Method: The researchers introduced LACUNA, a novel testbed with controlled injection of PII data into predefined model parameters using masked continual pretraining. They benchmarked SOTA unlearning strategies to assess their effectiveness.

Result: Current unlearning methods were shown to lack precision and were vulnerable to resurfacing attacks, while parameter-level localization significantly enhanced unlearning efficiency and robustness.

Conclusion: Precise localization-based unlearning methods are essential to reliably remove sensitive data from LLMs while preventing future resurfacing issues. Releasing LACUNA supports ongoing research in robust data erasure.

Abstract: LLMs memorize sensitive training data, including personally identifiable information (PII), creating a pressing need for reliable post hoc removal methods. Unlearning has emerged as a promising solution, with state-of-the-art(SOTA) methods often following a localize-first, unlearn-second paradigm that targets specific model parameters. However, existing benchmarks evaluate unlearning solely at the output level, leaving open the question of whether unlearning truly erases knowledge from a model's parameters or merely obfuscates it, a concern reinforced by the success of resurfacing attacks. To bridge this gap, we introduce LACUNA: the first unlearning testbed with ground-truth parameter-level localization. LACUNA injects PII of synthetic individuals into predefined parameters of 1B and 7B OLMo-based models via masked continual pretraining, enabling direct evaluation of whether unlearning targets the weights responsible for knowledge storage. We use LACUNA to benchmark current SOTA unlearning methods and find that, despite strong output-level performance, existing methods are highly imprecise and susceptible to resurfacing attacks. We further show that when localization is successful, even a simple gradient-based unlearning method achieves strong erasure and robustness to resurfacing attacks, highlighting the importance of precise unlearning. We release LACUNA to complement behavioral evaluations and drive further advances in robust, localization-based unlearning.

</details>


<div id='cs.CV'></div>

# cs.CV [[Back]](#toc)

### [148] [AnchorSplat: Fast and Structure Consistent Detail Synthesis for Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01290)
*Dexu Zhu,Jiangnan Shao,Xiaofeng Wang,Junxian Duan,Jie Cao,Zheng Zhu,Huaibo Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AnchorSplat is a novel 3D-native refinement method for high-fidelity rendering that avoids multi-view inconsistencies and high computational costs, achieving state-of-the-art results and exceptional speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address quality issues in 3D Gaussian Splatting representations, like missing details and texture noise, without causing inconsistencies or high computational overhead.

Method: AnchorSplat uses a Point Anchor Mechanism for geometric consistency and replaces iterative densification with a single-pass mechanism. It is end-to-end, source-free, and operates directly on 3D structures.

Result: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on the 3DGS-SR benchmark, with throughput up to 100,000 times faster than traditional optimization methods. It also generalizes well across diverse data types.

Conclusion: AnchorSplat significantly improves quality and efficiency for 3D asset refinement, providing a scalable and versatile approach for various data scenarios, including generative outputs and real-world scans.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a powerful representation for high-fidelity rendering. However, existing assets often suffer from quality bottlenecks such as missing details and texture noise. Prior attempts to enhance these assets via 2D image processing introduce multi-view inconsistencies and high computational costs. In this paper, we propose a novel 3D-native refinement paradigm named AnchorSplat. AnchorSplat is an end-to-end deep network operating directly on 3D structures, avoiding the expensive optimization overhead of traditional 3D-2D-3D pipelines. Crucially, AnchorSplat is a strictly source-free solution requiring no original multi-view images. Central to the proposed method is the Point Anchor Mechanism, which enforces geometric consistency via local offset constraints, mitigating ill-posed mapping and gradient confounding. Furthermore, AnchorSplat replaces iterative densification with a single-pass multiplication mechanism. To facilitate research, we construct 3DGS-SR, the first large-scale benchmark for this task. Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art results on the 3DGS-SR dataset, with throughput up to $10^5$ times faster than optimization methods. Notably, AnchorSplat exhibits robust zero-shot generalization across diverse data distributions, including generative model outputs and real-world scans.

</details>


### [149] [CPG-PAD: Concept-Informed Prompts Guided Presentation Attack Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01303)
*Haoyuan Zhang,Xiangyu Zhu,Li Gao,Ajian Liu,Siran Peng,Zhen Lei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces CPG-PAD, a framework using concept-informed prompts to enhance generalization in Presentation Attack Detection (PAD) across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PAD models struggle with unseen domains due to variations in sensors, lighting, and attack materials. VLMs, despite their potential, haven't been fully effective for PAD because of limited alignment with attack-relevant semantics.

Method: CPG-PAD integrates PAD-relevant concepts into prompts using a Visual Concept-driven Enhancement module and concept injection via a Visual-Prompt Decoder and concept-mapping loss.

Result: CPG-PAD exhibits state-of-the-art cross-domain performance across nine benchmark datasets under various experimental settings.

Conclusion: The proposed CPG-PAD framework enhances PAD by capturing domain-invariant attack cues and suppressing dataset biases, proving effective for cross-domain generalization.

Abstract: Presentation Attack Detection (PAD) serves as a crucial safeguard for face recognition systems against presentation attacks such as printed photos, replayed videos, and 3D masks. Despite significant progress, existing PAD models still struggle to generalize across unseen domains due to variations in sensors, lighting, and attack materials. Recent Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown strong generalization ability, yet their applications in PAD remain limited because learned prompts, typically optimized under class-label supervision, fail to explicitly align with fine-grained attack-relevant visual semantics. As a result, the learned representations often overfit domain-specific artifacts instead of capturing transferable attack cues. To address this, we propose Concept-Informed Prompts Guided Presentation Attack Detection (CPG-PAD), a framework that introduces model-level concept guidance into the prompt learning process.
