### [292] [Domain Knowledge Based Temporal-Spatial Graph Convolution Network for ECG Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01282)
*Wenting Ma,Zhipeng Zhang,Xiaohang Yuan,Ningwei Xie,Yuxin Xie,Xiaolin Wang,Meng Guo,Xingang Chai,Zhenjie Yao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a domain knowledge-based graph convolution network for ECG recognition, achieving improved F1 scores especially in rare categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address interpretability challenges in AI models applied to specialized domains like healthcare, particularly ECG recognition.

Method: The method involves integrating domain knowledge (key ECG landmarks) into a double-stream directed graph comprising spatial and temporal directed graphs.

Result: The proposed model achieves an overall average F1 score of 88.1% and 76.3% for rare categories, outperforming state-of-the-art models.

Conclusion: Incorporating domain knowledge into the model enhances detection performance, particularly for challenging rare ECG categories.

Abstract: In light of strides in Arti cial Intelligence (AI) and its wide spread application, challenges persist in the interpretability of AI models, particularly within specialized domains like healthcare, such as electro cardiograph (ECG) recognition. Rather than relying solely on end-to-end convolutional neural networks, this paper introduces a novel approach using a domain knowledge-based graph convolution network for ECG recognition. Key landmarks points of PRQST, vital to ECG interpreta tion, are incorporated as domain knowledge. The double-stream directed graph is employed to model both intra and inter ECG cycles. Speci cally, spatial directed graphs capture the positional relationships among key points, while temporal directed graphs delineate temporal dependencies between adjacent cycles in extended ECG sequences. Experimental re sults on the First Chinese ECG Intelligent Competition dataset, which speci cally classify ECG into nine categories, prove the e cacy of the proposed model. The overall average F1 score is 88.1%, the average F1 score of rare categories is 76.3%, both outperform the state-of-the-art models. The introduction of domain knowledge did enhance the detec tion performance, especially for rare categories.

</details>


### [293] [Scaling Laws for Grid-Based Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search in High Dimensions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01283)
*Matthew J Liu,Wei Hang Zheng,Vidhan Purohit,Siqi Xie,Chieh-En Li,Jerry Li,Noah Flynn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multiprobe grid algorithms for approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search demonstrate dimensional scaling advantages over other methods, particularly in high-dimensional or rebuild-heavy settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in modern scaling analyses of grid-based ANN search methods and explore their performance characteristics in comparison to other ANN approaches.

Method: The study conducts systematic characterization and experiments on a multiprobe grid algorithm, evaluating its dimensional scalability and indexing costs on datasets like the GloVe embedding family.

Result: Multiprobe grid search shows robust performance with constant dimensional scaling, near-linear query scaling with dataset size $N$, and lower indexing costs, outperforming graph-, tree-, and partitioning-based ANN methods in certain scenarios.

Conclusion: Grid-based ANN methods like multiprobe grid are competitive options in high-dimensional or rebuild-heavy use cases where indexing cost and dimensional robustness are critical. These findings also have implications for evaluating the efficiency of transformer architectures.

Abstract: Grid-based approaches to approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search have been absent from modern scaling analyses. We present a systematic characterization of a multiprobe grid algorithm with respect to dataset size $N$ and dimensionality $d$. Our experiments reveal a previously unreported $d$-scaling crossover on the GloVe embedding family, in which multiprobe grid search maintains an approximately constant dimensional scaling exponent while other graph-, tree-, and partitioning-based methods exhibit degrading throughput. The advantage comes with near-linear query scaling in $N$, but also with lower indexing cost than competing ANN methods. Our results suggest that grid-based methods such as multiprobe grid may be competitive in rebuild-heavy or high-dimensional settings where indexing cost and dimensional robustness dictate performance. More broadly, recent work has formalized self-attention as an ANN operation. Thus, the $N$- and $d$-scaling properties of ANN algorithms may guide cost analysis of efficient transformer architectures. Code is available at: https://github.com/weiz345/MultiProbeANN.

</details>


### [294] [IonSense-QKG: A Quantum-Readiness Metadata Framework for Lithium-Ion Battery Dataset Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01286)
*Sakthi Prabhu Gunasekar,Prasanna Kumar Rangarajan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces IonSense-QKG, a framework with quantum-readiness metadata to streamline lithium-ion battery dataset discovery for hybrid quantum-classical workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Increasing use of lithium-ion battery datasets in applications like state-of-health estimation and anomaly detection necessitates efficient dataset discovery, especially for quantum-classical workflows.

Method: IonSense-QKG enriches battery dataset records with quantum-relevant metadata and introduces a Quantum Readiness Score for ranking datasets.

Result: The framework enables query-based dataset discovery and provides tools including metadata tables, scoring scripts, and query utilities to support quantum-centric battery analytics.

Conclusion: IonSense-QKG facilitates dataset selection for hybrid quantum-classical applications, enhancing data management and research reproducibility in battery analytics.

Abstract: Public lithium-ion battery datasets are increasingly used for state-of-health estimation, remaining-useful-life prediction, anomaly detection, electrochemical diagnostics, second-life analytics, and battery safety research. However, these datasets vary substantially in chemistry, modality, scale, label quality, sequence structure, access status, and preprocessing complexity. These differences directly affect whether a dataset is feasible for near-term hybrid quantum-classical machine-learning workflows.
  This paper presents IonSense-QKG, a quantum-readiness metadata framework for lithium-ion battery dataset discovery. Starting from the EV-Battery-IonSense index, the proposed framework enriches public battery dataset records with quantum-relevant metadata, including task type, sensing modality, chemistry, label availability, sequence type, preprocessing requirements, candidate quantum encodings, estimated qubit range, and NISQ feasibility. A transparent Quantum Readiness Score is introduced to rank datasets as candidate resources for future hybrid quantum-classical battery benchmarks. The score is intended as a dataset-selection heuristic, not as evidence of quantum advantage.
  The framework demonstrates query-based discovery over enriched metadata to identify datasets suitable for compact quantum feature maps, quantum time-series workflows, limited-label anomaly detection, and future battery-health benchmarking. The released artifact includes metadata tables, scoring scripts, robustness checks, link-checking utilities, and SQL-style query examples. IonSense-QKG positions dataset selection as a data-management problem and provides a reproducible foundation for data-centric quantum battery analytics.

</details>


### [295] [A Novel Machine Learning Approach for Central Nervous System Tumor Classification from DNA Methylation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01307)
*Paulo R. Ferreira,Lucas Coutinho Freitas,Laís dos Santos Gonçalves,William Borges Domingues,Lucas Petitemberte de Souza,Mariana B. Michalowski,Vinicius F. Campos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a machine-learning method for CNS tumor classification, achieving higher accuracy than the state-of-the-art reference model and improving clinical outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for CNS tumor classification face challenges in accuracy, transferability across cohorts, and robust evaluation.

Method: The paper combines Sparse Random Projection for dimensionality reduction with multinomial logistic regression for classification.

Result: The proposed method achieved 96% accuracy on the reference cohort and improved accuracy by approximately 4-5% compared to the previous state-of-the-art model in independent cohort evaluations.

Conclusion: The novel approach improves CNS tumor classification accuracy, strengthens methodological reliability, and has significant clinical implications for cancer diagnosis and treatment decisions.

Abstract: NA methylation profiling has become a powerful approach for central nervous system (CNS) tumor classification, yet important challenges remain regarding cross-cohort transferability, methodological correctness, and robust multiclass evaluation. In this work, we propose a novel and methodologically rigorous machine-learning approach for methylation-based CNS tumor classification that combines Sparse Random Projection for dimensionality reduction with multinomial logistic regression for classification. We evaluate the proposed approach in the same general experimental setting established by a widely used reference classifier. On the 2,801-sample reference cohort, our method achieves a mean accuracy of 96\% under stratified 3-fold cross-validation. On the independent 1,104-sample clinical evaluation cohort, it reaches 86\% accuracy at the 91-class level and 93\% when predictions are evaluated at the methylation class family level. These results improve upon the corresponding state-of-the-art reference figures of 82\% class-level concordance and 88\% family-level concordance, yielding absolute gains of approximately 4 and 5 percentage points, respectively. This improvement is clinically relevant: in a diagnostic setting, a 5-point increase in correct tumor classification can directly affect cancer subtype assignment and, in turn, influence treatment selection and downstream clinical decision-making. Our results show that the proposed model, grounded in stronger methodological practice in machine learning, consistently outperforms the previous state of the art across evaluation settings and can materially improve the reliability of CNS tumor classification.

</details>


### [296] [Predicting Early Stages Of Alzheimer's Disease And Identifying Key Biomarkers Using Deep Artificial Neural Network And Ensemble Of Machine Learning Methodologies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02142)
*Debopriya Ghosh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a machine learning model for early detection of Alzheimer's disease (AD) using clinical data, neuropsychological tests, and neuroimaging. It employs iterative imputation for missing values, Borderline SVM-SMOTE for class imbalance, and feature selection techniques. A stacking ensemble model and an artificial neural network are applied, with performance evaluated using precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to improve early detection of Alzheimer's disease, addressing the challenge of late diagnoses which hinder timely management and interventions for patients.

Method: Data preprocessing involves iterative imputation, class imbalance adjustment using Borderline SVM-SMOTE, and feature selection. A stacking ensemble model combining Logistic Regression, Extra Trees, Bagging KNN, and LightGBM is developed, alongside an artificial neural network. Performance is assessed through multiple metrics.

Result: The models were compared using metrics like precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC to identify the best-performing algorithm for detecting Alzheimer's disease.

Conclusion: The paper concludes by identifying key biomarkers and the most effective machine learning classifier for aiding the early diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease.

Abstract: Alzheimers disease (AD) is a brain disorder that develops slowly and mainly affects memory, thinking, language, and daily activities. It is one of the most common causes of dementia and creates many difficulties for patients as well as their families. In the early stage, the symptoms are often mild and may look like normal ageing. For this reason, many people are diagnosed late, when the disease has already progressed. At present, there is no complete cure for AD. Still, early detection can help doctors manage the condition better and take suitable steps at the right time. In this study, a machine learning model is proposed to detect the early stages of Alzheimers disease using clinical details, neuropsychological test scores, and neuroimaging-related measures. The data used in this work is collected from the Alzheimers Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI). As the dataset has missing values, iterative imputation is applied to fill them. The dataset also has class imbalance, which is handled using Borderline SVM-SMOTE. After that, feature selection is carried out using wrapper-based and embedded methods so that only important features are used for training. The selected features are divided into training and testing sets, and feature scaling is applied. A stacking ensemble model is developed using Logistic Regression, Extra Trees, Bagging KNN, and LightGBM as base classifiers. Along with this, an artificial neural network is also trained on the same dataset. The performance of these models is compared using precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC. This study aims to find the best classifier and also identify important biomarkers that may help in the early diagnosis of Alzheimers disease.

</details>


### [297] [From Approximation to Emergence: A Theory of Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01311)
*Zhilin Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The monograph provides a unified, proof-oriented account of modern deep learning theory, linking classical concepts with contemporary mechanisms like overparameterization, transformers, and emergent behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to create coherence and depth in understanding various aspects of deep learning theory while filling gaps and organizing scattered literature.

Method: The authors explore deep learning concepts by examining objects they control, assumptions for validity, and phenomena left unexplained to generate a unified research narrative.

Result: The paper organizes diverse deep learning theories, connecting classical foundations with contemporary mechanisms and showing incomplete yet powerful knowledge pathways.

Conclusion: The work provides a rigorous map of deep learning theory emphasizing emergence, scale, data, architecture, and training, and highlights gaps in understanding.

Abstract: Deep learning has outgrown any single mathematical explanation. From Approximation to Emergence develops a unified, proof-oriented account of modern deep learning theory, tracing a path from the classical foundations of approximation, optimization, and generalization to the contemporary mechanisms of overparameterization, robustness, generative modeling, transformers, in-context learning, scaling laws, interpretability, alignment, and emergence. Rather than presenting isolated results, the book organizes a broad literature into a coherent research narrative: each theory is examined through the object it controls, the assumptions that make it valid, and the phenomena it leaves unexplained. Written for researchers, graduate students, and mathematically trained practitioners, this monograph offers a rigorous map of deep learning theory as it stands today: powerful, incomplete, and increasingly centered on the question of how learned mechanisms arise from scale, data, architecture, and training.

</details>


### [298] [Black-Box Inference of LLM Architectural Properties with Restrictive API Access](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01313)
*Christopher Ellis,Shreyas Chaudhari,Mei-Yu Wang,Leighton Barnes,Giulia Fanti,José M. F. Moura*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NightVision is a method to recover architectural details like hidden dimension, depth, and parameter count of an LLM using restrictive black-box API access.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Commercial LLM providers have reduced the information disclosed via APIs, aiming to safeguard architectural details. This creates a challenge for attackers wanting to extract model architecture with minimal access.

Method: NightVision employs common set prompting, spectral analysis, and TTFT measurements to infer architectural details.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 32 open-source LLMs reveals high accuracy in estimating hidden dimension (average relative error of 23%), depth, and parameter count.

Conclusion: Current API restrictions do not fully obfuscate underlying model architectures, indicating potential vulnerabilities in commercial LLM systems.

Abstract: In practice, most commercial LLM providers do not publicly release details of underlying LLM architectures. However, prior work has shown that given limited API access to an LLM (namely, top-$k$ logits and/or a logit bias function), one can recover certain architectural details of an LLM, such as the hidden dimension of the feed-forward network. Perhaps in response to these results, most commercial LLM providers have restricted their APIs to expose only the single logit for each decoded token, and they no longer give users the ability to bias logits. We show that even under current restrictive APIs, several architectural parameters are still recoverable. We present NightVision, an attack that uses restrictive black-box API access to estimate the hidden dimension, depth, and parameter count of an LLM. Algorithmically, NightVision relies on a novel common set prompting technique in which multiple prompts expose log probabilities for the same set of output tokens; a spectral analysis of these results is used to infer hidden dimension. NightVision additionally uses end-to-end time to first token (TTFT) measurements and the estimated hidden dimension to estimate depth and parameter count. We empirically evaluate NightVision on 32 open-source LLMs, recovering hidden dimension to within 23% average relative error across all models (9% on MoE models), and depth and parameter count to within 53% for models exceeding three billion parameters. We run extensive ablations to demonstrate how these accuracies scale with token budget and model properties. Overall, our results suggest that current LLM APIs are not sufficiently restricted to fully obfuscate the architectural details of their underlying models.

</details>


### [299] [Conditional Inference Trees and Forests for Feature Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01417)
*Robert Milletich,Justin Downes,Steve Goley,Newel Hirst*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The authors evaluate Conditional Inference Trees (CIT) and Forests (CIF) for top-k feature ranking, finding them computationally expensive but effective for downstream prediction tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to address the computational inefficiencies of CIT and CIF while exploring their effectiveness as feature-ranking methods for various prediction benchmarks.

Method: The study employs real-data benchmarks, runtime ablation studies, and synthetic experiments to evaluate CIT and CIF. Key focus is placed on runtime impacts of adaptive stopping and exact threshold searches, as well as the effect of forest feature sampling.

Result: CIF is ranked 4th among 17 classification methods and 3rd among 18 regression methods. Runtime was most affected by turning off adaptive stopping and using exact threshold search, significantly increasing computation time but with minimal downstream score changes.

Conclusion: CIF methods demonstrate strong performance as top-k feature-ranking methods in downstream tasks, though computational efficiency can be a challenge. Adaptive stopping and selected thresholds significantly influence runtime.

Abstract: Conditional inference trees (CIT) and conditional inference forests (CIF) reduce split-selection bias by testing features before choosing split thresholds, but repeated permutation tests and threshold searches can make these methods computationally expensive. We study CIT and CIF as top-$k$ feature-ranking methods for downstream prediction using real-data benchmarks, runtime ablations, and synthetic feature-recovery experiments. At a fixed node, if the features and permutation budget do not depend on the node responses, Bonferroni-corrected $+1$ Monte Carlo permutation $p$-values control nodewise rejection under the complete permutation null. CIF ranks 4th among 17 classification methods on 22 datasets and 3rd among 18 regression methods on 8 datasets. With Bonferroni correction held fixed, the CIF runtime ablations indicate that adaptive stopping and the number of thresholds searched have the largest measured effect on runtime: turning off adaptive stopping and using exact threshold search increase fitting time by 4.0--8.4$\times$ and 1.9--10.8$\times$, respectively, while downstream score changes are at most 0.011. Sparse high-$p$ simulations indicate that forest feature sampling can leave informative features out of many split decisions. Overall, the results support CIF as a top-$k$ feature-ranking method in the evaluated downstream prediction benchmarks.

</details>


### [300] [Multi-modal Rail Crossing Safety Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01365)
*Paimon Goulart,Chansong Lim,Nícolas Roque dos Santos,Yue Dong,Sheldon Peterson,Jia Chen,Evangelos E. Papalexakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a system to assess railway crossing safety using images and structured data, achieving promising results with a prototype pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to develop a robust AI system to evaluate railway crossing safety, integrating visual and structured data, and aligning with expert and Federal Railroad Administration (FRA) scoring systems.

Method: They introduce a proof-of-concept pipeline that combines multi-modal data (images and structured accident data), explores critical research challenges, and uses a fine-tuned visual language model (VLM) pipeline for classification and safety score estimation.

Result: The system identifies high-risk and low-risk crossings with a macro F1 score of 0.757, estimates FRA-based safety scores with an RMSE of 0.078 and a correlation of 0.492, and produces qualitative results consistent with expert opinions.

Conclusion: The study shows that integrating multi-modal data is a feasible and promising approach for railway crossing safety assessment, with performance metrics demonstrating its alignment with expert evaluations.

Abstract: Given one or more images of a railway crossing, can we leverage visual cues that allow us to robustly estimate how safe it is? Can we improve our ability to do so by introducing structured data (such as official accident reports) about the accident history of that crossing into our models? In this work, we explore how to best answer those questions towards building an AI system that can ingest multi-modal data for railway crossings and provide safety assessment and scores that align with expert opinion and with safety scoring used by the Federal Railroad Administration (FRA). To that end, we propose a proof-of-concept pipeline that delivers on that goal, while at the same time exploring and tackling a number of critical research challenges that pertain to different parts of the pipeline, from data preparation to different learning paradigms that can allow us to realize such a system. Indicatively, our proposed system identifies HIGH-RISK and LOW-RISK crossings with a macro F1 score of 0.757 and estimates FRA-based safety scores with an RMSE of 0.078 and correlation of 0.492 using a routed fine-tuned compact VLM pipeline, while producing qualitative results that align with domain-expert assessment.

</details>


### [301] [How to Allocate Your Tokens? Scaling Laws with Training Steps and Batch Size](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01487)
*Fabian Schaipp*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a scaling law involving model size, training steps, and batch size, to better predict optimal batch size and its scaling, requiring fewer training runs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve the understanding of scaling relationships between model size, training steps, and batch size, and to provide a method that works effectively even with suboptimal batch sizes.

Method: The authors propose a new scaling law (three-term law) evaluating it on training runs and deriving scaling laws for suboptimal batch sizes.

Result: The proposed three-term law successfully recovers optimal batch size scaling, matches empirical findings, and performs robustly with fewer training iterations.

Conclusion: The three-term scaling law is effective in modeling and predicting scaling relationships with reduced computational requirements, leveraging suboptimal training setups.

Abstract: We propose a scaling law that takes into account model size and training data while explicitly splitting the latter into training steps and batch size (called three-term law). Fitting the proposed law on a large set of training runs, we find that it correctly recovers the scaling of the optimal batch size. Moreover, because it makes use of training runs with suboptimal batch size, our proposed law can be robustly fit with a significantly smaller amount of training runs. We further show that the three-term law can be used to derive scaling laws for suboptimal batch sizes, and that it matches previous empirical findings related to the critical batch size.

</details>


### [302] [How Should Transformers Encode Numeric Values in Electronic Health Records?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01391)
*Maria Elkjær Montgomery,Christian Igel,Mikkel Odgaard,Martin Sillesen,Mads Nielsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates how to encode numeric values in transformer models for processing medical records, comparing different encoding strategies, and suggests hybrid token-based approaches as an effective alternative.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of encoding numeric values in transformer models, particularly for tasks involving electronic health record (EHR) data.

Method: The study compares discrete, continuous, and hybrid numeric value encoding strategies using both synthetic arithmetic and real-world clinical prediction tasks.

Result: The research identifies trade-offs between precision, optimization stability, and architectural flexibility, finding hybrid token-based approaches robust and practical, while achieving good numeric computation for various tasks.

Conclusion: Hybrid token-based approaches are recommended as a practical default for encoding numeric values due to their robustness and deployability, often outweighing the need for exact numeric precision.

Abstract: How do we encode numeric values in transformer-based sequence processing, particularly in electronic health record (EHR) data? We systematically compare discrete, continuous, and hybrid value encoding strategies using synthetic arithmetic tasks embedded within real-world EHR data, as well as real-world clinical prediction tasks. Our study reveals trade-offs between numeric precision, optimisation stability, and architectural flexibility. We find that approaches that explicitly model value-concept interactions perform best on precision-sensitive arithmetic tasks when architectural constraints permit. Hybrid token-based approaches that retain numeric values but apply binning prior to projection provide a more robust and broadly applicable alternative, with the optimal number of bins following a simple empirically derived power-law in dataset size. Across tasks, models consistently exhibit reliable "good enough" numeric computation rather than exact arithmetic, while clinical gains from incorporating laboratory values are task-dependent. This suggests that robustness and deployability often outweigh maximal numeric precision in practice, motivating hybrid token-based approaches as a practical default.

</details>


### [303] [Unveiling the Non-Monotonic Effect of Privacy on Generalization under Byzantine Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01492)
*Thomas Boudou,Batiste Le Bars,Nirupam Gupta,Aurélien Bellet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates the interplay between Byzantine robustness, local differential privacy (LDP), and generalization error in distributed learning, revealing a nuanced dependence on privacy noise levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore the impact of local differential privacy and Byzantine robustness on generalization error, challenging the established notion of a universal trilemma.

Method: The authors theoretically derive bounds on algorithmic stability under Byzantine-robust and LDP-constrained settings, complemented by empirical evaluations to validate their findings.

Result: In high-noise (strong privacy) regimes, privacy reduces generalization error, but in low-noise (weaker privacy) settings, the tension re-emerges, and privacy increases generalization error.

Conclusion: Generalization error behavior under Byzantine-robust distributed learning and LDP constraints is non-linear, depending on privacy levels, overturning previously held notions.

Abstract: Recent work has established a fundamental trilemma between Byzantine robustness, local differential privacy (LDP), and optimization error in distributed learning. We show that this trilemma does not universally extend to generalization error, but instead depends critically on the privacy regime. Specifically, in the high-noise regime (strong privacy), we prove that increasing privacy reduces the generalization error, i.e., there is no tension between robustness and privacy. In the low-noise regime (weaker privacy), however, the tension between robustness and privacy reappears and increasing privacy indeed degrades generalization. Our theory explains this surprising non-monotonic behavior of the generalization error via matching lower and upper bounds on the algorithmic stability of Byzantine-robust distributed learning under LDP constraints. We corroborate and further analyze these theoretical findings with empirical evaluations.

</details>


### [304] [NeuroBridge: Bridging Multi-Task MRI Knowledge for Neurodegenerative Disease Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01401)
*Mengyu Li,Guoyao Shen,Chad W. Farris,Xin Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'NeuroBridge,' a novel MRI-based framework for diagnosing Alzheimer's and related dementias, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and strong generalization across cohorts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the difficulty of diagnosing Alzheimer's and related dementias due to subtle and diverse brain structure changes, aiming to improve diagnostic accuracy using MRI data.

Method: The authors designed 'NeuroBridge,' which combines large-scale self-supervised MRI pretraining with tasks such as hippocampal segmentation, atrophy classification, and reconstruction, fine-tuned using a gated fusion mechanism.

Result: NeuroBridge achieved 88.17% and 82.78% accuracy in distinguishing AD from cognitively normal controls in ADNI and OASIS cohorts, respectively, and also excelled in MCI-related and mixed-diagnosis settings.

Conclusion: Multi-task learning frameworks like NeuroBridge outperform traditional single-task approaches for neurodegenerative disease diagnosis, with potential applications in large-scale dementia assessments and MRI-based screening.

Abstract: INTRODUCTION: Accurate MRI-based identification of Alzheimer's disease (AD), mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and related dementias remains challenging because disease-related structural changes are often subtle and heterogeneous. We developed NeuroBridge, a clinically guided multi-task MRI framework for neurodegenerative disease diagnosis. METHODS: NeuroBridge integrates large-scale self-supervised MRI pretraining with hippocampal segmentation, hippocampal atrophy classification, and reconstruction objectives, followed by gated fusion fine-tuning. Performance was evaluated across ADNI and OASIS cohorts, including cross-cohort transfer, probability-based analysis, and opportunistic screening. RESULTS: NeuroBridge achieved the highest performance across evaluated classification tasks, reaching 88.17% accuracy for AD versus cognitively normal controls in ADNI and 82.78% in OASIS. The largest gains occurred in MCI-related and mixed-diagnosis settings. The framework demonstrated strong cross-cohort generalization, systematic associations between predicted-class probability and accuracy, and the feasibility of probability-based opportunistic screening. DISCUSSION: Clinically guided multi-task representation learning improves neurodegenerative MRI diagnosis beyond conventional single-task approaches. NeuroBridge provides a robust and scalable framework for dementia assessment and MRI-based opportunistic screening.

</details>


### [305] [Spin-Weighted Spherical Harmonics Enable Complete and Scalable $\mathrm{E}(3)$-Equivariant Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01408)
*Chenxing Liang,Yuchao Lin,Andrii Kryvenko,Wendi Yu,Chuan Li,Jianwen Xie,Xiaofeng Qian,Shuiwang Ji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SpinGTP introduces an efficient solution to address the expressivity limitations of Gaunt Tensor Product (GTP) in 3D atomistic modeling while retaining computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to overcome the scalability issues of E(3)-equivariant networks caused by the high complexity (O(L^6)) of Clebsch-Gordan Tensor Products and the incomplete expressivity of Gaunt Tensor Products.

Method: SpinGTP generalizes from scalar functions to Spin-Weighted Spherical Harmonics (SWSH), enabling it to capture previously missing antisymmetric interactions while maintaining GTP’s computational efficiency.

Result: Evaluation of SpinGTP across diverse benchmarks demonstrates accuracies comparable to Clebsch-Gordan Tensor Products, with notable improvements in handling chiral materials and non-centrosymmetric geometries.

Conclusion: SpinGTP provides a scalable and mathematically complete framework for achieving high-order equivariance in large-scale 3D atomistic simulations, particularly excelling in scenarios requiring antisymmetric path representations.

Abstract: $\mathrm{E}(3)$-equivariant networks are promising for 3D atomistic system modeling, yet their scalability is limited by the $O(L^6)$ complexity of the Clebsch-Gordan Tensor Product (CGTP). The recently proposed Gaunt Tensor Product (GTP) reduces the complexity but is unable to capture the antisymmetric paths, resulting in incomplete expressivity. In this work, we present SpinGTP, an approach to overcome the GTP incompleteness by generalizing from scalar functions to Spin-Weighted Spherical Harmonics (SWSH). By relying on the algebraic properties of SWSH, SpinGTP recovers the missing antisymmetric interactions while maintaining the asymptotic efficiency of GTP. It also allows for a more expressive equivariant basis that naturally accounts for the parity-odd components of tensor products. We evaluate SpinGTP across diverse benchmarks, including Tetris, 3BPA, SPICE-MACE-OFF, and OC20. Our results show that SpinGTP achieves accuracies comparable to full CGTP. Notably, by explicitly capturing antisymmetric paths, SpinGTP exhibits superior performance in tasks involving chiral materials and non-centrosymmetric geometries. This work provides a complete, scalable, and mathematically rigorous path toward high-order equivariance in large-scale 3D atomistic system simulations.

</details>


### [306] [The Rollout Infrastructure Tax in Coding-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01415)
*Daniel Thi Graviet,Lovre Pesut,Ivan Dagelic,Vedran Jukic,Ivan Burazin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The study highlights the significant impact of execution infrastructure on reinforcement learning (RL) performance, showing variations up to 110x in latency and suggesting integrating infrastructure optimization into RL systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the issue of overlooked infrastructure overhead in coding-agent reinforcement learning systems, proposing that measuring and optimizing these overheads can lead to compounded efficiency gains at scale.

Method: The authors conducted a comparative evaluation across four distinct execution infrastructures: single containers, hosted sandboxes, Kubernetes-orchestrated containers, and cloud virtual machines, measuring metrics like cold-start latency and worker-hour projections for large-scale RL rollouts.

Result: The study found significant variability among infrastructures, including a 110x difference in cold-start latency and a 1.8x range in worker-hour requirements for processing one million 150-step trajectories.

Conclusion: The findings emphasize the need to integrate execution substrate optimization as a core component of RL system design, rather than relegating it to a background or peripheral consideration.

Abstract: Coding-agent reinforcement learning treats execution infrastructure as a background implementation detail, despite relying on large numbers of interactive software rollouts. This is a missed opportunity: measuring infrastructure overhead can reveal practical efficiency gains for RL post-training, where small per-rollout savings compound at scale. We present a comparative study of four execution substrates: single containers, hosted sandboxes, Kubernetes-orchestrated containers, and cloud virtual machines. We find up to $110\times$ variation in cold-start latency and a $1.8\times$ spread in projected worker-hours for one million 150-step trajectories. Our results suggest that future coding-agent RL systems should optimize execution substrates as part of the training system itself, not merely as deployment plumbing.

</details>


### [307] [Role-Aware Neural Convex Divergence Heads for Asymmetric Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01762)
*He Huang,Lu Shen,Yunfeng Huang,Li Qi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a role-aware neural convex divergence head designed for asymmetric representation learning applications, outperforming symmetric and some unstructured asymmetric scoring methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard approaches for learning directed relations in representation problems lack geometric structure or symmetry modeling capabilities, necessitating more sophisticated methods.

Method: The authors propose a head that uses role-specific projections followed by an input-convex neural Bregman divergence, creating a structured, nonnegative scoring mechanism.

Result: Experiments demonstrate improved directional accuracy on semantic and ontology benchmarks with the new head, although symmetric or hyperbolic baselines perform better in specific citation prediction scenarios.

Conclusion: The role-aware neural convex divergence head serves as a structured and interpretable distance module, offering advantages in tasks dealing with directional relations.

Abstract: Many representation learning problems involve directed relations, such as lexical entailment, sentence entailment, ontology hierarchy, and citation links. Standard Euclidean, cosine, and Mahalanobis heads are symmetric, while generic neural scorers can model directionality but provide limited geometric structure. This paper proposes a role-aware neural convex divergence head for asymmetric representation learning. The head applies source- and target-role projections before evaluating an input-convex neural Bregman divergence, yielding a nonnegative structured score in the role-projected space. We characterize its projected-space identity, source-role convexity, directional-gap decomposition, and Hessian-based local curvature. Experiments on lexical, sentence, ontology, and directed graph benchmarks compare symmetric distances, unstructured asymmetric scorers, order/hyperbolic baselines, plain ICNN-Bregman heads, and the proposed role-aware variant. Across ten random seeds on the main semantic and ontology benchmarks, role-aware projections consistently improve directional accuracy over plain ICNN-Bregman heads while preserving zero observed negative divergence rate. The results also identify a boundary case: on large fixed-feature citation prediction, specialized symmetric or hyperbolic baselines remain stronger in ranking accuracy. Overall, the proposed head is best understood as a structured and interpretable plug-in distance module for tasks where directional relations matter.

</details>


### [308] [Regularized Variational and Spectral Log-Density-Ratio Estimation in the Gaussian Location Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01895)
*Francis Bach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analyzes high-dimensional Gaussian location models using two ridge-regularized log-density-ratio estimation methods: a variational estimator and a spectral estimator. Asymptotic behavior and estimator risks are compared.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand the performance differences between variational and spectral estimators for ridge-regularized log-density-ratio estimation in high-dimensional Gaussian settings.

Method: Derives deterministic asymptotic equivalents of estimators, uses scalar entropy minimization (via CGMT) and weighted covariance matrix resolvents, performs population risk comparisons and numerical experiments.

Result: Concludes that the variational estimator performs better with many observations, while the spectral estimator has advantages with fewer observations due to lower variance. Also investigates nuclear penalties for feature learning.

Conclusion: The choice between variational and spectral estimators depends on the observation regime, with regularization and feature learning playing key roles in model performance.

Abstract: We study ridge-regularized log-density-ratio estimation in the Gaussian location model with a common covariance matrix. By affine invariance, the model is written as q $\sim$ N(0, I), p $\sim$ N($Δ$, I), with linear features, where $Δ$ is a mean vector. The variational estimator is the empirical Kullback-Leibler (KL) log-normalized fit with a squared L2-penalty on its nonconstant coefficient, and the spectral estimator recently introduced in [1] replaces a single variational problem by a continuum of ridge-regularized least-squares problems. We derive high-dimensional deterministic asymptotic equivalents when the numbers of observations and dimension tend to infinity with fixed ratios. The regularized variational limit is characterized by a scalar entropy minimization problem derived from the convex-Gaussian-min-max theorem (CGMT), while the regularized spectral limit follows from deterministic equivalents for resolvents of weighted sums of two independent Gaussian sample covariance matrices. We use these formulas to compare population risks, with experiments focused on fixed-signal aspect-ratio sweeps and optimized regularization. Our conclusion is that with many observations, under the criteria and asymptotic regimes analyzed here, the well-specified variational estimator has the smaller risk, while with fewer observations, the spectral estimator is favored because its covariance-based construction has lower variance. We also study how a nuclear penalty can be used and partially analyzed to perform feature learning.

</details>


### [309] [On the Utility and Factual Reliability of Pruned Mixture-of-Experts Models in the Biomedical Domain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01444)
*Atsuki Yamaguchi,Szymon Palucha,Léo Bijar,Aline Villavicencio,Nikolaos Aletras*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper explores the effects of domain-specific expert pruning on the utility and reliability of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, particularly in biomedicine. Moderate pruning maintains utility but extreme pruning risks hallucinations, while tasks in general domains experience significant degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address resource constraints in deploying Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, while ensuring both utility and factual reliability, especially in high-stakes domains like biomedicine.

Method: The study evaluates four MoE models using six pruning methods and various pruning ratios across biomedical and general domain tasks. Both generation and classification tasks were assessed for utility and reliability.

Result: Moderate pruning retains utility in biomedical tasks without immediate reliability decline, while extreme pruning increases hallucination risks. General domain tasks exhibit rapid utility and reliability loss under pruning.

Conclusion: Reliable deployment of pruned MoE models requires assessing both utility and reliability, as task and domain significantly impact safe compression outcomes.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models offer inference speedups via selective activation but impose substantial memory requirements because the whole network must remain loaded. Structured expert pruning is a practical approach for reducing deployment costs in resource-constrained settings. However, prior studies primarily evaluate benchmark utility, leaving the effect of pruning on factual reliability underexplored, particularly in high-stakes domains such as biomedicine. In this paper, we investigate how domain-specific expert pruning affects both utility and reliability. We assess four MoE models, six pruning methods, and multiple pruning ratios across generation and classification tasks under in-domain (biomedical) and cross-domain settings. Results reveal that moderate pruning preserves in-domain utility without immediate reliability decline, although hallucination risks increase at extreme pruning ratios. When shifting to the general domain, both utility and reliability degrade rapidly. These findings indicate that safe compression depends heavily on the task and domain. Evaluating pruned MoE models solely on utility is inadequate for high-stakes deployment without reliability assessment.

</details>


### [310] [Geometry-Aware R-Structured Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01449)
*Sergei Kucherenko,Nilay Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Geometry-aware R-Structured Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (GRS-KAN), a hybrid neural architecture that enhances prediction accuracy and interpretability by integrating explicit geometric constraints into a trainable framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in neural networks regarding smooth nonlinear structure learning and the representation of geometric or logical constraints, thereby improving both accuracy and interpretability in regression problems.

Method: A novel architecture combining Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) for smooth modeling and R-functions for encoding geometrical and logical constraints is proposed. It includes variants such as additive, multiplicative, and agnostic branch-weighted architectures.

Result: The approach significantly improves predictive accuracy (by up to 67% in test RMSE) and boundary localization in regression tasks with discontinuities while increasing model interpretability.

Conclusion: GRS-KAN successfully integrates analytical geometric constraints into neural networks, substantially enhancing both performance and explainability, with flexibility to determine the utility of geometric priors for specific tasks.

Abstract: We propose a novel hybrid neural architecture, the Geometry-aware R-Structured Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (GRS-KAN), which integrates V.L.Rvachev's R-functions into the Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) framework. The proposed approach combines two complementary modeling mechanisms: smooth nonlinear structure is learned by KAN branches, while known geometric or logical constraints are encoded analytically using differentiable R-functions. This enables explicit representation of discontinuities, feasible regions, and implicit geometric boundaries within a trainable neural architecture.
  The framework implements differentiable logical operations through R-conjunctions and R-disjunctions, allowing complex geometric supports to be represented analytically and incorporated directly into regression models. Several GRS-KAN variants are introduced, including additive, multiplicative, and agnostic branch-weighted architectures.
  The method is demonstrated on regression problems involving discontinuities with circular and rectangular supports. Numerical experiments show that explicit geometric encoding substantially improves predictive accuracy and boundary localization compared with standard KANs. In the considered benchmarks, geometry-aware GRS-KAN models reduce test RMSE by up to 67% while simultaneously improving interpretability through explicit analytical representation of the learned geometric structure. The agnostic variant further demonstrates the ability to automatically determine whether geometric priors are beneficial for a given learning task.

</details>


### [311] [Token Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01455)
*Kathan Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Ember, a lightweight optimizer for embedding and LM-head matrices, improving efficiency and scalability while reducing VRAM usage and optimizer state.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiencies in optimization processes for embedding tables and LM-head matrices, particularly reducing VRAM and eliminating the need to shard optimizer states.

Method: Ember is a lightweight optimizer that requires only O(V + D) VRAM for embedding and LM-head matrices, diverging from Adam’s O(2VD) requirement. It also characterizes token optimization though a 1D ray trajectory.

Result: Ember improves the Pareto frontier across supervised finetuning, RL, and pretraining. It is scalable with both batch size and parameter count and offers an efficient optimization mechanism.

Conclusion: Ember provides an efficient and scalable solution for optimizing embedding tables and LM-head matrices, significantly enhancing Transformer training processes, and is made open-source for further research.

Abstract: Language models learn continuous programs over discrete symbols, with the embedding table and LM-head acting as the read/write interface between them. We show that this interface has gradient geometry distinct from dense hidden weights which can be exploited to improve the Pareto frontier across supervised finetuning, RL, and pretraining, while only utilizing kilobytes of optimizer state. We introduce Ember, a lightweight optimizer for embedding and LM-head matrices that utilizes O(V + D) VRAM, instead of Adam's O(2VD), and forgoes the need to shard both token table optimizer states. We provide empirical evidence that Ember scales effectively across batch size and parameter count. We show that the optimization trajectory of tokens can be well described by a simple 1D ray, counter to the popular belief that neural net parameters navigate a heavily nonconvex landscape. We provide a principled view on the surprisingly narrow space of optimizers that suffice for Transformer training. Finally, we open-source our distributed Ember implementation that merges cleanly with existing ZeRO/FSDP setups to support further research at https://github.com/katop1234/ember

</details>


### [312] [DeadPool: Resilient LLM Training with Hot-Swapping via Zero-Overhead Checkpoint](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01646)
*Haotian Xie,Junlin Chen,Mingkai Zheng,Lishan Yang,Zhao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DeadPool introduces a fault-tolerance mechanism for LLM training that enables zero overhead during normal operation and minimizes recovery latency by hot-swapping failed nodes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address issues in the fault-tolerance mechanisms for large language model (LLM) training, which are either costly during failure-free operations or result in long recovery times, especially for permanent node failures.

Method: DeadPool utilizes hot-swapping via two innovations: an off-critical-path in-memory checkpointing mechanism and a communicator reconstruction protocol, both enabling fast replacement of failed nodes.

Result: DeadPool achieves zero checkpoint overhead and recovery completion in under 40 seconds during evaluations involving up to 512 GPUs and LLMs containing up to 65 billion parameters.

Conclusion: DeadPool effectively balances fault-tolerance objectives by ensuring zero overhead during error-free execution and quick recovery from failures, making it highly practical for large-scale LLM training.

Abstract: State-of-the-art large language model (LLM) training takes tens of thousands of graphics processing units (GPUs) for months and encounters failures across the software and hardware stack. Existing fault-tolerance mechanisms either impose non-trivial overhead during failure-free execution or suffer from prolonged recovery latency, particularly under scenarios where a small subset of compute nodes experience permanent failures. %The tradeoff between failure-free overhead and recovery latency forms a space forms a Pareto frontier We present DeadPool to simultaneously address both optimization objectives. DeadPool incorporates a fault-tolerance mechanism that restores LLM training via hot-swapping, namely by replacing failed nodes with spare nodes without terminating the complete job. The hot-swapping of DeadPool is enabled by two ideas: First, it exploits an off-critical-path in-memory checkpointing mechanism for spatial redundancy. Second, it introduces a communicator reconstruction protocol that replaces failed nodes with spare nodes at runtime. DeadPool efficiently overlaps the in-memory checkpointing with computation, thus introducing zero overhead during error-free execution. Upon permanent node failures, DeadPool can rebuild memory states with minimal recomputation by leveraging in-memory checkpoints. We evaluate DeadPool across scales (up to 512 NVIDIA A100 GPUs) and LLMs (up to 65B parameters), and observe zero checkpoint overhead with hot-swapping recovery completing in under 40 seconds. These results show that DeadPool simultaneously achieves both zero-overhead error-free execution and extremely low recovery cost.

</details>


### [313] [Class-Grouped Normalized Momentum and Faster Hyperparameter Exploration to Tackle Class Imbalance in Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01474)
*Haemin Park,Diego Klabjan,Martin W. Braun,Xiuqi Li,Balakrishnan Ananthanarayanan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses the challenge of class imbalance in federated learning by proposing FedCGNM, a client-side optimizer that balances gradients across class groups, and FedHOO, a rate-optimizing algorithm leveraging federated parallelism. Both show superior empirical results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle class imbalance in federated learning, which results in poor predictive performance for underrepresented classes and cannot be addressed with centralized methods due to privacy and heterogeneity constraints.

Method: FedCGNM tackles class imbalance by grouping classes based on variance, normalizing group momentum to equalize gradients, and ensuring robust update directions. FedHOO optimizes resampling rates using an X-armed-bandit approach in small-client federations.

Result: The proposed methods outperform baselines across four public benchmarks and a proprietary chip-defect dataset, with FedCGNM and FedHOO demonstrating notable gains in handling class imbalance.

Conclusion: FedCGNM provides effective momentum normalization for better class balance in FL, while FedHOO optimizes resampling efficiently in small-client setups, showcasing superior results empirically.

Abstract: Class imbalance poses a critical challenge in federated learning (FL), where underrepresented classes suffer from poor predictive performance yet cannot be addressed by standard centralized techniques due to privacy and heterogeneity constraints. We propose FedCGNM (Federated Class-Grouped Normalized Momentum), a client-side optimizer in FL that partitions classes into a small number of groups based on minimum within-group variance, maintains a momentum per group, normalizes each group momentum to unit length, and uses the summation of the normalized group momentums as an update direction. This design both equalizes gradient magnitude across majority and minority groups and mitigates the noise inherent in rare-class gradients. We further provide a theoretical convergence analysis explicitly accounting for time-varying resampling-rates. Additionally, to efficiently optimize these rates in small-client regimes, we introduce FedHOO, an X-armed-bandit (XAB) based algorithm that exploits federated parallelism that evaluates many combinations of two candidate rates per client at linear cost. Empirical evaluation on four public long-tailed benchmarks and a proprietary chip-defect dataset demonstrates that FedCGNM consistently outperforms baselines, with FedHOO yielding further gains in small-scale federations.

</details>


### [314] [SCAPE: Accurate and Efficient LLM Training with Extreme Sparse Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01678)
*Mingkai Zheng,Junlin Chen,Haotian Xie,Zhao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCAPE is a new communication-efficient optimizer for large language model (LLM) training, leveraging sparsification techniques to significantly enhance training efficiency while maintaining model quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Increasing communication costs during LLM pre-training, driven by gradient synchronization and parameter reconstruction, pose challenges, especially with large-scale models and modern optimizers.

Method: SCAPE utilizes first-moment statistics instead of raw gradients for aggressive sparsification, aligns mask generation with optimizer sharding, delays mask usage for compute overlap, and reconstructs required quantities via a sparse synchronized buffer.

Result: Experiments on GPT-345M and Llama models show SCAPE achieves high sparsity (90%-99%) without degrading model convergence or accuracy, while reducing pre-training time by 43.3% and providing step speedups up to 3.26×.

Conclusion: SCAPE is an effective solution for reducing communication overhead in LLM pre-training, maintaining model stability and accuracy while drastically improving computational efficiency.

Abstract: Communication increasingly dominates the cost of Large Language Model (LLM) pre-training, especially under data-parallel and sharded training schemes, where gradient synchronization and parameter reconstruction overhead increase with model size and system scale. Existing communication-reduction methods either sparsify raw gradients, which can be unstable for modern Adam-style optimizers at high sparsity, or quantize communication, whose savings are fundamentally bounded by bit width and often incur additional runtime overhead. We present SCAPE, a communication-efficient distributed optimizer for LLM training that exploits the stability of AdamS's first-moment to enable aggressive sparsification without loss of LLM quality. Instead of constructing masks from raw gradients, SCAPE derives them from first-moment-based statistics, partitions mask generation across workers to align with optimizer sharding, and delays mask usage by one step so that mask synchronization can overlap with computation. SCAPE also reconstructs the quantities required for second-moment updates from a single synchronized sparse buffer, avoiding an additional collective. We implement SCAPE in Megatron-LM and evaluate its convergence by pre-training GPT-345M on OpenWebText and Llama-500M on SlimPajama-6B using 32 NVIDIA GH200 GPUs on TACC Vista. In both models, SCAPE preserves training stability, validation loss, and downstream task accuracy under 90\% and 99\% sparsity. For Llama-500M, SCAPE reduces end-to-end pre-training wall-clock time by up to 43.3\% while maintaining model quality comparable to dense AdamW and AdamS. For Llama-1.8B, SCAPE achieves up to 3.26$\times$ speedup per step compared to dense AdamS.

</details>


### [315] [Don't Let Gains FADE: Breaking Down Policy Gradient Weights in RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01490)
*Juliette Decugis,Sean O'Brien,Francis Bach,Gabriel Synnaeve,Taco Cohen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces FADE, a dynamic reinforcement learning method for improving reasoning in large language models, overcoming issues like instability and lack of diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning post-training enhances reasoning in large language models but faces instability and diversity issues. The research seeks a principled approach to address these challenges using advantage functions.

Method: The paper introduces FADE, a dynamically adjusting advantage function that adapts to training dynamics by balancing entropy and gradient weighting automatically, allowing better trade-offs during learning.

Result: FADE accelerates training convergence, achieving peak performance faster than static baselines at both 7B and 32B model scales and ensures improved accuracy and diversity in benchmarks like LiveCodeBench and AIME.

Conclusion: Dynamic advantage functions like FADE effectively resolve key challenges in reinforcement learning for LLMs, offering faster training and a balanced trade-off between accuracy and diversity.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning post-training dramatically improves LLM reasoning, but suffers from training instability and diversity collapse. Advantage functions offer an appealing fix: they reshape the training objective, reweight which rollouts drive learning, and are trivial to implement. Yet a proliferation of methods makes it unclear which advantage to use and when. We cut through the confusion with a unifying framework that decomposes any advantage into its positive and negative gradient mass along two orthogonal axes. On the sign axis, imbalanced updates collapse either entropy or weight geometry. On the difficulty axis, hard-problem focus sharpens signal but costs sample size. Both trade-offs shift during training: exploration favors balance and hard focus; exploitation favors suppression and medium focus. This motivates FADE (Focal Advantage with Dynamic Entropy), a self-adapting advantage that reads training dynamics to schedule the gradient weight automatically. FADE reaches peak pass@1 20k steps earlier than the best static baseline at the 7B scale and 2k steps earlier at the 32B , while achieving the best accuracy-diversity trade-off across all pass@k on LiveCodeBench and AIME.

</details>


### [316] [Towards Learning Representations of Policies in Two-Player Zero-Sum Imperfect-Information Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01498)
*Kevin Wang,Kevin Yang,Arjun Prakash,Amy Greenwald*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper focuses on learning useful policy representations in two-player zero-sum imperfect-information games through dataset creation, embedding techniques, and evaluation tasks, and applies them to Kuhn and Leduc Poker.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to explore how self-supervised learning techniques can be applied to generate meaningful policy embeddings in games, as this area has been underexplored.

Method: They introduce datasets of policies, propose learning methods for embeddings, and evaluate their approach on downstream tasks using Kuhn and Leduc Poker as testbeds.

Result: Their basic methods successfully extracted useful behavioral representations in the learned embeddings, demonstrating the potential of these techniques.

Conclusion: This study showcases a systematic comparison of self-supervised learning methods for learning policy representations, offering foundational insights and providing resources for future research.

Abstract: We investigate the problem of learning useful policy representations (embeddings) in two-player zero-sum imperfect-information games. We make three contributions: First, we introduce methods of creating datasets of policies for a given game. Second, we propose methods to learn policy representations. Third, we introduce downstream tasks to evaluate the effectiveness of such representations.
  We evaluate each dataset method, embedding method, and downstream task on Kuhn and Leduc Poker. Although our methods are very basic, we demonstrate that useful behavioral representations are present in the learned embeddings. To our knowledge, this work is among the first to systematically compare self-supervised learning techniques for learning policy representations in games. Our code is available at https://github.com/VitamintK/ssl-project for others to extend.

</details>


### [317] [The risk of KV cache compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01520)
*Lukas Haverbeck,Carmen Amo Alonso,Andres Felipe Posada-Moreno,Sebastian Trimpe,Marco Pavone*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a theoretical framework for key-value (KV) cache compression in transformer models, overcoming previous challenges, and introduces a practical algorithm with strong experimental results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inefficiency of transformer inference on long sequences due to costly KV cache operations, while providing theoretical guidance for effective cache compression.

Method: The authors develop a framework to characterize KV cache compressibility using minimax risk, derive design principles for compression, and implement these in a practical algorithm for causal masking in transformer tasks.

Result: The proposed algorithm performs well in targeted experiments on LongBench, demonstrating the relevance of the theoretical framework for designing practical KV compression techniques.

Conclusion: The study offers a systematic and theoretical approach to KV cache compression, enabling efficient transformer inference with provable guarantees, paving the way for practical applications.

Abstract: Transformer inference on long sequences is expensive because softmax attention repeatedly reads from a large KV cache. The prevalent approach to this bottleneck is KV cache compression, which replaces the full cache with a compact summary. Despite its practical importance, the design of such summaries is largely driven by empirical experimentation. On the theoretical side, existing results show that KV cache compression can be impossible in the worst case, but offer little systematic guidance for designing algorithms in regimes where accurate compression is possible. We bridge this gap by characterizing the minimax risk of KV cache compression in terms of the intrinsic compressibility of a cache, revealing when and how accurate compression is possible. These results yield novel design principles for KV cache compression under causal masking that map efficiently to prefill and autoregressive decoding while achieving minimax-optimal risk. We instantiate these principles in a practical algorithm and report promising performance on LongBench in targeted experiments. Overall, our results provide a principled avenue for practical KV cache compression with theoretical guarantees.

</details>


### [318] [Multi-Head Recurrent Memory Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01523)
*Jiatong Li,Samuel Yeh,Sharon Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new framework called Multi-Head Recurrent Memory (MHM) to improve memory reliability for long-context recurrent memory agents, addressing issues of memory retention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recurrent memory agents degrade in performance over long contexts due to challenges in memory retention, as current designs risk overwriting stored information.

Method: The authors introduce Multi-Head Recurrent Memory (MHM), a framework that partitions memory into independent heads with a select-then-update strategy, ensuring that only one head is updated and others remain protected from overwriting.

Result: A variant called MHM-LRU shows significant improvements in both retention and accuracy on benchmarks with long token contexts, demonstrating a memory retention rate increase from less than 30% to 73.96% on specific tasks.

Conclusion: MHM and its lightweight variant MHM-LRU effectively enhance retention and accuracy for long-context tasks and offer a scalable, training-free solution that generalizes across models and tasks.

Abstract: Recurrent memory agents extend LLMs to arbitrarily long contexts by iteratively consolidating input into a fixed-size memory window. Despite their scalability, these agents exhibit a well-documented reliability problem: end-to-end performance degrades systematically as context length grows. We diagnose this failure by decomposing performance into two factors--memory capture and memory retention--and quantitatively confirm that retention is the dominant bottleneck. Retention collapses because existing designs maintain memory as a monolithic text block, forcing every update to risk overwriting previously retained content. Motivated by this diagnosis, we propose Multi-Head Recurrent Memory (MHM), a general, training-free framework that partitions memory into independent heads governed by a stage-wise select-then-update strategy. At each step, exactly one head is selected for update while the remaining heads are structurally shielded from overwriting, shifting the burden of retention from model behavior to architectural design. As a lightweight instantiation, we introduce Least-Recently-Updated MHM (MHM-LRU), which guarantees uniform head utilization with zero additional token overhead. Extensive experiments on long-context benchmarks show that MHM-LRU substantially improves both retention and end-to-end accuracy across the 100K--1M token range, where baselines degrade sharply. On RULER-HQA at 896K tokens, MHM-LRU improves the memory retention rate from less than 30% to 73.96%. These gains generalize across model families, scales, and task types, positioning architectural optimization as a practical and cost-efficient path toward reliable long-context recurrent memory.

</details>


### [319] [Wind-Aware Reinforcement Learning Control of a Small Quadrotor Using Learned Onboard Wind Estimation in Simulated Atmospheric Turbulence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01528)
*Abdullah Al Tasim,Wei Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a two-stage learning method using wind estimation and reinforcement learning to improve flight control for multirotor aircraft in turbulent winds, reducing trajectory tracking errors significantly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of multirotor aircraft maintaining trajectory tracking in the atmospheric boundary layer, where turbulent winds severely degrade conventional feedback control performance.

Method: The method involves a two-stage learning pipeline: 1) a wind estimator using an attention-augmented gated recurrent network trained on simulated flight data to estimate wind parameters, and 2) a reinforcement learning flight controller (via proximal policy optimization) that uses these wind estimates to improve control.

Result: The wind estimator achieves high accuracy (RMSE of 0.40 m/s and direction error of 3.2 degrees) and generalizes well to vertical wind profiles. The reinforcement learning controller reduces tracking error by 48% compared to a baseline and consistently outperforms in evaluation episodes across different wind conditions.

Conclusion: This research demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of integrating wind estimation and reinforcement learning to improve flight control for small multirotor aircraft, especially under challenging wind conditions, with graceful degradation in extreme scenarios.

Abstract: Small multirotor aircraft are increasingly tasked with operations in the atmospheric boundary layer, where turbulent winds comparable to the vehicle's airspeed degrade trajectory tracking and can defeat conventional feedback control. This work illustrates a two-stage learning pipeline that first estimates the local wind from onboard kinematics and dynamics and then exploits that estimate inside a reinforcement learning (RL) flight controller. The wind estimator, an attention-augmented gated recurrent network trained on thousands of simulated flights through von Karman turbulence with power-law shear and veer, recovers the horizontal wind vector with a per-flight root-mean-square error of 0.40 m/s and a direction error of 3.2 degrees on unseen wind regimes, an accuracy near the floor imposed by unresolved turbulence, and generalizes to vertical ascent profiles with a skill score of 0.861 over a constant-wind reference. A proximal policy optimization controller receiving the frozen estimator's output reduces horizontal trajectory tracking error by 48% relative to a wind-blind proportional-derivative baseline across mean winds of 4 m/s to 12 m/s, winning on 100% of evaluation episodes. A three-way ablation decomposes this improvement into a kinematic component, available without wind information, and a wind-perception component; the perception share rises with wind speed, from small in light winds toward roughly half the total benefit in strong winds, consistent with the quadratic scaling of aerodynamic drag. The controller degrades gracefully on out-of-distribution winds of 13 m/s to 15 m/s, where the baseline fails catastrophically.

</details>


### [320] [Certified World Models as Sensing Clocks: Drift-Aware Deadlines for Active Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01537)
*Hongbo Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a sensing clock based on the validity horizon of certified world models to dictate when agents should re-sense, improving model deployment reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address uncertainty in how long a world model's predictions remain valid during deployment, and to operationalize this knowledge into a more reliable sensing strategy for agents.

Method: The authors derive a deadline for no-sensing intervals using audited equivariant world models and incorporate drift-aware techniques to ensure that learned models maintain valid sensing schedules during deployment.

Result: The proposed clock method effectively minimizes certificate violations both in tested benchmarks and deployment settings, substantially reducing sensing-related errors.

Conclusion: The study presents a reliable sensing-clock mechanism and a drift-aware methodology for deployment, advancing how learning models manage sensing intervals. However, its contribution does not claim absolute superiority over all non-spectral scheduling mechanisms.

Abstract: Certified world models estimate how long their predictions remain valid. We turn this validity horizon into an operational sensing clock: a rule for when an agent should stop coasting and re-sense. Starting from an audited equivariant world model, we derive a deadline for no-sensing intervals and show that deployable deadlines in learned world models must be drift-aware: on-manifold Lyapunov rates alone overestimate coasting validity, while calibrated native rollout-drift envelopes carry the deployed guarantee. On a frozen 3D VN-JEPA model, the resulting clock controls held-out interval-simultaneous certificate violation across seeds and data shards. In a cue-conditioned theorem-bed (a synthetic bench where all schedulers share the exact model, isolating the scheduling rule), the clock remains valid on the deployment distribution and substantially reduces eventful-tail violations relative to exact-mixture expected-belief scheduling at matched sensing budget. We also report limits: in the short-horizon frozen VN-JEPA regime, empirical conformal horizons match the deployed clock on validity and budget, and a partial-reset exploration finds no clean budget-matched advantage for the spectral term. Thus the contribution is a certified sensing-clock primitive and drift-aware deployment method, not a claim that spectral clocks empirically dominate all non-spectral schedulers.

</details>


### [321] [Evolutionary Feature Engineering for Structured Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01548)
*Ege Onur Taga,Yilin Zhuang,M. Emrullah Ildiz,Petros Mol,Abhimanyu Das,Karthik Duraisamy,Samet Oymak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Evolutionary Feature Engineering (EFE), which uses large language models (LLMs) to evolve preprocessing programs for structured data and improve machine learning outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of automatically designing preprocessing transformations for structured data, enhancing performance and interpretability in machine learning workflows.

Method: EFE uses Python-based programs with fit/transform functionality, leveraging dataset-specific statistics and validation feedback for evolutionary refinement. It is applied to time-series forecasting (EFE-Time) and tabular prediction (EFE-Tab).

Result: EFE-Time improves time-series forecasting, reducing errors by up to 19% on specific datasets, while EFE-Tab enhances tabular prediction, particularly benefiting decision trees with competitive accuracy and maintained interpretability.

Conclusion: EFE demonstrates that LLM-driven evolutionary optimization can significantly enhance structured data processing, improving both predictive accuracy and the interpretability of machine learning workflows.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used as open-ended search operators in evolutionary optimization. We introduce Evolutionary Feature Engineering (EFE), a framework for using LLM-based evolution to discover preprocessing transformations for structured data. EFE represents transformations as Python programs with a standardized fit/transform interface, allowing them to be inserted directly into existing machine learning pipelines. During evolution, candidate programs are refined using dataset context, summary statistics, and downstream performance feedback on validation set. We instantiate EFE in two settings. For time-series forecasting, EFE-Time learns invertible, dataset-specific normalizations that improve off-the-shelf time-series foundation models. It reduces forecasting errors (MASE, WQL, MAE) 3% or more when averaged across datasets and improvements are as much as 19% on the COVID-Deaths dataset. Notably, these improvements occur with recent TSFMs such as Chronos-2. For tabular prediction, EFE-Tab evolves compact feature programs that add useful interpretable features and remove redundant ones, improving or matching existing LLM-based feature-engineering methods.
