Synthetic experiments show that weighted tilted conformal Bayes restores marginal coverage with smaller sets than weighted source-score calibration, while revealing a trade-off between marginal coverage and component-wise behavior across atoms and interior observations.

</details>


### [524] [Cross-Audit Projection for Model Risk Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02328)
*Yijian Huang*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper examines an issue with $K$-fold cross-validation (CV) in binary classification and proposes a novel method, Cross-Audit Projection (CAP), to improve risk prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the observation that $K$-fold CV, while widely trusted for mitigating over-optimism in empirical risk, performs poorly when estimating class-specific risks and may even be outperformed by the empirical estimator.

Method: The authors use higher-order asymptotic analysis to investigate CV's limitations and develop a two-step procedure called CAP. CAP combines cross-auditing to estimate over-optimism with projection steps informed by asymptotic theory to adjust for bias in empirical risk.

Result: CAP achieves first-order asymptotic equivalence to empirical risk and second-order asymptotic unbiasedness. Simulation studies and a real-world application in breast cancer detection validate its performance.

Conclusion: The paper identifies critical shortcomings in $K$-fold CV for risk prediction and introduces CAP as a theoretically sound and practically effective alternative.

Abstract: For training-data-based model risk prediction, $K$-fold cross-validation~(CV) is widely used to mitigate the well-known over-optimism of the empirical risk and is often regarded as reliable. However, for binary classification via empirical risk minimization, our numerical studies reveal a surprising phenomenon: $K$-fold CV may perform poorly in estimating class-specific risks, even worse than the empirical estimator. We perform a higher-order asymptotic analysis showing that $K$-fold CV may converge at a slower rate, whereas the empirical estimator exhibits a second-order asymptotic bias that explains its over-optimism. These findings motivate a novel two-step procedure for model risk prediction, termed cross-audit projection (CAP). The cross-audit step adopts the same resampling scheme as $K$-fold CV to estimate over-optimism in subsamples, while the asymptotic-theory-informed projection step adjusts for the reduced sample size in bias correction of the empirical risk. The resulting CAP estimator is first-order asymptotically equivalent to the empirical risk while achieving second-order asymptotic unbiasedness. An accompanying inference procedure is also developed. Simulation studies support theoretical advantages of CAP and demonstrate favorable finite-sample performance. An application to breast cancer detection further illustrates the proposed method.

</details>


<div id='cs.NI'></div>

# cs.NI [[Back]](#toc)

### [525] [Fully Unsupervised Detection of Physical Contacts on Subsea Cables via State-of-Polarization Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01484)
*Agastya Raj,Alvaro Doval,Tian Tian,Steinar Bjørnstad,Marco Ruffini*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: An unsupervised detector identifies cable contact events among a large dataset, highlighting instances such as trawler contacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance monitoring of State-of-Polarization changes on subsea cables and detect events like cable interference without needing labeled data.

Method: Use the Fast-Slow DSVDD detector, an unsupervised machine learning tool, applied to continuous monitoring across a deployed subsea cable.

Result: The detector successfully ranked five known trawler contacts within the top 13 instances out of 122,174 total recordings while detecting additional cable-contact events.

Conclusion: The approach effectively uncovers significant cable events, demonstrating the potential of unsupervised techniques for monitoring and analysis in subsea environments.

Abstract: We present a fully unsupervised Fast-Slow DSVDD detector for continuous State-of-Polarization monitoring on a deployed subsea cable. Trained without event labels, it ranks all five confirmed trawler contacts within the top 13 of 122,174 recordings and surfaces additional corroborated cable-contact events.

</details>


<div id='astro-ph.HE'></div>

# astro-ph.HE [[Back]](#toc)

### [526] [AI-enabled gravitational-waves searches for binary neutron stars at optimal sensitivity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01372)
*Bhavya Gupta,Deep Chatterjee,William Benoit,Ethan Marx,Christina Reissel,Seiya Tsukamoto,Kyungseop Yoon,Michael W. Coughlin,Philip Harris,Erik Katsavounidis*

Main category: astro-ph.HE

TL;DR: The paper introduces Aframe, an AI-based algorithm for identifying binary neutron star gravitational wave signals, achieving sensitivity akin to traditional methods but with lower computational demands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the computational challenges of detecting binary neutron star gravitational wave signals in real-time, which require significant resources due to millions of waveform comparisons.

Method: Aframe employs neural networks to identify signals in gravitational wave detector data and uses optimization techniques like heterodyning to manage longer-duration signals efficiently.

Result: The approach not only matches the sensitivity of traditional matched-filter pipelines but also significantly reduces computational and latency demands, requiring only a single GPU for online application.

Conclusion: Aframe demonstrates the capability of AI-enabled methods for rapid and efficient gravitational wave detection, applicable for both real-time and archival data analysis with reduced resource usage.

Abstract: Gravitational Waves (GWs) represent the newest window of astronomy, furthering our understanding of compact objects like black holes and neutron stars in the Universe. The signal from two merging neutron stars is especially interesting since it brings the prospect of concordant electromagnetic and neutrino emissions. Such multi-messenger observations have a transformational impact on fundamental physics, nuclear matter, astrophysics, and gravity. It was first witnessed in 2017 with the detection of the binary neutron star (BNS) merger GW170817. However, searching for BNS signals in real-time in the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA (LVK) GW detectors presents a computational challenge, as the data streaming out must be matched against $\sim$ million reference waveforms, which requires up to a thousand CPU cores. We present a different approach using neural networks to learn the presence of a signal in the data. Our algorithm, called Aframe, was deployed in the LVK's fourth observing run and was the first artificial intelligence (AI)-enabled search to detect multiple binary black holes (BBHs) live. In this work, we demonstrate that the approach extends to the lower-mass BNS regime, and is the first AI-enabled search that achieves sensitivity comparable to matched-filter pipelines at lower computational and latency costs. The challenge of the longer-duration BNS signals is addressed by heterodyning the data, following which the network architecture used for BBHs is sufficient to distinguish signal versus background. We also show that this analysis requires a single non-flagship GPU for online deployment. Furthermore, the design and adoption of inference-as-a-service tools allow rapid offline analysis using a distributed pool of GPU resources. Hence, aside from the use case of rapid online data analysis, we also establish the use of Aframe for efficient archival data analysis.

</details>


<div id='cs.GR'></div>

# cs.GR [[Back]](#toc)

### [527] [Benchmarking Federated Learning and Knowledge Distillation for Point Cloud Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01272)
*Aizierjiang Aiersilan*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks federated learning (FL) and knowledge distillation (KD) for privacy-sensitive, resource-limited 3D point cloud classification, revealing challenges in combining FL-KD pipelines effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the barriers of deploying 3D point cloud analysis in privacy-sensitive and resource-constrained settings where centralized data sharing is infeasible and models must run on edge devices.

Method: The authors developed a comprehensive benchmark encompassing 13 FL algorithms and 10 KD objectives, conducting 504 training runs across two datasets (ModelNet40 and a clinical dataset). They analyzed performance under extreme non-IID conditions and evaluated the combined FL-KD pipeline.

Result: Three key findings emerged: (1) FL under non-IID label skew shows sharp performance degradation, (2) KD compresses models significantly while maintaining or surpassing teacher model performance, and (3) evaluation pitfalls arise when distillation relies on labeled proxy splits, masking the effects of poorly performing FL models.

Conclusion: FL-KD pipelines should use label-free distillation objectives to ensure evaluation accuracy reflects the federated model's quality, avoiding misleading results influenced by reused proxy labels.

Abstract: Deploying 3D point cloud analysis in privacy-sensitive, resource-constrained settings faces two barriers: data cannot be centralized, and models must run on limited edge hardware. We present a multi-seed benchmark jointly evaluating federated learning (FL) and knowledge distillation (KD) for 3D point cloud classification. It spans 13 FL algorithms and 10 KD objectives (a 130-pair cross-product) across 504 training runs, evaluated on ModelNet40 and a clinical craniosynostosis dataset. We report three findings. First, under extreme non-IID label skew, standalone FL degrades sharply: on ModelNet40, the strongest method reaches 76.32% against a 92.26% centralized reference; on clinical data, the best reaches 75.83% against 100%. Second, distillation successfully compresses the teacher into a student 74.51% smaller and roughly twice as fast at inference, often matching or surpassing the teacher. Third, the combined pipeline exposes an evaluation pitfall: when distillation keeps a hard-label cross-entropy term on a labeled proxy split, a collapsed federated teacher (8.50%) paired with Logit-MSE still yields a 92.94% student. This 84.4-point gap reflects the proxy labels rather than the federated model, reusing the very labels whose privacy motivated federation. Objectives without hard labels instead track teacher quality ($r \approx 0.99$) and collapse when the teacher does. We therefore recommend evaluating FL-KD pipelines with label-free distillation so reported accuracy reflects the federated teacher, not the proxy.

</details>


<div id='cs.DL'></div>

# cs.DL [[Back]](#toc)

### [528] [Gender Differences in Research Topic and Method Selection in Library and Information Science: Perspectives from Three Top Journals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01828)
*Chengzhi Zhang,Siqi Wei,Yi Zhao,Liang Tian*

Main category: cs.DL

TL;DR: The paper investigates gender differences in research method selection within Library and Information Science (LIS), finding women prefer interviews while men favor theoretical approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the influence of gender on research method selection and examine potential gender-specific patterns to foster inclusivity in academia.

Method: A study in LIS using a fine-grained method classification system and the CogFT automatic classification model based on full-text cognition.

Result: Women show a preference for interviews, while men lean towards theoretical approaches, across various research topics.

Conclusion: The research highlights gender-specific tendencies in method selection and suggests strategies to improve gender inclusivity in academic research practices.

Abstract: Research in the social sciences has shown that there are gender differences in the selection of research methods, with women often opting for qualitative methods while men prefer quantitative methods. However, it is important to consider that research methods are generally chosen based on the research topic. To figure out the influence of gender on research method selection, a study was conducted in the field of Library and Information Science, using a more fine-grained method classification system and an automatic classification model called CogFT, which is based on full-text cognition. The findings showed that women tend to use Interview while men prefer Theoretical approach, across a range of topics. The study offers insights into the specific research design processes that contribute to gender differences in method selection and suggests ways to promoting gender inclusivity and equality in academia by considering research method use and guidance.

</details>


### [529] [Non-synchronism in Global Usage of Research Methods in Library and Information Science from 1990 to 2019](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01833)
*Chengzhi Zhang,Liang Tian*

Main category: cs.DL

TL;DR: The study analyzed 5,281 LIS research papers from 81 countries, using deep learning to classify research methods, and found significant cross-country differences in method usage that have declined over 30 years.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how Library and Information Science (LIS) research methods differ across countries and their impact on discipline development.

Method: The authors manually annotated research methods in a subset of LIS papers, developed a deep learning model for automatic classification, and conducted a comparative analysis of method usage internationally.

Result: The paper revealed that countries have unique research method profiles and that disparities in method use at national vs. international levels have diminished over the past three decades.

Conclusion: The findings provide insights into the unique characteristics of LIS research method usage per country, offering guidance for national discipline development and fostering international collaboration.

Abstract: The global development of Library and Information Science (LIS) is influenced by various factors such as the economy, society, culture, discipline, tradition, and more. Consequently, the research methods of LIS vary greatly among countries. To better understand these differences, we conducted a study of 5,281 research papers from 81 countries published in internationally representative journals over the past thirty years. We manually annotated the research methods used in some articles through content analysis, and subsequently developed and trained a deep learning model for automatic classification of research methods. Using this method, we conducted a comparative analysis of the usage of research methods in different countries. Our findings reveal that there are differences in the research methods used across countries, with each country having its unique research profile and distribution of research methods. Even when investigating the same topic, research methods can differ between countries. Our study also uncovers that there are differences between the national and international distribution of research methods, these differences have decreased over the past 30 years. By highlighting the characteristics of discipline development in various countries from the perspective of research methods, our study can help guide discipline development at the national level. This study provides insights into the usage trends of research methods across different countries and highlights the unique characteristics of discipline development in each country. This information can be valuable in promoting collaboration and understanding between countries and in guiding discipline development at the national level.

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.quant-gas'></div>

# cond-mat.quant-gas [[Back]](#toc)

### [530] [Q-GAIN: A Python Package for Machine Learning and Physically Informed Analysis Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02413)
*M. Doris,S. Guo,S. M. Koh,L. Ritter,A. R. Fritsch,S. Mukherjee,I. B. Spielman,J. P. Zwolak*

Main category: cond-mat.quant-gas

TL;DR: The paper introduces Q-GAIN, a Python package for applying machine learning and physics-informed analysis to cold-atom experiments, including tasks like classification, object detection, and feature detection in BEC images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to streamline ML and physics-informed techniques for analyzing cold-atom experiments, particularly in tasks like BEC feature recognition.

Method: Q-GAIN employs a modular workflow encompassing data preprocessing, ML-based feature identification, and physics-based analysis. It is tested on handwritten digit classification, soliton detection, and vortex identification in BEC images.

Result: Q-GAIN successfully implements its modular framework across common ML tasks (like MNIST classification) and specialized tasks (e.g., detecting solitons and vortices in BEC images).

Conclusion: Q-GAIN is an effective, modular tool for rapid deployment of ML and analysis techniques in the context of cold-atom experiments, demonstrating versatility across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Here we describe the quantum gas analysis and inference (Q-GAIN) Python package, which enables rapid deployment of machine learning (ML) and physics-informed analysis techniques for cold-atom experiments. Out of the box, Q-GAIN implements classification, object detection, and physics-informed metrics for feature detection in images of atomic Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs). Q-GAIN encourages a natural, module-based workflow: starting with data loading and preprocessing, followed by ML-based feature identification, and ending with conventional analysis techniques. We demonstrate this modularity by configuring Q-GAIN for three ML tasks. First, we demonstrate the basic workflow of the Q-GAIN framework by implementing the standard task of classifying handwritten digits from the MNIST dataset. Then, we re-implement our earlier soliton detection (SolDet) package in the Q-GAIN framework, enabling the detection and analysis of solitonic excitations in time-of-flight data. Finally, we develop an object-detection tool that identifies quantized vortices in images of ring-shaped BECs.

</details>


<div id='cs.LO'></div>

# cs.LO [[Back]](#toc)

### [531] [ADVENT: LLM-Driven Automatic Predicate Invention for ILP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01585)
*Tingting Yu,Pei-Cing Huang,Chan Hsu,Chan-Tung Ku,Yihuang Kang*

Main category: cs.LO

TL;DR: Predicate invention in ILP is advanced with ADVENT, leveraging Large Language Models for rule creation and cross-task reuse, achieving better success rates and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current PI methods depend heavily on domain expertise and fail to generalize across tasks due to semantically opaque predicates.

Method: ADVENT integrates LLM-driven abductive generation with Prolog deductive verification in an iterative loop, making use of LLMs to generate predicates with clear names and definitions.

Result: ADVENT improved success rates to 58% with LLM-driven PI (vs. 0% for ILP alone), formal verification raised this to 80%, and reusing the knowledge pool boosted performance by up to 31%.

Conclusion: ADVENT enhances predicate invention by utilizing LLMs and formal verification, opening paths for automating logic generation and improving cross-task knowledge reuse in ILP.

Abstract: Predicate invention (PI), the creation of new predicates to extend the hypothesis space, remains a critical bottleneck in Inductive Logic Programming (ILP). Existing methods rely on domain expertise and produce semantically opaque predicates, hindering adaptation to unfamiliar domains and cross-task reuse. We present ADVENT, an LLM-driven PI mechanism for ILP. ADVENT pairs LLM abductive generation with Prolog deductive verification, forming an iterative loop in which concrete execution results guide the LLM to refine candidate predicates. The mechanism leverages Large Language Models to identify implicit patterns in structured relational data and invent auxiliary predicates with meaningful names and definitions. Invented predicates and learned rules accumulate in a knowledge pool for cross-task reuse. Experiments on nine poker-hand concepts across seven LLMs show that LLM-driven PI achieves 58% success rate where ILP alone fails entirely, formal verification raises this to 80%, and the knowledge pool yields gains up to +31 percentage points, while producing human-interpretable rules. These results suggest that ADVENT offers a promising direction for automating predicate invention and enabling cross-task knowledge reuse in ILP.

</details>


<div id='eess.AS'></div>

# eess.AS [[Back]](#toc)

### [532] [Self-Supervised Test-Time Tuning for Packet Loss Concealment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01823)
*Yehoshua Dissen,Joseph Keshet*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: TTT-PLC introduces a method for tuning Packet Loss Concealment (PLC) models dynamically during inference time using self-supervised adaptation, without requiring external data or original signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation of this paper is to address the static nature of pretrained PLC models, which do not utilize the information exposed during a specific call or recording to improve performance, leading to suboptimal audio reconstruction.

Method: The proposed method, TTT-PLC, adapts PLC models through self-supervised learning by synthetically masking received audio packets, training the model against its own PLC objective, and then using the adapted model to handle actual packet loss. It operates without needing clean reference signals or external adaptation data.

Result: The results demonstrate that TTT-PLC effectively improves the concealment performance of two widely used PLC backbones, FRN and PARCnet, in both causal and non-causal settings, by dynamically adapting the models to the specific signal being processed.

Conclusion: The study concludes that pretrained PLC systems can benefit from dynamic test-time tuning, and the received portions of audio signals can serve as a valuable training signal for improved audio packet loss concealment.

Abstract: Packet loss concealment (PLC) reconstructs audio packets that are missing at the receiver, usually with a trained model whose parameters remain fixed at deployment time. This treats the PLC model as static, even though each call or recording exposes signal-specific information through the packets that did arrive. We present TTT-PLC, a self-supervised test-time tuning framework that adapts existing PLC models using only those received packets. The method creates supervision by synthetically masking portions of the available signal, training the model to conceal them with its native PLC objective, and then using the adapted model to reconstruct the true packet losses. No clean reference signal, external adaptation data, or architectural modification is required.
  We study TTT-PLC in two deployment settings. In the non-causal setting, the received file is available before reconstruction, allowing repeated self-supervised adaptation passes and providing a per-file adaptation ceiling. In the causal setting, audio is streamed without revising emitted samples; adaptation is performed only on completed past blocks, and updated parameters affect only future audio. We instantiate the framework on two public PLC backbones, FRN, a recurrent full-band speech PLC model, and PARCnet, a hybrid autoregressive-neural model for networked music. Across these settings, the results show that pretrained PLC systems do not need to be treated as fixed at inference time, the still-observed portions of a lossy signal can provide an effective training signal for improving concealment on that same signal.

</details>


### [533] [CNN Models for Microphone Array Covariance Matrix Upsampling and Acoustic Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01295)
*Marianthi Adamopoulou,Parthasaarathy Sudarsanam,David Diaz-Guerra,Meng Jiang,Archontis Politis,Seyed Jalaleddin Mousavirad,Tuomas Virtanen,Jan Lundgren*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: This paper develops and evaluates deep learning techniques to upsample a tetrahedral 4-microphone array's spatial resolution, achieving results comparable to a spherical 32-microphone array through covariance matrix estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limited sensor availability in practical systems restricts the spatial resolution of acoustic imaging, necessitating methods to enhance resolution without increasing hardware complexity.

Method: Five neural network architectures, using 2D convolutional layers and frequency dynamic convolution, are proposed to estimate a 32-microphone time-frequency covariance matrix from 4-microphone input data, trained on the STARSS23 dataset.

Result: The proposed architectures outperform a baseline in RMSE (best-performing model achieving 0.432 vs. baseline at 0.548) and demonstrate significant improvements in beamforming acoustic imaging, producing sound maps similar to those of a 32-microphone array.

Conclusion: Covariance upsampling through deep learning enhances the effective performance of a 4-microphone array to rival that of a 32-microphone array, offering a practical solution for high-resolution acoustic imaging with limited sensors.

Abstract: Acoustic imaging visualization is a core methodology in acoustics, enabling spatial analysis of sound sources and acoustic scenes. However, limited sensor availability in practical systems motivate approaches that enhance spatial resolution without increasing the hardware complexity. In this paper, we focus on upsampling virtually a tetrahedral 4-microphone array to a spherical 32-microphone array by estimating the covariance matrices of the channels employing deep learning techniques. Five neural network architectures are investigated for covariance upsampling for acoustic imaging using the real-world STARSS23 dataset. These models are developed to estimate a 32-microphone, time-frequency covariance matrix from a 4-microphone input covariance representation. The proposed architectures are based on 2D convolutional layers to capture the underlying spatial-spectral structure of covariance matrices, and are further enhanced with frequency dynamic convolution to model their frequency-dependent properties. The proposed architectures are evaluated in terms of root mean square error (RMSE) and using delay-and-sum beamforming acoustic imaging. Quantitative results show that all models outperform a random-guess baseline, which yields an RMSE of 0.548, with the best-performing architecture achieving an RMSE of 0.432. We analyze qualitatively the performance of the proposed models through beamforming heatmap visualizations derived from the 4-channel input covariance, the 32-channel ground truth, and the predicted 32-channel covariance matrices. These results demonstrate that covariance upsampling significantly enhances the effective performance of the 4-channel microphone array, producing sound maps that closely resemble those obtained with the 32-channel array.

</details>


### [534] [Few-Shot Open-Set Audio Classification Using Attention Information-Fused Prototypes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01297)
*Yanxiong Li,Jiaxin Tan,Qianqian Li,Guoqing Chen,Sen Huang,Tuomas Virtanen*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method for Few-shot Open-set Audio Classification (FOAC) that identifies query samples of known classes while rejecting those from unseen classes, showing improved performance and reduced computational complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the limitation in existing audio classification approaches, which often fail to reject samples of unseen classes during classification.

Method: The method involves a model composed of a ResNet-based encoder for embedding extraction and a classifier for generating prototypes for both seen and unseen classes. Training uses supervised learning for the encoder and meta-training for the classifier with joint loss supervision for the prototypes.

Result: The proposed method outperformed prior approaches in AUROC and accuracy metrics on three public datasets (LS-100, NSynth-100, FSC-89), with statistically significant results and lower computational complexity.

Conclusion: The FOAC method balances effective classification for few-shot learning with open-set rejection capabilities, marking progress over prior methods. The approach is computationally efficient and performs well across multiple datasets.

Abstract: Most existing audio classification methods suppose that each query (testing) sample belongs to a class of support (training) samples, and misrecognize samples of unseen classes as seen classes (cannot reject samples of unseen classes). In this study, we propose a method for Few-shot Open-set Audio Classification (FOAC), which can recognize query samples of seen classes after updating the model using a few support samples, and meanwhile reject query samples from unseen classes. We design a model consisting of an encoder and a classifier. The encoder is the backbone of a ResNet used for extracting embeddings. The classifier consists of prototype generators of few-shot classes and open-set classes. Prototypes of few-shot classes are obtained by fusing the class-discriminative information of support and query embeddings and by assigning larger weighting coefficient to representative part of the support embeddings. One prototype is generated for open-set classes using the proposed prototype generator. The encoder is trained with abundant samples of base classes in supervised manner, and then the prototypes of base classes are generated under the supervision of a joint loss. The classifier is trained using a few samples of few-shot classes in a meta-training way. Three public datasets (LS-100, NSynth-100, and FSC-89) are used to assess the performance of our method. Experiments show that our method has advantage over prior methods in AUROC and accuracy. This advantage has statistical significance for most prior methods. Our method has lower computational complexity than most prior methods. The code is at https://github.com/Jessytan/FOAC-AIFP.

</details>


### [535] [An Efficient vLLM-Based Inference Pipeline for Unified Audio Understanding and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02119)
*Haoran Wang,Jinchuan Tian,Siddhant Arora,Shinji Watanabe*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The paper presents a vLLM-based pipeline for unified speech understanding and generation, overcoming bottlenecks like multimodal generation delays and improving throughput for Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of high-throughput engines regarding multimodal generation, particularly in speech models, while preserving efficiency.

Method: The method involves extending autoregressive decoding for delay-pattern de-interleaving and coordinated multi-stream sampling. It integrates an on-GPU acoustic decoder for waveform synthesis and optimizes CFG implementations to maintain high throughput.

Result: The proposed framework overcomes CFG-related throughput reductions, achieving 80% of non-CFG throughput while enabling efficient multimodal inference.

Conclusion: The work provides an open-source solution improving efficiency in speech generation tasks, ensuring unified multimodal processing with minimal throughput compromise.

Abstract: While Large Multimodal Models excel in comprehension, high-throughput inference engines lack native support for multimodal generation. This is severe in Speech Language Models, where generating multi-layered audio tokens via decoupled AR+NAR or synchronous Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) with delay-pattern interleaving conflicts with standard single-stream loops. We present a vLLM-based inference pipeline for unified speech understanding and generation. We extend autoregressive decoding to natively execute delay-pattern de-interleaving and coordinated multi-stream sampling, integrating an on-GPU acoustic decoder for end-to-end waveform synthesis. Crucially, we overcome the shared intuition that Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) halves throughput. By co-scheduling paired conditional and unconditional requests within a continuous batch, our CFG implementation sustains 80% of non-CFG throughput, absorbing dual-request and logit merging overheads. We open-source our framework.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [536] [Scene-Conditioned PINN-GNN for Multipath RF Maps: Cross-Scene Generation and In-Scene Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01777)
*Lizhou Liu,Xiaohui Chen,Zihan Tang,Mengyao Ma,Wenyi Zhang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper presents a framework for RF map construction using physics-informed and graph neural networks, yielding accurate results under sparse data conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a robust method for constructing RF maps that accurately represent multipath propagation under various scenarios.

Method: A unified framework using a physics-informed neural network (PINN) for embedding electromagnetic constraints and a graph neural network (GNN) for enforcing spatial consistency, along with a novel evaluation metric.

Result: The proposed method outperforms baselines in RF map construction, providing robust generalization and high fidelity across sparse observations.

Conclusion: The framework successfully integrates physics-based and graph-based learning to construct RF maps, offering a practical tool for wireless optimization and channel modeling.

Abstract: Radio frequency (RF) maps provide a compact representation of multipath propagation characteristics and are fundamental to channel modeling, coverage analysis, and environment-aware wireless optimization. This paper proposes a unified RF map construction framework based on a physics-informed neural network (PINN) and a graph neural network (GNN), supporting both cross-scene generation and in-scene completion with 2D and 2.5D environmental representations. The PINN embeds electromagnetic propagation constraints to establish a physically consistent mapping from receiver locations to multipath parameters, including path gain, time of arrival, and angles, while the GNN enforces spatial consistency by modeling correlations among neighboring receivers. To comprehensively evaluate multipath reconstruction quality, we propose a peak-weighted dynamic time warping metric that jointly accounts for amplitude errors and peak delay misalignment in channel impulse responses. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms image-based, diffusion-based, and interpolation baselines across both map-level and multipath-level metrics, achieving robust generalization and high-fidelity RF map construction under sparse observations.

</details>


### [537] [Fourier Preconditioning for Neural Feature Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02199)
*Preston Pitzer,Anish Pradhan,Harpreet S. Dhillon*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper explores improving mutual information-inspired feature extraction through H-Score objectives, introducing unitary preconditioning via FFT to enhance predictive dependence in low-data regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-dimensional embeddings that retain nonlinear dependencies are often noisy with direct MI estimation in limited data scenarios, necessitating robust alternatives like H-Score.

Method: The authors apply unitary preconditioning (specifically FFT-based) to H-Score feature extraction networks and evaluate performance using spectral entropy and energy metrics to predict inference improvements.

Result: Empirical evaluations across eight datasets reveal FFT preconditioning can achieve up to 50% NMSE reduction in data-constrained environments.

Conclusion: FFT preconditioning enhances H-Score-based feature extraction in stationary processes, and proposed metrics reliably predict performance boosts and identify cases where preconditioning is counterproductive.

Abstract: Mutual information (MI)-inspired feature learning techniques are capable of generating low-dimensional embeddings that retain nonlinear dependence structures, but direct estimations of MI suffer from noisy probability distribution estimates in the low-data regime. The H-Score objective, computed from second-order statistics, provides a practical proxy metric for training feature extraction networks. We prove that H-Score is invariant to invertible transformations in the unrestricted functional setting, but becomes sensitive to input basis rotations under constrained approximation classes. Consequently, we study unitary preconditioning for H-Score networks and show that selecting an appropriate basis rotation reduces finite-width truncation error by concentrating predictive dependence into fewer dominant modes. We identify the fast Fourier transform (FFT) as an effective data-independent, low-cost preconditioner for approximately stationary processes, where spectral structure induces concentration of the cross-covariance singular value spectrum. We introduce training-free metrics based on spectral entropy and cumulative dependence energy to quantify basis suitability and predict downstream inference gains prior to network training. Experiments across eight multivariate datasets demonstrate that FFT preconditioning is particularly useful in resource-constrained regimes, achieving up to 50% normalized mean squared error (NMSE) reduction, while the proposed metrics correlate with observed performance gains and correctly identify cases where spectral preconditioning is detrimental.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [538] [Evaluating Chunking Strategies for Retrieval-Augmented Generation on Academic Texts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01852)
*Valentin J. J. Kreileder,Johannes Reisinger,Andreas Fischer*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper assesses cluster-based semantic chunking in Retrieval-Augmented Generation systems, finding it does not outperform simpler chunking methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve retrieval and answer quality in RAG systems using cluster-based semantic chunking.

Method: Evaluating cluster-based semantic chunking against fixed-size and recursive chunking using long academic documents and the RAGAs framework.

Result: Cluster-based chunking showed no improvement over simpler strategies, with performance influenced by document formatting and preprocessing.

Conclusion: Cluster-based chunking may not be a reliable enhancement for RAG systems under certain conditions, necessitating further exploration of preprocessing and formatting nuances.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems use the question-answering capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) to access information outside their parameters. We evaluate if cluster-based semantic chunking improves retrieval and answer quality compared to fixed-size and recursive chunking evaluating on long, structured academic theses using the Retrieval Augmented Generation Assessment (RAGAs) framework. RAGAs based faithfulness shows limited reliability in this setup. Performance on fixed versus document specific questions varied substantially, likely related to the formatting of documents and preprocessing. Under the tested configuration, cluster-based chunking did not outperform simpler strategies.

</details>


### [539] [Bi-NAS: Towards Effective and Personalized Explanation for Recommender Systems via Bi-Level Neural Architecture Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01387)
*Longfeng Wu,Yao Zhou,Tong Zeng,Zhimin Peng,Bhanu Pratap Singh Rawat,Lecheng Zheng,Giovanni Seni,Dawei Zhou*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper introduces Bi-level Neural Architecture Search (Bi-NAS) to optimize recommendation explanations by refining neural architectures and integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) for better personalized justifications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current recommender systems struggle with providing effective and personalized explanations, which are essential for user engagement, trust, and decision-making.

Method: The Bi-NAS framework optimizes explanations by exploring intra-layer and inter-layer neural design spaces for cross-attention and feature interaction. It also uses LLMs with zero-shot prompting for enhanced explanation generation.

Result: Evaluations on four real-world datasets show that Bi-NAS not only improves recommendation accuracy but also enhances the depth and transparency of explanations.

Conclusion: Bi-NAS offers a novel approach to boost both the functional accuracy and explanatory quality of recommender systems, aligning closely with user preferences and item attributes for better user experience.

Abstract: Recommender systems are vital in helping users navigate vast amounts of information, offering personalized suggestions and effective explanations for these recommendations. While previous efforts have attempted to provide such explanations, evaluating their effectiveness across various scenarios remains a challenge. Enhancing these explanations is essential for improving user engagement, trust, and decision-making. To facilitate effective explanations within the recommender system, we propose a Bi-level Neural Architecture Search (Bi-NAS) framework to optimize explanations. This approach simultaneously refines cross-attention mechanisms and feature interaction functions by exploring both intra-layer and inter-layer design spaces. Furthermore, we integrate Large Language Models (LLMs) to enhance explanation generation, leveraging zero-shot prompting to produce more effective and personalized justifications. By aligning user feature preferences with item quality scores, our approach ensures that explanations reflect both user intent and item attributes, improving transparency and reasoning depth. Extensive evaluations on four real-world datasets demonstrate that Bi-NAS not only boosts recommendation accuracy but also significantly improves the effectiveness of explanations for recommender systems, providing users with clear and reliable insights into the suggestions they receive.

</details>


### [540] [IntentTune: Using user demand and personalization to resolve "unknown" query intents for e-commerce search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01530)
*Rachith Aiyappa,Ishita Khan,Chester Palen-Michel,Jayanth Yetukuri,Samarth Agrawal,Mehran Elyasi,Shuang Zhou*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces IntentTune, a framework that resolves ambiguous e-commerce queries by leveraging user-specific behavioral signals and population-level demand, finding user-specific signals to be more effective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of interpreting under-specified user queries in e-commerce search, which lack explicit attributes and hinder the accurate retrieval of relevant results.

Method: Utilizes IntentTune to analyze and infer user intent by utilizing either user-specific behavioral signals or aggregated population-level demand patterns, conducting experiments on e-commerce data to evaluate performance.

Result: User-specific behavioral signals, particularly prior search queries, outperform population-level statistics and static profile information in interpreting under-specified queries for attributes like age, gender, and size.

Conclusion: Personalized behavioral data significantly enhances the accurate detection of user intent in ambiguous or under-specified e-commerce search queries, surpassing the effectiveness of general population-level insights.

Abstract: Understanding user intent is fundamental to delivering relevant search results in e-commerce. However, substantial fraction of real-world queries are under-specified (e.g., "watch" or "shirt"), lacking explicit attributes such as gender or age group. This ambiguity poses a significant challenge for query intent detection models in e-commerce search systems, which must accurately infer latent user intent (e.g., age, gender) to support effective downstream retrieval. We introduce IntentTune, a framework for resolving ambiguous or under-specified query intents by leveraging either (1) user-specific behavioral signals including search history, browsing activity, and profile attributes or (2) population-level demand patterns aggregated across all users. Through experiments on real-world e-commerce data, we first demonstrate that population-level demand patterns alone are insufficient to reliably infer intent in under-specified queries. We then demonstrate that user-specific behavioral signals -- particularly prior search queries -- outperform both population-level statistics and static profile information for inferring gender, age group, product category, and size intent from underspecified queries.

</details>


### [541] [Bringing Agentic Search to Earth Observation Data Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02387)
*Minghan Yu,Youran Sun,Chugang Yi,Yixin Wen,Haizhao Yang*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This study introduces an agentic search system for NASA's geoscience datasets and tools, demonstrating that knowledge graphs paired with advanced language models can significantly improve dataset-tool retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The difficulty in finding relevant datasets and tools for geoscience research even for domain experts motivated the development of a more efficient search system.

Method: The authors used NASA Earth Observation Knowledge Graph to create the NASA-EO-Bench benchmark, fine-tuned a neural scorer on 47k query-dataset pairs, combined it with BM25 for retrieval, and added zero-shot agentic reranking with LLMs.

Result: The fine-tuned neural scorer exceeded traditional methods with over 5x improvement in R@10 and MRR when fused with BM25. The zero-shot agentic reranking further improved MRR by 28%.

Conclusion: Agentic search systems leveraging both supervised methods and LLM reasoning can transform dataset retrieval in geoscience, showcasing the effectiveness of combining knowledge graphs and advanced AI techniques.

Abstract: NASA and its data centers hold thousands of geoscience datasets and tools like Worldview, Giovanni, the Science Discovery Engine, and Harmony. Finding the right one is hard even for domain experts. We present an agentic search system, deployed as a public service for the geoscience community, that takes a natural-language research query and returns the matching datasets and tools. We demonstrate that, in the era of large language models, the latent value of knowledge graphs (KGs) can be substantially amplified through agentic search. From the NASA Earth Observation Knowledge Graph (NASA EO-KG) we derive NASA-EO-Bench, an open benchmark of 47k query-dataset pairs (21k task-based queries). A neural scorer fine-tuned on NASA-EO-Bench beats cosine and BM25 baselines. Further combining it with BM25 via score fusion raises both Recall@10 (R@10) and MRR by over 5x. On top of this supervised pipeline, we add a zero-shot agentic reranking stage that, without any additional training, lifts MRR by 28% on a stratified N=200 subset, showing that LLM reasoning is complementary to supervised retrieval.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [542] [ExPerT: Personalizing LLM Responses to Users' Domain Expertise via Query-Wise Semantic and Keystroke Behavioral Cues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01242)
*Yeji Park,Jiwon Tark,Taesik Gong*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: ExPerT is a personalized query framework for adapting LLM responses to users' expertise, significantly improving inference accuracy and satisfaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing personalization methods fail to dynamically adapt to query-specific expertise variation.

Method: ExPerT integrates semantic-behavioral expertise inference using query analysis and keystroke dynamics with tailored response generation.

Result: Experimentation showed ExPerT reduced expertise inference error by 65.7% and increased user satisfaction by 17.52%.

Conclusion: ExPerT effectively personalizes language model responses based on expertise, enhancing overall response quality and user satisfaction.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used by end users, yet existing personalization methods relying on static profiles or text-only signals fail to capture query-specific expertise variation. We present ExPerT, a query-wise personalization framework that adapts LLM responses to users' query domain expertise by combining semantic and behavioral cues. ExPerT consists of two key components: (i) a semantic-behavioral expertise inference module that jointly interprets query text and keystroke dynamics via in-context LLM prompting, and (ii) an expertise-conditioned response generation that adapts the level of detail, terminology, and conceptual complexity. Our user study with 40 participants and 1270 queries demonstrated that ExPerT reduced expertise inference error by 65.7% compared to the strongest baseline (MAE = 0.398 vs. 1.162) and improved response satisfaction by 17.52% (from 3.71 to 4.36) on a 5-point Likert scale.

</details>


### [543] [What Types of Human-AI Teams Exist?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02198)
*Nathan Hughes,Ibrahim Habli*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper analyzes 53 studies on human-AI teams, identifies five team-type clusters, and raises concerns about the transferability of insights between studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To clarify the diverse types and characteristics of human-AI teams studied in literature, and address the lack of synthesis in the field.

Method: The researchers categorized 53 papers into five clusters: AI Assistant, Ad-hoc Dependency, Ad-hoc Forced Dependency, Paired Equanimity, and Group Equanimity, using psychological taxonomies of teaming.

Result: They discovered disparity in team types under the same definition and highlighted issues in transferring insights between studies.

Conclusion: The paper provides a framework for identifying human-AI team types, proposes a checklist for reporting these teams, and suggests methods to better synthesize future research.

Abstract: Human-AI teaming has received increasing attention in the literature. However, the range of studies conducted in multiple domains make it difficult to understand what types of teams are being studied, and in what ways are they similar/different from one another. In this study, we analyse 53 papers on human-AI teams and categorise them into five main clusters based on psychological taxonomies of teaming; AI Assistant, Ad-hoc Dependency, Ad-hoc Forced Dependency, Paired Equanimity, and Group Equanimity. Each cluster represents a unique combination of holistic team-level characteristics, indicating there are multiple disparate team types studied under the same definition. In turn, this raises the question of whether insights are truly transferable between papers. We conclude with guidance on how to identify the types of human-AI teams studied, a checklist for reporting a human-AI team in research work, and ways in which the field can be further synthesised.

</details>


<div id='cs.DS'></div>

# cs.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [544] [Scalable and Distributed Silhouette Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01993)
*Ilie Sarpe,Federico Altieri,Andrea Pietracaprina,Geppino Pucci,Fabio Vandin*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: The paper introduces efficient and rigorous sampling-based algorithms to estimate the silhouette score of clustering with significantly reduced computational complexity compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Silhouette scores are widely used to evaluate clustering quality, but their exact computation requires $Θ(n^2)$ calculations, which is computationally prohibitive for large datasets. Existing approximation methods lack provable guarantees on accuracy.

Method: The paper proposes sampling-based algorithms that perform $O(nk\varepsilon^{-2}\ln (nk/\delta))$ distance computations to estimate the silhouette score with additive error $O(\varepsilon)$, controlled by parameters $\varepsilon$ and $\delta$. It also introduces distributed algorithms compatible with MapReduce and MPC frameworks.

Result: The proposed methods achieve the best trade-off between accuracy and efficiency compared to state-of-the-art approaches. The distributed algorithms also scale effectively to handle large datasets.

Conclusion: The paper provides the first rigorous approximation methods for local and global silhouette estimation, offering a practical solution for clustering evaluation on massive datasets.

Abstract: The silhouette is one of the most widely used measures to assess the quality of a $k$-clustering of a dataset of $n$ elements. Its evaluation requires no information beyond the clustering assignment. In addition, the silhouette is extremely easy to interpret, providing a score to measure the quality of a clustering as a whole or for each element. The exact computation of the: (i) silhouette of each element of a dataset; and (ii) the global silhouette of the clustering; require $Θ(n^2)$ distance calculations, under general metrics. The quadratic complexity $Θ(n^2)$ is extremely prohibitive, especially on massive modern datasets. Surprisingly, existing approximate methods using $O(n^2)$ distance calculations are heuristics not offering provable and controllable guarantees on the quality of their results.
  We introduce the first rigorous and efficient algorithms to estimate: (i) the (local) silhouette of each element of a dataset; and (ii) the (global) silhouette; of any metric $k$-clustering. Our methods, based on sampling, perform $O(nk\varepsilon^{-2}\ln (nk/δ))$ distance computations, and provide estimates with additive error $O(\varepsilon)$ with probability at least $1-δ$. That is, parameters $\varepsilon$ and $δ$ in $(0,1)$ control the trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. We also introduce a scalable and distributed design of our methods for the MapReduce and Massively Parallel Computing (MPC) frameworks. Our distributed algorithms use a constant number of rounds and sublinear local memory. Finally, we perform extensive experiments against state-of-the-art approaches. The results show that our new techniques yield the best trade-off between accuracy and efficiency for both local and global silhouette estimation. In addition, our methods scale efficiently to massive datasets for which an exact computation of the silhouette is not practical.

</details>


### [545] [Tight Lower Bounds for the Multi-Secretary Problem via Bellman Certificates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02150)
*Jiawei Zhang*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: This paper establishes that the additive regret in the one-resource multi-secretary problem grows as \((\log T)^2\) for certain gapped distributions, proving existing upper bounds are tight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether the additional logarithmic factor in the \((\log T)^2\) regret bounds for bounded-density distributions with support gaps is necessary.

Method: The authors use Bellman certificates, a relaxation of the Bellman recursion, to construct explicit lower bounds and understand the role of support gaps in increased regret.

Result: The paper proves that for a mixture of two separated uniform distributions at critical capacity, the optimal regret grows as \((\log T)^2\). It also establishes similar results for other gapped distributions with vanishing densities near support edges.

Conclusion: The upper bound of \((\log T)^2\) for bounded-density gapped distributions is tight, providing crucial insights into the role of support gaps in regret growth in the multi-secretary problem.

Abstract: This paper studies additive regret in the multi-secretary problem, defined as the gap between the expected offline prophet reward and the reward of the best online policy. Prior work established \(O(\log T)\) regret for bounded-density distributions with connected support and \(O((\log T)^2)\) upper bounds for bounded-density distributions with support gaps. It was unknown whether the extra logarithmic factor is necessary even in the one-resource model. We prove that it is necessary. For a mixture of two separated uniform distributions at the critical capacity, the optimal regret grows at least on the order of \((\log T)^2\). Thus the existing \(O((\log T)^2)\) upper bounds for bounded-density gapped instances, including those implied by network revenue management models with continuous rewards, are tight in this simplest specialization. The same framework also yields a matching lower bound for gapped distributions whose gap-facing densities vanish near the support edges; this companion result is given in the appendix. The proofs use Bellman certificates: feasible solutions to a relaxation of the exact Bellman recursion. This framework converts lower bounds into explicit certificate constructions and identifies why support gaps permit larger regret.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [546] [Quantifying the Uncertainty of Blindly Estimated Room Embeddings Using a Dispersion-Calibrated Score](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01527)
*Yang Xiang,Philipp Götz,Emanuël A. P. Habets,Andreas Walther,Wenwu Wang,Philip J. B. Jackson*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper addresses unreliable room embeddings from reverberant speech by proposing a framework for robust embeddings and uncertainty scoring without supervision for downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Room embeddings are heavily impacted by variable speech content and recording degradation, reducing their reliability and effectivity in downstream applications.

Method: The framework anchors room embeddings to a latent RIR space, employs multi-view data structures with KL-based alignment, adds a multi-positive contrastive term for robustness, and uses a lightweight uncertainty head optimized with rank-based objectives.

Result: The proposed model improves robustness of room embeddings against speech variability and corruption, while the uncertainty score correlates well with representation dispersion, aiding selective predictions.

Conclusion: The framework enhances the reliability of room embeddings using a structured approach, enabling accurate predictions with minimal supervision and inference requirements.

Abstract: Room embeddings derived from reverberant speech are often unreliable: speech content and recording degradation can alter the representation even when speaker, room, and source-receiver geometry remain unchanged, degrading downstream task performance. We propose a framework that learns room embeddings robust to speech-content variation and a representation-level uncertainty score from reverberant speech without downstream-task supervision. The embedding is anchored to a structured room impulse response (RIR) latent space and trained using a multi-view data structure with Kullback-Leibler (KL)-based alignment; a multi-positive contrastive term further refines robustness. A lightweight uncertainty head is calibrated using the dispersion of corruption-induced embeddings and optimized with a rank-based objective. Across waveform- and spectrogram-level corruptions, the score is consistent with representation dispersion and enables effective selective prediction while requiring only a single utterance at inference.

</details>


### [547] [A Multi-Branch Hierarchy-Aware Framework for Heterogeneous Audio Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01974)
*Beile Ning,Jiayi Yu,Zitong Wang,Yufei Hu,Wenjun Xu,Yuanhang Qian,Zhongxin Bai,Gongping Huang*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper presents a system for the DCASE 2026 Challenge's audio classification using CLAP-based representations with hierarchical post-processing, achieving a best hierarchical F1 score of 81.25%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve classification accuracy for heterogeneous audio recordings according to the Broad Sound Taxonomy (BST), focusing on both second-level predictions and top-level consistency.

Method: The system employs training set expansion using BSD35k, feature-specific branches for acoustic modeling, and hierarchy-aware classifiers combined with KNN-based post-processing.

Result: The system achieves a single-model Hier. F1 score of 80.84% and ensemble systems reach 81.25% and 81.18%, improving upon baseline performance.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates the effectiveness of incorporating hierarchical and feature-specific strategies, achieving high performance for the audio classification task in the DCASE 2026 challenge.

Abstract: This technical report describes our system for Task 1 of the DCASE 2026 Challenge, which aims to classify heterogeneous audio recordings according to the Broad Sound Taxonomy (BST). The task requires both accurate second-level prediction and consistency with the top-level taxonomy. Our system is built on CLAP-based audio-text representations and is improved along three strategies: expanding the training set with a filtered subset of BSD35k, enhancing acoustic modeling with feature-specific branches, and refining predictions using hierarchy-aware classifiers and KNN-based post-processing. Among the acoustic features considered, the log-STFT branch provides the strongest single-model performance. With KNN-based post-processing, our best single system achieves a hierarchical F1 score (Hier. F1) of 80.84% on the BSD10k-v1.2 set under the same evaluation protocol as the baseline. We further construct ensemble systems by combining models with complementary acoustic features and classification heads, achieving Hier. F1 scores of 81.25% and 81.18%, respectively.

</details>


### [548] [SelectTSL: Prompt-Guided Selective Target Sound Localization in Complex Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02343)
*Ziyang Jiang,Yu Chen,Zexu Pan,Xinyuan Qian,Bowen Xing,Ivor W. Tsang,Xu-Cheng Yin,Haizhou Li*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper proposes SelectTSL, a novel architecture for selectively localizing user-specified sound sources using prompt-guided embeddings and refined spatial cues, outperforming existing methods in diverse environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep learning methods struggle to selectively localize specific sound sources in complex multi-source scenarios, motivating the need for a system capable of focusing on user-specified targets.

Method: The authors introduce SelectTSL, an end-to-end model that uses a Prompt-Guided Selective Attention Module (PGSA) to create prompt-informed embeddings, which guide an inter-channel phase difference (IPD) enhancer to refine phase cues for selective sound source localization.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that SelectTSL exceeds baseline performance and generalizes well to both synthetic and real-world acoustic settings.

Conclusion: SelectTSL effectively addresses selective localization challenges by integrating prompt-based embeddings with spatial cue refinement, proving its utility in diverse and dynamic auditory environments.

Abstract: Humans can selectively attend to a target sound and estimate its direction in complex scenarios, whereas such selective localization remains challenging for current deep learning-based systems. Sound source localization (SSL) has achieved remarkable success with deep learning, yet most methods localize all active sources without selectivity. Conversely, target sound extraction (TSE) extracts sources using multimodal prompts but typically fails to preserve the multichannel spatial information required for accurate localization. To bridge this gap, we formulate the task of prompt-guided selective target sound localization and propose SelectTSL, an end-to-end architecture that localizes only the user-specified target in multi-source acoustic scenes. Specifically, we design a target-aware selective localization strategy that employs a Prompt-Guided Selective Attention Module (PGSA) to generate prompt-informed embeddings. These embeddings guide an inter-channel phase difference (IPD) enhancer to refine raw phase cues, fusing with target magnitudes to jointly estimate direction of arrival (DoA) and target-source cardinality, i.e., the number of target sound sources. This coupled design effectively focuses on the user-specified target spatial cues for selective localization and also handles time-varying numbers of target sources. Extensive experiments on both synthetic data and real-world recordings demonstrate that our proposed method consistently outperforms other baselines and exhibits robust generalization to real acoustic environments.

</details>


<div id='physics.ins-det'></div>

# physics.ins-det [[Back]](#toc)

### [549] [APEIRON: composing smart TDAQ systems for high energy physics experiments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02429)
*Roberto Ammendola,Andrea Biagioni,Carlotta Chiarini,Andrea Ciardiello,Paolo Cretaro,Ottorino Frezza,Francesca Lo Cicero,Alessandro Lonardo,Michele Martinelli,Pier Stanislao Paolucci,Pierpaolo Perticaroli,Cristian Rossi,Francesco Simula,Matteo Turisini,Piero Vicini*

Main category: physics.ins-det

TL;DR: APEIRON is a multi-FPGA distributed processing framework for smart trigger and data acquisition systems in high-energy physics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the computational challenges in high-energy physics experiments, particularly TDAQ systems, by leveraging multi-FPGA architectures.

Method: Developed distributed hardware architecture and software stack, from device drivers to a high-level dataflow programming model based on High-Level Synthesis.

Result: Successful design of APEIRON's framework and communication infrastructure, demonstrated through a particle identification application for the NA62 experiment.

Conclusion: APEIRON effectively integrates hardware and software to enable advanced TDAQ systems, showcasing its applicability in particle identification scenarios.

Abstract: We present APEIRON, a distributed heterogeneous processing framework comprising both hardware architecture and software stack for multi-FPGA systems. Targeting smart trigger and data acquisition (TDAQ) systems in high energy physics, APEIRON spans the full software hierarchy: from low-level device drivers to a high-level dataflow programming model based on High-Level Synthesis. We describe the framework design, its core communication infrastructure, and a particle identification application for the NA62 experiment as a representative physics use case.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [550] [LIB-TRAP: Standard Cell Library Hardware Trojan Risk Assessment and Prevention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01526)
*Harish Kumar Dharavath,Md Muhtasim Alam Chowdhury,Rozhin Yasaei,Soheil Salehi*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper identifies security risks posed by tampered standard cells in digital designs, proposing a new hardware Trojan threat model where malicious standard cell libraries are used for hardware synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the underexplored security vulnerabilities in standard cells, which are central to modern digital designs, and the potential threats posed by malicious Hardware Trojan insertion in these building blocks.

Method: The study develops a threat model where standard cells of a library are tampered to include deactivated Hardware Trojans. Upon replacement during fabrication, these Trojans become active. Libraries Saed32nm and Sky130nm are used and benchmark circuits synthesized using clean and tampered libraries for comparison, evaluating features like cell count, area, and power.

Result: Malicious standard cells successfully masked Hardware Trojans from IC designers, demonstrating stealth and efficacy in multiple benchmark circuits, including AES-128, an Ethernet controller, and DMA engine.

Conclusion: The research highlights the risks posed by untrusted standard cell libraries and proves their capability to stealthily introduce Hardware Trojans into digital designs, posing serious hardware security concerns.

Abstract: Vulnerabilities inherent to the fabless semiconductor manufacturing model have significantly increased the risk of malicious Hardware Trojan (HT) insertion, posing severe threats to hardware security. Several HT mitigation and detection strategies have been developed, and existing works explore the insertion of HTs in the space between standard cells in an integrated circuit. However, there is a lack of research into the vulnerabilities posed by the building blocks of most digital designs on the market today, the standard cells. This work investigates a novel threat model in which standard cells are considered untrusted. Our proposed threat model provides the design house with a tampered standard cell library. The intended netlist is synthesized and implemented using the tampered library. During fabrication, a nefarious foundry replaces the library's deactivated HT cells with activated counterparts. Using open-source and industry-standard Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools, existing standard cell libraries, Saed32nm and Sky130nm, are converted into malicious libraries capable of masking the presence of arbitrary HTs from IC designers. The malicious library is then applied and characterized in multiple standard benchmark designs. To demonstrate the efficacy and stealthiness of this standard cell-based attack vector, three benchmark circuits, an AES-128 encryption core, an Ethernet controller, and a WISHBONE DMA engine, were synthesized using both clean and Trojan-infected libraries across Synopsys 32nm and SkyWater 130nm technologies. Design-level features, including total cell count, total area, dynamic power consumption, and static power, were extracted from these synthesized circuits to serve as inputs for binary classification

</details>


### [551] [Overthink-Triggered Slowdown Attacks on LVLM-Based Robotic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01518)
*Qiang Han,Jie Wu,Bo Chen*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper discusses a novel attack on robotic systems utilizing Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) by embedding scene text to induce overthinking behavior, causing significant inference delays with high success across various models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the critical safety risk posed by overthinking behaviors in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) within robotic systems, which adversaries can exploit to slow down decision-making.

Method: Introduced a three-stage framework: constructing reasoning-intensive scene text corpus, performing black-box searches guided by prefix-based scores, and evaluating black-box transfer across LVLMs for latency amplification and attack validation.

Result: Experimental results show that the identified triggers significantly amplify latency in all tested LVLMs, with slowdown ratios ranging from 1.0x to 6.96x. Physical implementation of triggers still caused up to 4.74x latency amplification.

Conclusion: The discovered triggers are transferable across multiple LVLMs and consistently induce significant inference delays, highlighting a vulnerability in LVLM-integrated robotic systems that poses safety concerns.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have been increasingly integrated into robotic systems. However, these models may exhibit overthinking behaviors, where they generate excessively long reasoning traces, incurring an excessive inference time. This overthinking behavior poses a serious risk to robotic systems, as the adversary can deliberately trigger overthinking to slow down the decision making of a victim robotic system, causing a variety of safety issues (i.e., an overthinking-induced slowdown attack). To initiate this attack, an adversary can embed carefully crafted, human-readable scene text into the visual scene observed by a victim robotic agent, causing significant inference delays even under a strict black-box setting. Therefore, the embedded scene text serves as a significant "trigger" for the attack.
  This work systematically identifies and validates transferable triggers of overthinking in robotic systems by introducing a three-stage framework. First, we construct a diverse corpus of reasoning-intensive scene text and extract overthinking-correlated lexical features from short response prefixes. Second, we perform an efficient black-box search guided by a prefix-based proxy score while selectively confirming a small set of top candidates with full latency measurements. Third, we evaluate black-box transfer using a fixed pool of triggers on unseen images and multiple LVLMs, reporting latency amplification and attack success rates under standard thresholds. Across three representative LVLMs, all triggers yield slowdown ratios greater than 1.0x, with the strongest single-trigger case reaching 6.96x. The physical printing of the text trigger still causes up to 4.74x latency amplification. These results demonstrate that our discovered triggers are transferred between multiple LVLM models and consistently cause significant slowdowns in robotic systems.

</details>


### [552] [Knowledge Over Parameters: Evolving Smart Contract Vulnerability Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01742)
*Yuqiang Sun,Han Liu,Ying Li,Yiran Zhang,Zong Cao,Ziyun Guo,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces EvoVuln, a new method for automated smart contract vulnerability detection that requires minimal labeled samples to refine detection logic using procedural knowledge evolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for detecting smart contract vulnerabilities rely heavily on extensive labeled datasets or manual crafting, which are scarce and inefficient.

Method: EvoVuln employs two mechanisms: a Runtime with IoC architecture for faithful policy execution and a two-phase evolution pipeline for refining detection rules using both cold-start stress tests and few-shot grounding.

Result: EvoVuln achieves a 71% macro-average F1-score across five vulnerability types, significantly outperforming existing methods and demonstrating portability across models with minimal cost.

Conclusion: EvoVuln provides an efficient and portable solution for vulnerability detection that does not depend on large datasets or heavy resource usage, offering improvements in both accuracy and accessibility.

Abstract: Smart contract vulnerabilities are predominantly logic bugs whose detection requires structured, step-by-step procedural knowledge of attack patterns and contract semantics. Existing LLM-based methods struggle to generate this knowledge automatically: prompt-based methods rely on manually crafted detection rules, while fine-tuning requires massive labeled datasets that are inherently scarce in this domain. We present EvoVuln, an automated framework that reformulates vulnerability detection as a procedural knowledge evolution problem, synthesizing and refining detection logic using only a minimal number of labeled samples.
  To achieve this, EvoVuln introduces two key mechanisms. First, a Runtime with an Inversion of Control (IoC) architecture compiles detection rules into Executable Policies. This strictly decouples deterministic control flow from LLM semantic reasoning, ensuring faithful logical adherence and producing dense diagnostic telemetry for precise error localization. Second, a two-phase evolution pipeline refines the rule via abductive semantic debugging without any parameter updates: Cold Start bootstraps and stress-tests an initial rule using auto-synthesized corner cases; Few-Shot Evolving then grounds the policy in real-world semantics using only five vulnerable and five safe examples per vulnerability type.
  Evaluated across five real-world vulnerability types, EvoVuln achieves a 71% macro-average F1-score, outperforming all baselines. The evolved procedural knowledge is portable across models: it enables a lightweight, low-cost model to surpass a much larger zero-shot model by 19 percentage points, and transfers to other LLMs without retraining, at a one-time evolution cost under $50.

</details>


### [553] [Cloak and Detonate: Scanner Evasion and Dynamic Detection of Agent Skill Malware](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02357)
*Zimo Ji,Congying Xu,Zongjie Li,Yudong Gao,Xin Wei,Shuai Wang,Shing-Chi Cheung*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper identifies security vulnerabilities in LLM coding agents relying on third-party skills and presents a novel runtime auditor, SkillDetonate, which detects malicious behavior with high accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the significant security risk posed by third-party skills utilized by LLM coding agents, particularly due to insufficient defenses against adaptive evasion techniques employed by malicious payloads.

Method: The research introduces an evasion framework, SkillCloak, which uses Structural Obfuscation and Self-Extracting Skill (SFS) Packing to bypass static skill scanners. To improve defenses, the authors developed SkillDetonate, a runtime auditor that detects attacks via behavior-centric analysis, using sandbox execution and marker-based taint analysis.

Result: SkillCloak successfully evades most existing scanners with over 90% effectiveness. In contrast, SkillDetonate achieves a 97% detection rate with a low 2% false-positive rate and maintains high performance on real-world malicious skills.

Conclusion: Appearance-based static auditing techniques are insufficient for detecting malicious third-party skills in LLM coding agents. A behavior-centric, runtime approach like SkillDetonate offers a more robust defense against evolving attack techniques.

Abstract: LLM coding agents increasingly rely on third-party agent skills from public marketplaces, which execute with the agent's privileges and create a software supply-chain attack surface: a malicious skill can steal credentials, exfiltrate source code, or install backdoors. Existing defenses use static skill scanners based on pattern matching or LLM-as-judge analysis, but it remains unclear whether they withstand adaptive evasions that preserve malicious behavior while changing payload appearance.
  This paper first presents an adversarial study of existing skill scanners through SkillCloak, a payload-preserving evasion framework that keeps the attack semantics intact while transforming their visible form. SkillCloak uses two complementary strategies: Structural Obfuscation, which rewrites visible payload indicators into semantically equivalent forms, and Self-Extracting Skill (SFS) Packing, which hides malicious components from the install-time view and restores them during agent execution. Across eight scanners and 1,613 in-the-wild malicious skills, SFS Packing bypasses every scanner at over 90%, while Structural Obfuscation bypasses over 80% on most static scanners and reaches 96% on a hybrid scanner, showing that appearance-based auditing is insufficient.
