  Motivated by this finding, we propose SkillDetonate, a behavior-centric runtime auditor that executes skills in a sandbox and detects malicious effects through OS-boundary information-flow evidence rather than install-time appearance. SkillDetonate combines on-demand closure lift, which observes instructions materialized during execution, with marker-based taint analysis, which tracks sensitive-data flows across the agent context, files, processes, and network operations. The results show that SkillDetonate detects 97% of attacks at a 2% false-positive rate and sustains 87% detection on real-world malicious skills.

</details>


### [554] [Embedding Inference Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01276)
*Cedric Fitiavana Raelijohn,Sébastien Gambs,Jean-Francois Rajotte*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Embedding Inference Attacks (EIA), a method for identifying embedding models in black-box IR systems, and validates its effectiveness even against certain defense mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address security vulnerabilities in black-box IR systems, notably embedding inversion attacks, and explore how tailored queries could impact the privacy of such systems.

Method: The authors developed embedding inference attack techniques that utilize tailored queries to identify embedding models used in IR systems, tested against rerankers, and applied on a Retrieval-Augmented Generation system.

Result: EIA successfully identifies embedding models in black-box settings and bypasses rerankers and LLM defenses. Additional mitigation strategies like similarity thresholds are proposed.

Conclusion: Embedding inference attacks pose a significant privacy risk to black-box IR systems, highlighting the need for better mitigation strategies.

Abstract: Embedding models are essential components of modern Information Retrieval (IR) systems, yet they are typically hidden behind APIs. Recent works have shown that dense IR system can lead to security vulnerabilities such as embedding inversion attacks. However, such attacks usually require that the attacker knows the embedding model for the attack to be applicable. In this paper, we study IR systems under a black-box setting in which the adversary observes only the unordered set of retrieved documents, without ranking or similarity scores. We demonstrate that in such contexts, tailored queries allow an adversary to identify which embedding model is in use from a set of known model candidate, which we coin as an embedding inference attack (EIA). We also show that certain queries remain discriminative even when the system includes a reranker as a potential defense mechanism. We further validate our method on a real Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system, in which the tailored queries bypass the LLM's tendency to reject inputs it does not recognize as well-formed questions. Finally, we propose and evaluate other mitigation strategies such as similarity thresholds.

</details>


### [555] [Generative AI and Federated Learning for Intrusion Detection Systems: A Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01305)
*Jiefei Liu,Abu Saleh Md Tayeen,Pratyay Kumar,Qixu Gong,Wenbin Jiang,Huiping Cao,Satyajayant Misra,Jayashree Harikumar*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper surveys the integration of generative AI and federated learning (FL) techniques for intrusion detection systems (IDS), addressing challenges in data availability, privacy, and attack evolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the challenges of designing reliable IDS models, such as data imbalance, privacy constraints, incomplete data, and the evolving nature of cyber-attacks, by leveraging advances in generative AI and FL.

Method: The paper categorizes and reviews applications of generative AI in IDS based on model types (e.g., autoencoders, GANs, diffusion models, LLMs) and their tasks and examines studies that integrate these with FL methodologies.

Result: The survey identifies applications of generative models for anomaly detection, synthetic and adversarial traffic generation, alert explanation, and FL contributions to privacy-preserving IDS training in distributed environments.

Conclusion: The paper highlights open challenges and research directions, such as improving synthetic data quality, handling non-IID data distributions, and designing domain-specific LLMs, emphasizing the potential of generative AI and FL in advancing IDS.

Abstract: Intrusion Detection Systems (IDSs) are essential for monitoring network traffic and identifying malicious activities in modern cyber-physical, Internet of Things (IoT), enterprise, and distributed network environments. However, developing reliable IDS models remains challenging because attack behaviors evolve over time, realistic datasets are difficult to obtain, traffic records may be incomplete, attack classes are often imbalanced, and privacy constraints limit centralized data collection. Recent advances in generative artificial intelligence (AI) and Federated Learning (FL) provide new opportunities to address these limitations. Generative models can support anomaly detection, synthetic traffic generation, data augmentation, data imputation, adversarial traffic generation, and IDS alert explanation. FL enables distributed IDS training without directly sharing local network traffic, making it suitable for privacy-sensitive and geographically distributed environments. This survey provides a structured review of generative AI and FL techniques for IDS. We first summarize representative IDS research directions, including adversarial machine learning, anomaly-based detection, IoT-oriented IDS, explainable IDS, and benchmark datasets. We then categorize generative AI applications in IDS according to model families and task objectives, covering autoencoder-based models, Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), diffusion models, and Large Language Models (LLMs). Finally, we review emerging studies that integrate generative AI with FL-based IDS and discuss open challenges, including synthetic data quality, realistic traffic generation, dual-use adversarial risks, non-IID client distributions, communication-efficient model sharing, federated IDS benchmarking, and domain-specific LLMs for network security.

</details>


### [556] [Hamm-Grams: An Algorithm for Mining Regular Expressions of Bytes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01445)
*Derek Everett,Edward Raff,James Holt*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'hamm-grams,' a more robust feature extraction method for malware detection using single-character wildcards and an efficient locality-sensitive hashing algorithm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Malware detection is critically hindered by the brittleness of commonly used $n$-gram features in static analysis for machine learning systems.

Method: The authors propose 'hamm-grams,' a feature extraction method based on single-character wildcards and fixed-length regular expressions, along with a novel locality-sensitive hashing algorithm to efficiently find and cluster these features.

Result: The proposed hamm-grams improve the performance of malware classification and detection tasks.

Conclusion: Hamm-grams provide a more robust alternative to $n$-grams features for malware analysis and could lead to improved detection systems.

Abstract: Malware poses a critical and ever-evolving threat, and robust and effective systems for detecting and classifying malware are of essential importance. $n$-grams features are among the common static features used in effective machine learning systems for malware, but these features are inherently brittle. We propose an algorithm for constructing more robust features, hamm-grams, which are a special class of regular expressions having a fixed length and single-character wildcards. We devise an efficient algorithm for finding common hamm-grams using a new locality-sensitive hash designed to produce collisions among pairs of small Hamming distance and a clustering within hash buckets to place wildcards. We then demonstrate the advantages of these features in malware classification and detection tasks.

</details>


### [557] [Beyond Gradient-Based Attacks: Adversarial Robustness and Explainability Stability in Cybersecurity Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01679)
*Mona Rajhans,Vishal Khawarey*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper examines how adversarial attacks affect both predictions and SHAP-based explanations of cybersecurity classifiers for Random Forest and XGBoost models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adversarial attacks undermine cybersecurity predictive models and their explanations, which are crucial for analysts to interpret and manage alerts.

Method: The authors evaluated Random Forest and XGBoost models against various adversarial attacks across four datasets, introducing the Explainability Stability Index (ESI) for measuring SHAP attribution drift during such attacks.

Result: Gradient-based black-box attacks like ZOO show near-perfect robustness for XGBoost but still cause substantial attribution drift. Score-based attacks like Square Attack reveal genuine prediction vulnerabilities.

Conclusion: Prediction robustness and explanation stability are separate dimensions requiring joint evaluation, with a new two-axis framework guiding improved assessment of adversarial attack impacts on tree ensembles.

Abstract: Adversarial attacks on cybersecurity classifiers pose a dual threat: degrading predictions and destabilising the SHAP-based explanations that security analysts rely on to understand and triage alerts. We extend our prior MLP conference study to Random Forest and XGBoost across four tabular security datasets (phishing URLs, UNSW-NB15, NF-ToN-IoT, HIKARI-2021), evaluating five attacks including three black-box methods applicable to non-differentiable tree models. We introduce the Explainability Stability Index (ESI), a scalar metric computed from TreeSHAP attribution drift under adversarial perturbation, reported on the same [0,1] scale as the Robustness Index (RI). A key finding is that gradient-based black-box attacks (ZOO) produce degenerate results against XGBoost (apparent RI ~0.98) due to piecewise-constant prediction surfaces, while score-based Square Attack reveals genuine vulnerability (RI ~0.36). These degenerate perturbations still drive substantial attribution drift: XGBoost ESI ~0.06-0.16 despite near-perfect ZOO robustness, versus 0.14-0.29 for RF, showing that prediction robustness and explanation stability are distinct axes requiring joint measurement. A two-axis framework (gradient dependence, query efficiency) explains the observed attack ranking and yields practical guidance for tree ensemble evaluation. A step-size ablation explains a counterintuitive PGD anomaly on z-score normalised tabular data.

</details>


### [558] [From Forgeries to Foundation Models: A Systematic Survey of Identity Document Attack and Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01442)
*Gourab Das,Pavan Kumar C,Raghavendra Ramachandra*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper addresses the new capabilities of generative AI enabling high-fidelity identity document forgeries, examines current detection shortcomings, and proposes a unified threat model while identifying areas for improvement in ID verification systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the rising threat of AI-driven identity document forgery, which current detection systems and benchmarks fail to address adequately.

Method: The study surveys detection methods, audits public datasets, evaluates large multimodal models, and benchmarks unseen synthesized identity documents to highlight shortcomings in existing processes.

Result: The findings show that existing detection methods suffer from a Reality Gap, Script-Dependent Generative Instability (SDGI) in certain languages, and poor generalization, with strong models still failing with APCER values above 25%.

Conclusion: The paper suggests the need for forensically grounded, privacy-preserving, and legally accountable identity verification systems to address these vulnerabilities effectively.

Abstract: Identity document forgery has undergone a fundamental capability shift: generative AI tools now enable high-fidelity document synthesis and field-level manipulation with minimal technical expertise, while detection methods remain constrained by benchmarks that do not reflect this threat. The resulting attack surface spans physical presentation, digital injection, and fully generative synthesis, introducing distinct forensic failure modes that require a unified threat model and evaluation framework. This survey provides, to our knowledge, the first unified treatment of Presentation Attacks, Digital Injection Attacks, and GenAI-driven synthesis within a single identity verification threat model. We trace detection methodologies from rule-based heuristics through forensic localisation, injection-aware pipelines, foundation models, and few-shot frameworks. A systematic audit of public datasets from 2019--2025 exposes a persistent Reality Gap between benchmark conditions and operational deployment. We further analyse large multimodal models for identity document manipulation, identifying Script-Dependent Generative Instability (SDGI) as a recurring typographic failure mode in non-Latin script inpainting. Finally, zero-shot benchmarking on unseen synthesised ID cards shows that even the strongest publicly available models achieve APCER values above 25% under security-oriented operating conditions, highlighting substantial limits in cross-domain generalisation. We conclude by outlining future directions toward forensically grounded, privacy-preserving, and legally accountable identity verification systems.

</details>


### [559] [Pmeta-TLA: Backdoor Attacks for Speech Classification Models via Meta-Learning with Timbre Leakage Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01702)
*Yueming Huang,Wenhan Yao,Fen Xiao,Xiarun Chen,Weiping Wen*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Timbre Leakage Attack (TLA) as a novel backdoor attack exploiting timbre-level features, alongside an innovative training strategy Pmeta-TLA for embedding multiple backdoors with better performance and reduced costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the vulnerability of speech classification models to backdoor attacks and highlight the need for advanced attack detection and prevention strategies.

Method: Developed the Timbre Leakage Attack (TLA) which uses frame-level timbre information to deceive models, alongside Pmeta-TLA, a meta-learning-based training strategy for embedding multiple backdoors using Projected Conflicting Gradients (PCGrad).

Result: Experimental findings on keyword spotting tasks demonstrate higher attack efficacy, improved stealthiness, robustness, and lower attack costs compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The techniques presented show promise in revealing vulnerabilities of speech classification systems and provide insights into enhancing model security against sophisticated backdoor attacks.

Abstract: Recently, speech classification methods have gained widespread adoption in intelligent gadgets. Current study indicates that backdoor attacks provide a substantial security concern to these models, underscoring the pressing necessity to investigate additional potential attack techniques to expose and prevent such risks. This work discusses the vulnerability of current speech triggers to detection by deep neural network defenders and introduces the Timbre Leakage Attack (TLA). The suggested trigger disseminates timbre information at the frame level within the deep self-supervised features, producing poisoned samples that appear natural to human perception. Furthermore, we introduce Pmeta-TLA, an innovative training mechanism for embedding numerous backdoors one time. This method proposes a multi-backdoor injection training strategy using meta-learning and Projected Conflicting Gradients (PCGrad) and introduces TLA as a multi-target attack tool within it. We performed tests on data-poisoning backdoor attacks in keyword spotting tasks utilizing some deep neural network models. Experimental results indicate that the proposed strategy attains superior Attack efficacy, enhanced stealthiness, robustness, and a reduced attack cost relative to baseline methods.

</details>


### [560] [Has This Checkpoint Been Abliterated? A Two-Signal Audit and Its Failure Map](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01854)
*Gabriel Hurtado*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper proposes a checkpoint audit tool using internal signals to detect the absence of a refusal mechanism in AI systems, achieving high accuracy but recognizing limitations under certain edge cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to identify whether AI checkpoints lack refusal mechanisms, which runtime guards cannot currently detect effectively.

Method: The approach combines two internal signals—activation refusal-gap and weight-recovery energy—into a threshold-free audit system. These are evaluated on a large registry of AI checkpoints.

Result: The proposed method achieves high AUROC (0.95) and balanced accuracy (0.89) for distinguishing benign and tampered checkpoints, outperforming individual signals.

Conclusion: The audit tool is effective for checkpoint triage but not foolproof, requiring attested references and being limited by the evaluation registry.

Abstract: Can a platform tell, before deployment, whether an open-weight checkpoint has had its refusal mechanism stripped? Runtime guards cannot: they score generations, not the artifact. We combine two cheap internal signals, a reference-anchored activation refusal-gap and a weight-recovery energy of the base-to-candidate weight difference, into a threshold-free checkpoint audit. The two are negatively correlated and label-complementary: the gap supplies refusal-specificity and the weight energy supplies recall. On a 273-checkpoint registry spanning Qwen, DeepSeek-distilled Qwen, Llama, and Gemma, their z-sum separates 57 public abliterations from 37 benign fine-tunes, merges, and instruction-tunes at AUROC 0.95, significantly above either signal alone (0.84, 0.90), and a Youden-calibrated threshold transfers to held-out families at balanced accuracy 0.89 (FPR 0.11), missing only 4 of 57. We then map two failures, in order of severity: a spoofed reference evades both axes with no training (ΔW=0, \r{ho}=1 by construction), and a white-box owner trains a checkpoint past the threshold while it stays guard-unsafe and coherent. The audit is effective triage, not tamper-proofing: it presumes an attested reference, and its claims are bounded by the registry we evaluate it on.

</details>


### [561] [Behind the Refusal: Determining Guardrail Activation via Behavioral Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02121)
*William Hackett,Peter Garraghan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new methodology to detect and analyze guardrail systems in AI that are used to block malicious instructions, achieving high accuracy in identifying guardrail presence and differentiating it from LLM safety rejections.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensuring the safety of LLMs and agentic systems in real-world applications is vital, but researchers often face challenges distinguishing between a guardrail block and an LLM safety alignment rejection when conducting black-box testing. This distinction is crucial for optimizing adversarial strategies.

Method: The paper proposes a black-box guardrail reconnaissance methodology using behavioral monitoring of HTTP, lexical, and timing signals, assuming minimal knowledge of guardrails and AI systems.

Result: The methodology detects the presence of guardrails with 100% accuracy, demonstrates clear behavioral separation between benign and malicious interactions, and distinguishes between guardrail blocks and LLM rejections with an average F1 score of 98%.

Conclusion: The research provides a novel and effective approach for detecting and analyzing guardrail systems, improving understanding of AI security mechanisms and paving the way for more precise adversarial testing.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) and agentic systems become integrated into real-world applications, ensuring their safety and security is critical. Guardrail systems that detect and block malicious instructions sent to and from an LLM are an essential component of AI security. However, researchers conducting black-box adversarial emulation against production AI systems often struggle to determine whether a guardrail block or an LLM rejection has occurred. This distinction is important because the techniques used to bypass guardrails can differ substantially from those used to bypass LLM safety alignment, and has a material impact on attack technique selection and optimization. We propose the first black-box guardrail reconnaissance methodology, which detects the presence of a guardrail within a target AI system through behavioral monitoring of HTTP, lexical, and timing signals, assuming only black-box access and zero prior knowledge of the guardrail or AI system. Experiments demonstrate that our approach detects guardrail presence with 100% accuracy, with statistically significant behavioral separation between benign and malicious interactions (q < 0.001). Our approach further identifies the content categories a guardrail is designed to block, and distinguishes guardrail blocks from LLM rejection on unseen prompts with an average F1 score of 98%.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [562] [Aggregation with Exponential Weights is Optimal in Expectation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02247)
*Mikael Møller Høgsgaard,Patrick Rebeschini,Tobias Wegel*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: This paper resolves whether the aggregation with exponential weights (AEW) estimator is minimax-rate optimal in expectation for large temperatures under random design. It concludes that AEW achieves the desired excess risk without requiring a Bernstein-type assumption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address an open problem posed by Lecué and Mendelson (2013) regarding whether AEW can achieve minimax-rate optimality for squared loss in model selection aggregation.

Method: The authors analyze the AEW estimator under random design and prove that it achieves the excess risk in expectation under specific conditions involving temperature and distribution characteristics.

Result: AEW achieves the excess risk $T\log(M)/(n+1)$ when the temperature $T$ satisfies certain inequalities. For squared loss, a sufficient temperature of $T \geq 4 b^2$ is identified.

Conclusion: AEW has a sharp phase transition from suboptimal to optimal when the temperature is adequately large, confirming conjectures about its behavior and settling the open problem.

Abstract: The aggregation with exponential weights (AEW) estimator is not fully understood in the basic setting of model selection aggregation with squared loss. In particular, whether it is minimax-rate optimal in expectation for large enough fixed temperatures and under random design has been an open problem since its introduction, which was explicitly posed by Lecué and Mendelson (2013). In this paper, we settle this problem by showing that \emph{without} requiring a Bernstein-type assumption, the AEW indeed achieves the excess risk $T \log (M) / (n+1)$ in expectation, whenever the temperature $T$ satisfies $(L^2/T)\exp(B/T)\leq μ/2$. Here, the number of dictionary elements is $M$, the estimator has observed $n$ i.i.d. samples from any distribution, and the loss is assumed to be bounded by $B$, $L$-Lipschitz continuous and $μ$-strongly convex. For squared loss, we show that $T\geq 4 b^2$ suffices when the predictions and labels are $[0,b]$-valued. Because AEW is known to be suboptimal in expectation for temperatures below some constant, this shows that AEW has a sharp phase transition when the temperature is large enough but constant, as conjectured by Lecué and Mendelson.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [563] [Quantum-Inspired Vision: Leveraging Wave-Particle Duality for Low-Illumination Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01731)
*Yiquan Gao*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper expands the Data Relativistic Uncertainty (DRU) framework by adopting a physics-inspired approach for modeling and enhancing images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a physics-to-AI paradigm that formalizes probabilistic methods to improve image enhancement while enhancing Explainable AI (XAI) interpretability.

Method: Images are modeled as probabilistic wave functions incorporating wave-particle duality to explain the DRU framework's functioning and its handling of illumination bias and noise.

Result: The framework leverages physical uncertainty in light to mitigate illumination bias and enhance robustness against image noise.

Conclusion: The paper proposes a rigorous theoretical structure for integrating physics concepts into AI, expanding the scope of DRU for better interpretability and image enhancement.

Abstract: This study provides a theoretical expansion of the recent Data Relativistic Uncertainty (DRU) framework by formalizing a physics-to-AI paradigm for image enhancement. By modeling images as probabilistic wave functions rather than deterministic states, the paradigm explicitly integrates wave-particle duality to illustrate the system flow of how DRU leverages the intrinsic physical uncertainty of light, a dimension requiring further theoretical discussion. Consequently, this paradigm provides a rigorous Explainable AI (XAI) approach that enhances the interpretability of how DRU mitigates illumination bias and maintains robustness against data noise.

</details>


### [564] [Population-Scale Segmentation of Penile Tissue in DIXON MRI using Deep Learning for Quantitative Phenotyping in Male Reproductive Health](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02127)
*Jan Ernsting,Gunnar Paul Kordes,Nils Johannaber,Lynn Ogoniak,Wolfgang Roll,Tim Hahn,Alexander Siegfried Busch,Benjamin Risse*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This study developed a deep learning framework for automated segmentation and volumetric analysis of the whole penis using MRI, achieving high accuracy and scalability for urological and reproductive health studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Penile measurement is clinically crucial for assessing male reproductive and urinary health conditions, but traditional methods are limited in precision and coverage of internal structures.

Method: The researchers optimized a deep learning-based 3D nnU-Net with expert-annotated MRI data to achieve automated segmentation and analyzed its performance using metrics like Dice scores and Hausdorff distances. They applied this model to analyze data from the UK Biobank.

Result: The model achieved high accuracy (Dice score: 0.92) on independent testing, was deployed on 34,412 participants for population-scale analysis, and showed consistent inter-session reproducibility in longitudinal data.

Conclusion: This framework offers a robust, reproducible, and scalable solution for MRI-based penile assessment, enabling advanced phenotyping and research in male reproductive health.

Abstract: Penile measurement is clinically relevant across male reproductive and urogenital health, including conditions such as micropenis, congenital and endocrine disorders, and sexual or urinary dysfunction. However, quantitative assessment of penile size has relied mainly on external length or circumference measurements, which are difficult to standardize, sensitive to measurement conditions, and unable to capture the internal portion of the penis. MRI enables volumetric assessment of the whole penis in vivo, but automated segmentation has not previously been established at population scale. Automated whole-organ volumetry would enable high-throughput phenotyping for multi-omics and clinical studies of male reproductive disease.
  Here, we present a deep learning framework for whole-penis segmentation in multi-channel DIXON MRI. Using a newly curated expert-annotated training dataset ($n = 145$ subjects; $13,050$ annotated slices) and a double-annotated independent test benchmark ($n = 24$ subjects; $2,160$ double-annotated slices), we optimized a 3D nnU-Net architecture. The model achieved a 5-fold cross-validation Dice score of $0.90$ and performed at observer-level accuracy on the independent test set (Dice: $0.92$; Hausdorff distance: $3.58$).
  We deployed the model in $34,412$ UK Biobank participants, enabling automated quantification of total penile tissue, including both external and internal components. Longitudinal evaluation in 2,282 men demonstrated high inter-session reproducibility ($r = 0.87$). This framework establishes a reproducible and population-scalable method for MRI-based assessment of penile anatomy and provides an open technical resource for future studies in urological imaging and male reproductive health. The trained model weights will be publicly released.

</details>


### [565] [Self-Auditing Residual Drifting for Pathology-Preserving Accelerated Knee MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.02428)
*Qing Lyu,Jianxu Wang,Mohammad Kawas,Ge Wang,Christopher T. Whitlow*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper introduces SA-RDM-DC, a novel reconstruction method for accelerated knee MRI, which ensures high-quality imaging, retains diagnostic structures, and identifies reconstruction errors effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accelerated MRI often suffers from blurred diagnostically relevant structures or undetected reconstruction errors that global image metrics fail to capture.

Method: SA-RDM-DC employs a physics-conditioned generative drift field to refine image reconstruction from undersampled k-space. It predicts image and k-space corrections, ensures k-space data consistency, and uses frequency-aware supervision for fine details. Additionally, it produces dense error maps and reliability scores concurrently with the reconstruction.

Result: SA-RDM-DC outperforms zero-filled reconstruction and state-of-the-art baselines on fastMRI knee data, achieving superior SSIM, subsecond inference, and better pathology preservation. It also partly generalizes its reliability scores under protocol shifts.

Conclusion: SA-RDM-DC facilitates enhanced MRI reconstruction balancing image fidelity, diagnostic task preservation, runtime efficiency, and reconstruction reliability assessment.

Abstract: Accelerated magnetic resonance imaging reduces acquisition time, but reconstruction from undersampled k-space can blur diagnostically relevant structures or introduce failures that are not captured by global image metrics. We propose SA-RDM-DC, a Self-Auditing Residual generative Drifting Model with Data Consistency for accelerated knee MRI. The method adapts the newly proposed generative drifting paradigm to accelerated MRI by training a physics-conditioned drift field from the zero-filled reconstruction toward the fully sampled residual correction. It predicts image- and missing-k-space residual corrections, enforces data consistency with acquired k-space, uses frequency-aware and residual drifting supervision to recover fine detail, and produces dense error maps and slice-level risk scores in the same inference pass. We evaluate SA-RDM-DC on multi-coil fastMRI knee data at acceleration factors of 4, 8, and 12, with fastMRI+ pathology annotations for region-level and classifier-based task preservation, and on SKM-TEA for zero-shot and fine-tuned protocol-shift evaluation. Compared with zero-filled reconstruction, UNet-image-SENSE, DC-UNet, Score-Diffusion, ELF-Diff, SENSE-VarNet, and MoDL baselines, SA-RDM-DC achieves the highest SSIM across fastMRI acceleration factors while retaining subsecond per-slice inference and avoiding the long sampling time of iterative diffusion baselines. In pathology-aware analysis, SA-RDM-DC preserves lesion-region structural fidelity and reduces meniscus prediction instability. Its self-auditing scores strongly identify high-error reconstructions on fastMRI and partially transfer as a selective-review signal under SKM-TEA protocol shift. These results support reconstruction evaluation that jointly considers image fidelity, pathology preservation, runtime, and case-specific reliability.

</details>
